author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Dear Lisa Donovan, 
This cake sounds great -must try it! But first, please help!
By Sweet Potatoes, I assume you mean the ones that have lighter skin and pale yellow flesh. Could what I call Yams, the ones with dark reddish skin and golden-orange flesh, be used instead? Or do you call those darker ones 'Sweet Potatoes'? Those two tubers' names get mixed up in recipe descriptors, there are many species, but my local supermarket and my brain only know those two varieties of pointy-ended potatoes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Grocery stores always mix the names up-the ones with dark reddish skins and orange flesh are sweet potatoes and the lighter skinned ones with pale yellow flesh are also sweet potatoes but the lighter skinned ones are properly named Jersey white sweet potatoes, yams are a tropical vegetable not usually seen in N American markets-use the darker skinned ones that you call yams.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: First, yams are not sweet potatoes. A goof-up many years back, along with a legal ruling allowing said goof-up to stick around, has perpetuated this misunderstanding. Beauregard, Jewel, Garnet--these are orange-flesh varieties. Bonita, Hannah, O'Henry--these are pale-flesh varieties. Now, the starch and water content, not to mention beta carotene content, likely vary between these two broader groupings, so that may be information worth knowing prior to cake making. Best to you in your endeavor!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: So curious minds want to know why after many explanations of the difference in kosher salts, this recipe switches to Morton's (after a clear preferance by many chef's in NYT Cooking for Diamond Crystal).  From what I have read, it is much more concentrated, so much more sodium.  Perhaps you could give recommendations for the different brands, so we don't need to have multiple kosher salts in our pantry.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Claire Morton’s is not more concentrated than Diamond Crystal; both are pure sodium chloride. The difference is in the shape and density of the salt flakes, which determines how many flakes fit into a given volume.

Diamond Crystal flakes are pyramidal and hollow, so they occupy more space and do not compact as neatly as Morton’s flat, denser flakes. As a result, significantly more Morton flakes fit into a teaspoon than Diamond Crystal flakes.

Because volume measures the space an ingredient occupies rather than its mass, the denser Morton flakes pack more tightly into a teaspoon. This means a teaspoon of Morton’s weighs about 5 grams, while the larger, lighter Diamond Crystal flakes take up the same space but weigh only about 2.8 grams. 

If you convert a recipe written for Morton’s to Diamond Crystal, you will need to use the same weight of salt—not the same volume, to achieve the same sodium level and flavor intensity. For example, if a heaping teaspoon of Morton’s weighs 6.5 grams, a baker should use 6.5 grams of Diamond Crystal to maintain the same saltiness.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There are many different ways of doing a potato gratin (gratin dauphinois pomme de terre) although I prefer the very simplest -- cream or milk, nutmeg, salt, pepper, garlic, potatoes and butter.  You could add a layer of sliced carrots or sweet potatoes or turnips or parsnips if you like and flavor the cream to your own taste.  Add cheese for the very American Potatoes Au Gratin or add some stock as Alice Waters does; it's a very adaptable dish.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Having lived in France for decades, my take on this is simple: Steep 6-8 sliced garlic cloves in cream with salt, 5-bay ground pepper, and nutmeg for at least 30 minutes; stirring so it doesn’t stick.
Peel potatoes and slice paper thin. Don’t bother ‘arranging’ them in the baking dish, just layer them and don’t leave gaps.
Test the cream, it should have the same salinity as sea water.
Pour liquid over potatoes and bake until bubbling over. 
No cheese. To the French that is heresy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Elizabeth de L. I too have lived in France for decades. While a traditional gratin dauphinois doesn’t have cheese in it, LOTS of French people put cheese in it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Kathoco I distinctly remember eating this with gruyère in France many times.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
"Normally", the cream (crème fraîche), does the gratin job , no need for gruyère at the end which hardens the surface.  Apart from that, easy on thyme, which gives the dish a slight unwished mediterreanan tinge.
Otherwise it seems a good and valid recipe

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There was an old recipe in The NY Times for a Normandy-style gratin where the potato slices are blanched in milk because there is an abundance of milk in Normandy.  Even if blanched in just water the potatoes retain enough starch to tighten the gratin just don' rinse them after cooking.  If you dot the top of the gratin with butter it will brown nicely in a hot oven.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve made the same gratin for years from my well worn cookbook “The Secrets of Great French Restaurants. “. Gently bring potatoes cut half inch thick and to a simmer then add heavy cream. Coon until potatoes are somewhat tender but still firm enough. Rub a gratin dishwith cut clove of garlic and butter. Add potatoes and milk mixture. Season with salt and pepper and nutmeg. Add grated Gruyère and bake until crusty and bubbly. Delicious. Great with roast lamb and  vs if peas

commentBody: Some may be horrified, but I find steeping the garlic and aromatics in the cream can be done gently in the microwave, directly in a Pyrex that has a spout and handle. No need to transfer the steeped cream from pot to spouted container.  Saves a step, a pot, and some mess.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I’ve been making the gratin dauphinois for decades and often serve it as a main course. It started with a Joël Robuchon recipe (he used cheese, fyi) and has evolved over the years as I’ve picked up “astuces” from friends, French and American, I’ve made it with. Here is some of their sage advice:
-cut the potatoes with a knife. The irregularity of widths and angles allows the liquid to seep between the slices.
- Use white pepper to avoid a speckled cream. 
-bake at 400 degrees for about an hour, but keep it covered the first 30 minutes to avoid over browning. 
- after rubbing the garlic in the dish, save the cloves and just before baking tuck one in each corner of the dish (I use 2 cloves, each cut in half).
-use crème fraîche (or a mixture of liquid cream and crème fraîche)
- if you heat the seasoned cream, there is no need to cook the potatoes in it. Just add a lot of the hot cream between each layer. It’s far less messy this way and you can arrange the potato slices more precisely. I’ve tried both ways and it makes no difference in consistency.
-for extra crustiness dot the top with butter. 
-and pure heresy: I put in a few pinches of piment d’espelette. Not enough that anyone would notice, really, but it adds a savory layer I like.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @LUC I have made and frozen dauphinois successfully. Allow  very well in cling film & foil and then cook from frozen, or, if you have a vacuum sealer (long shot, but I do!), cool, cut into portions and vacuum seal, then freeze and reheat on the stove, boil in the bag style.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @LUC ooos, hit send halfway through replying! Cool in the pan then wrap very well in clingfilm & foil and freeze. Reheat from frozen (remove clingfilm, cover with foil) in a 350 oven for 45 mins to 1.5 hrs depending on depth of the pan. If you want, defrost it before heating but it will be a bit watery. Or, if you have a vacuum sealer, portion, freeze or refrigerate in vacuum bags, and then reheat on the stove, boil in the bag style. This is what I do as it’s MUCH faster, but of course not many people have vacuum sealers! Happy Thanksgiving.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I love cheese as much as anybody but I like this dish a lot better without it. 

I also like to make sure there's a little extra cream than it looks like it needs. As it begins to brown toward the end, use a large spoon or similar to push down the surface and allow the unbrowned cream to rise. Keep this up for a good 1/2 hr or so. The starchy cream will reduce and thicken, and you'll wind up with a gorgeous gratin on top.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
My cousin, who was a chef in his hotel-restaurant of the Dauphiné region (where the gratin dauphinois comes from) would always, always say there is absolutely no cheese in the real gratin. The cream and nutmeg should be enough. I cross the line and add Comté, whose fruity flavor complements the nuttiness so well.
He also would pre cook the potatoes in milk infused with cloves of garlic before hand and then just lay them in a buttered dish, then lightly cover with some crème and pepper.
I have heard there is as many ways to make this dish as there are villages of this beautiful region.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Billybudd 

Agreed. I made this last night as written, and it came out deliciously, but a bit flat. I’m a baker, so I weigh everything. Six medium Yukon gold potatoes came in at 1.78 pounds. And they were a smallish medium as well.

I hate to say “ignore weight“, but I would definitely ignore weight here and use six large, or eight medium, potatoes.

Also, don’t skimp on the salt! Whenever I see “diamond crystal“, a brand I don’t use, I aim low with my store brand. But I think 1/2  teaspoon per layer as indicated is right.

Lastly, I’ve never used that much thyme in a recipe but it totally works!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Beryl off the top of my head - could it be that you used a lot of berries / frozen berries and they accumulated in the center making it very moist? I’d try to dry the berries and toss them with a tablespoon of flour before folding them into the batter to ensure better distribution. 

As for the cake sinking, it could happen if you overdo the baking soda / powder. Make sure to shake the container before you measure (two scant teaspoons!) - if you don’t shake it up beforehand, you end up with compacted leavener and if there’s too much of it, your bake will rise a lot and then collapse.

commentBody: Quick cake, very tasty! Used a Spanish olive oil and did not find the oil taste overpowering- in fact, it seemed to complement the lemon beautifully. I would add more berries next time. Also, I would cook it a little longer next time. Edges were browned and the middle sprung back, but it did sink a little upon cooling and seemed a little Undercooked  in the very middle. In a cast-iron, I would take it to the edges browner than suggested, maybe five extra minutes.

author: Lisa Donovan

This pretty cake is so great

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Didn't want to use my cast iron pan and no 10" cake pan, so used a 9" cake pan plus a silicone muffin pan. Worked! 

But... next time I'll put more batter in the cake pan and use only half or so of the muffin cups instead of all 6 (filling the empties with plain water) because the cake came out too shallow.

I should've greased & floured the silicone cups too or else waited longer for cooling; they stuck somewhat, leaving remnants in the pan (which of course had to be taste tested!).

Next time. And there will be a next time - really delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Great cake

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this yesterday exactly as written in a 10"cast iron skillet.  Well, I might have thrown in a few extra berries, but it is light and delicious and easy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I like a fast good cake recipe any day. Even with a lot of sugar. I mean I don’t eat 4 slices daily…,67yro 210lbs 5’11”. Not perfect by my standards but I like cake…..

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I haven't made this cake, but have made similar olive oil cakes incl one where the 1/2 cup of milk called for in this recipe is replaced w a 1/2 cup of citrus juice. To those questioning the amount of olive oil or sugar: the ingredient ratio works beautifully. Yes you can decrease the sugar, and also replace half the olive oil with smooth applesauce. It won't be the same cake but it should be good. I personally would double the berries, fresh not frozen as that affect how the cake bakes

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this in a 9” springform pan. I cut down the amount of sugar by half. I topped it with Chantilly cream and added orange essence and squeezed lemon juice. Everyone loved it. I’m going to make it again using orange instead of lemon & cranberry instead of raspberry!

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: What would happen if I omit the salt? My spouse has hypertension so I'm looking for ways to reduce/eliminate salt where possible.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Ditto (I too want to keep my spouse healthy) and eliminate the salt in most of my recipes, we're now sensitive to saltiness and don't miss it. Use the glaze tho, as it helps amp up the flavor profile. Peace.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Freddy I understand your concerns for your spouse’s health! I’d try reducing the salt rather than eliminating completely, which could make the cake taste a little one note. If I read correctly the recipe calls for only a half teaspoon of salt, and at 8-10 servings, the amount in one piece is very small.

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Pretty much a disaster. Cooking time needs to be MUCH more than 35 minutes. I took it out after top was browned as prescribed. Waited for it to cool. Glazed it. But when I cut into it the cake was way undercooked in the middle. In all it took at least another 25 minutes back in the oven to firm up the crumb. And prep time is "10 minutes"? It takes almost that long just to grate all that lemon zest. A big waste of a lot of good ingredients.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The recipe prep time assumes that one starts from mise en place, ie has all the ingredients ready to go, everything already weighted, lemon zested and juiced, whatever needs to be diced or sliced diced and sliced etc. So yes then it's only 10 mn to whisk the batter together. It's a pain to read thru the ingredient list and estimate how long the mise en place would be.

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Oskian the surface area of 10 inch pan is 78 vs 64, so you could use a 20% reduction. Multiply the gram weight by 0.8

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: 8" cake is about 2/3 the size of a 10" by volume . I find this calculator very handy https://www.omnicalculator.com/food/cake-pans

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The recipe only "works" using a top-quality olive oil. It has to be good enough to be able to sip a teaspoon of it alone.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
The sugar glaze at the end is so easy to make, adds sweetness to a very tart dessert, and it helps the cake look good (more polished).  Therefore, I wouldn’t consider it optional.  

Plus, if you want to elevate this to “dinner party level” I would make a thick whipped cream to serve alongside a slice of this cake.  It will add a creamy texture and elegance.

author: Lisa Donovan

All my olive oils are pretty peppery, but rather than using a neutral oil to tone them down, I’m thinking of butter which would also add a warm note. Yet I know butter can alter texture.  Any advice?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Donna S, the more flavorful the olive oil, the better it is. I would stick with the olive oil.

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan

Next time I will increase the sugar a bit and/or make the glaze. It was plenty lemony though!

author: Lisa Donovan

I could not get it out of the cast iron pan without it breaking apart. So I went in reverse. Cut it into wedges and then took them out in individual pieces. I'm thinking a cake pan would be easier.

I used only 1/2 cup of sugar for the batter (and less than 1/4 cup of glaze), and it was sweet enough to let the lemon shine through.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe is a keeper, written just as is. Those commenters wishing to add more berries would be doing so at their peril : the recipe already results in a texture that is moist without being soggy, and I think adding any more liquid to the batter would be a mistake. In fact, I would be curious to try the recipe with a "drier" additive such as prunes or raisins, or possibly figs. I don't think the cook time is sufficient to break down Italian plums, however.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
For a few years now I have asked (as have others) for high altitude modifications on each recipe. Apparently, to no avail; despite getting emails in return that basically say - “we are working on it and it will be available soon”. Many of us are reticent to try your baking recipes; thinking that we would be wasting ingredients - especially at a time when  eggs cost nearly $1 per egg!

Come on folks, even store bought cake mixes do this. Many of us live at 5000 ft. or higher and really need help making such modifications on baking.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You know, you can simply Google "high altitude baking instructions." King Arthur is a good resource: www.kingarthurbaking.com/learn/resources/high-altitude-baking.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @William Wroblicka - Agree! we lived at 8500' for 7 years, and were able to figure out high altitude cooking and baking pretty quickly by asking neighbors and doing some simple reasearch. You can too, and free yourself from depending on commercial cake mixes and modifications to create delicious dishes and baked goods with your own modified recipes!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Apple and Plum Croustade (Croustade aux Pommes et Prunes) Recipe
commentBody: A caution for folks who want to adapt this recipe to other fruits: tart apples and plums are both high in pectin, which is essential to avoid having your pie turn into a big, messy, squishy fruit soup dumpling. That's also the reason for using a thin layer of filling. Most other fruits will need significant thickening and/or the structure of a pan, though if you froze some rhubarb in the spring, it's a fine sub.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Apple and Plum Croustade (Croustade aux Pommes et Prunes) Recipe
commentBody: From a born and raised Frenchie: no cinnamon if you want the real Country French experience! I never knew that spice existed outside Moroccan savory food until I moved to the US in my twenties, but I'll see myself out as I realize  that Americans can't conceive of an apple dessert without the dreaded spice - and everyone should enjoy their food however they prefer it:)

commentBody:
This recipe is just like the quick dessert my mother made us in my childhood when there were too many apple “seconds” (slightly bruised or damaged) leftover to sell from our apple orchard. 
The best part was - it really was a hand held slice of apple pie, thinner and less juicy, we could have more than one slice at a time. Great to eat anytime, like a cookie.

commentBody: I made this for a dinner party and it was delicious. However, I had to fiddle with the recipe a little. Where I live (Midwest), none of the apple varieties called for in the recipe are available in August (when the recipe was originally published). I solved the problem by substituting half Granny Smith apples and half Gala. Also, the amount of apples (2 pounds) was far too much. Next time I will use 1.5 pounds at most.

commentBody: Has anyone tried cardamom rather than cinnamon in this recipe?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Apple and Plum Croustade (Croustade aux Pommes et Prunes) Recipe
commentBody:
Finally pulled this recipe into use - late winter so no plums, and took the admonition on cinnamon to heart - added some tapioca flour and almond flour to the fruit (old apples) along with some cardamom and amaretto in place of Armagnac.
Took more like 60 minutes to cook, but came out well served with whipped cream- everyone at the dinner party loved it!
Thanks

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Apple and Plum Croustade (Croustade aux Pommes et Prunes) Recipe
commentBody: A hint of cinnamon is delicious with plums but dont over do the amount or it masks the fruit. I’d use 1/4 -1/2 tsp for the fruit in this recipe. Is there a reason some cornstarch couldn’t be tossed with fruit to help control the fruit moisture and keep the crust from getting soggy?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Overall enjoyed the flavours in this one and my fam also quite liked it. It was a surprise to not see salting and draining the zucchini before combining. This is a must or you end up with salad soup. Mandolin the zucchini’s, salt heavily and set aside to drain for 20-30min, then continue.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
If your problem is excess zucchini, the solution is this brilliant three-ingredient Spanish soup, astoundingly good for the effort:
1) Thin-slice half as much onion as zucchini. (If you're confronting doorstop marrows on the zucchini front, scoop out the spongy center first.)
2) Heat a goodly amount of olive oil in a pot and cook the onions until tender and translucent.
3) Add the zucchini and proceed likewise.
4) Take a stick blender to it, taste for salt, and serve with a lashing of olive oil.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Definitely need to salt generously and let rest and drain

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Yes to salting and draining the zucchini and fennel!  Also, tbh, I felt the salad was missing some mouthfeel.  I mixed some of the lemon juice with balsamic vinegar and an excellent olive oil.  Viola!  Delish!   Bonus:  It seemed to get even better after 24-48 hours (!)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Remington
It’s ‘voilà ‘ not ‘viola’. The latter is a stringed instrument.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I kind of liked “viola.”

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @kidrid why?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I know humans have been eating stuff for long enough that there's probably no such thing any more as an original recipe, but this seems uncannily like Heidi Swanson's published recipe for "Shaved Fennel Salad", which is also on her website, 101 cookbooks... https://www.101cookbooks.com/shaved-fennel-salad/

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Sonia I make shaved vegetable salads all of the time. I have several different ones featuring shaved fennel, several featuring zucchini, these sometimes have cheese, sometimes not, always with herbs and lemon.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Sonia thanks for that recipe.  It’s missing the mint, but I love the idea of serving over arugula

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Sonia Been a big fan of Heidi’s for years! She just *knows*!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I try to salt and let the onions sit for a few hours before adding to any final salad, which seems to coax forward an extra level of umami. Also helps to add the vinegar ahead of other ingredients, which gives the onions a bit more tang and contrast.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve done a variation on this for years. Mine includes crumbled feta and arugula. Tossed together with the vegetables the feta really takes off with the lemon and herbs, and the arugula is simply another strong flavor component. Plus, it goes further with the greens!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you have really fresh zucchini, this is delicious. I added Aleppo pepper and zaatar.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Too much uncooked zucchini causes digestive upset.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Followed advice from others to salt and then drain and rinse the zucchini and fennel, and then added shallot etc. according to recipe. No additional salt except to taste after lemon and vinegar.  Added some blackberries for some colour and a bit of sweetness — an absolutely beautiful and very tasty dish to put on the table.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious!  Light and refreshing

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love the flavor combination. Had a bit of a time shaving the zucchinis but it worked. Used pecans for nuts as that’s all I had. Added chives to the herbs. We all loved it! Will definitely make again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I found this salad disappointing. 
And wont make it again. 
So much labor for a lot of grass. 
Some olive oil helped a bit.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Nice fresh summer salad. Be very careful not to add too much pepper, vinegar or lemon juice at the start. I thought I was cautious, but it was way too much even so. Feta is a nice addition. Served on a bed of lightly dressed baby spinach.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This salad is so delicious and cleansing and easy to make. Make sure to salt and drain the zucchini.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Really enjoyed this salad. Perfect for summer. I used two zucchinis since that’s what I had on hand. Two fennel bulbs. All other measurements were the same.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is a delicious summer salad! We topped with lump crab for added protein, and the flavors came together perfectly. Can’t wait to make again!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: When you're mandolining the squash, you will be tempted to try and get all of it (the lengthwise slices don't give you too many per squash). Take it from me: don't do that. Your fingers will thank you. Get an extra squash.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was one of the best summer salads we've ever had!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Delicious! I heavily salted the zucchini and fennel and let them sit in a colander for 20 minutes to remove some moisture. (Did not rinse, they’d end up too soggy. Despite the heavy salting, they did not taste overly salty when done draining.) 

Had a bag of sugar snap peas in the fridge I added—cut off the tips then sliced on the bias. Added some nice substance to the meal without disrupting the light and fresh vibe. 

Skipped the mint as I didn’t have any at hand, though I bet it would be a delicious addition. 

Added a light drizzle of some good olive oil and some extra black pepper before serving.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Very tasty! I also recommend salting and draining extra water from the zucchini first. I skipped the mint and didn't miss it. I probably did a little less parsley and dill. The salad goes wonderfully with the Garlicky Chicken with Lemon-Anchovy Sauce and a bottle of chilled white wine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I don’t love raw raw zucchini - so I quickly cooked each piece in a little olive oil for only like 1 min each side. Also I only had one lemon on hand, and I used dill and fennel fronds for the herbs. 12/10 will make this again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The slicer opening on the side of my box grater worked perfectly for this! Was trying to use up farmers' market leftovers so subbed thinly sliced cucumber and julienned carrots for the fennel. I feared the cucumber would make salad leftovers slimy, and I reserved all nuts to mix in with each serving, but it was delicious day-of and as lunch for 2 days following. If you end up with very watery salad you could def puree into an improv salsa!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: One of the most refreshing and delicious salads I have ever made.  similar to one we had in Italy. I added parmesan!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made a variant of this salad -- I added some small, dark salad leaves and olive oil -- and served it to my lemon-loving wife, Lise (Danish spelling, although she's Swedish) Donovan, who liked it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Forgot the shallot, had to sub pepitas for tree nuts (which I’m not sure was worth it) and this was still absolutely delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Not to be "one of those commentators" but it was delicious with the fennel substituted for endive, and the shallots swapped for garden chives. Seems quite versatile

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious! Cut the recipe in half. Forgot to buy dill, mint in our garden is dying, so I used dried mint. Lovely perfume taste. I followed others’ advice and salted the zucchini and let it sit for 20 min. Did not wash it, just blotted it dry. I could eat this often.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
'Followed the recipe and then added (because I had to use them up):
avocado, tokyo turnip, shredded carrots, persian cukes and feta; had mint and parsley, no dill. Added bay shrimp. I also salted and drained the zucchini and used my new and sharp box slicer, as someone suggested. Added a glug of olive oil. Delish! Two of us ate and ate!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Def soak first and cut back on the salt.  Way too salty for me.  Added some sugar to try and save the salad from going into the trash because it’s way too salty. I also drained the liquid at the end and added some olive oil  ; ). Will try again with modifications.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I'm thinking a drizzle of bittersweet chocolate, and these could be dubbed Dubai-Krispy Treats.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I'm planning to drizzle a chocolate ganache!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: So I'm going to try this, because pistachios--yes! But my favorite method is to take normal rice krispy treat mixture, spread out half in a buttered dish, sprinkle it with some chopped dark chocolate and place the dish in a 200 degree oven so the chocolate softens. Then bring the dish to a counter and use an offset spatula to spread the chocolate. Then press the remaining rice krispy mixture on top of the chocolate (using wet hands helps). Let cool completely. It's like an inside out candy bar.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
@Lurlene I also will try because PIstachios! And think dark chocolate topping will be the next variation.. for 25yrs our family fave kid version has been std RKT w melted chocolate mixture as ‘frosting’ Ours is 2cups bittersweet chips, shy 1 cup butterscotch chips, heaping tbsp PB melted together and then slathered on top. Have to store in fridge and then pull out 10 mins prior to slicing - but I and fam are addicted to that combo! 
Will try this pure and then w just the chocolate

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Pistachio butter?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Marc g. I’m wondering if it’s the same as pistachio cream or pistachio spread? My grocery store has both, but not butter…

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @louis Yes! I usually buy Italian pistachio paste. I look for one that has the prettiest green color and no added sugar.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Marc g It’s like peanut butter, but it’s made with pistachios. Sometimes also called Pistachio paste or cream.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Can pistachio butter just be made by blending pistachios, as you would with almonds or peanuts?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Sobremesa my question too!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I have purchased pistachio butter (like peanut butter or almond butter) before, but for such a small amount, I would probably just make it at home with my food processor. I will report back once I have time to make these because for me, this sounds like a match made in heaven.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @anne marie Please keep us updated on how it works for this recipe. All the butters/creams/spreads I can find locally have sugar added (and a lot of other stuff). Thank you :)

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I think it's funny that anyone would need to extend the shelf life of a krispy treat.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Suz- I just asked my husband “who is this person who still has Rice Krispie treats around on day 3?” Mythical, no doubt.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @AngelaR as in he didn’t like them?!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Very cute. But if it ain't broke, don't fix it.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Where have these been all my life?!  This is an amazingly simple recipe with a surprising flavor combo.  I used almond butter since I couldn't find pistachio butter.  I just read in the comments that others are using Italian pistachio paste.  If my next batch is any better I'm buying bigger pants.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: You can get unsalted puffed rice in any Indian store. Do not need Rice Krispies for this. But otherwise, the recipe is interesting and shall definitely try it out.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
@SBa
In my experience the bags of puffed rice sold in Indian Stores are not crispy like the Rice Krispies cereal? The product I have seen would alter the texture and result in a different treat. Nothing wrong with that, same same but different.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @French Creek Baker  — During the pandemic, I snapped up what seemed like bushel-size bags of puffed rice and sometimes puffed wheat at the Indian grocery store to make “Rice Krispie” treats with marshmallows, butter and add-ins like chocolate or salted caramel chips. The result is a little spongier, but it’s a very economical and interesting substitution.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @French Creek Baker I made the mistake once of making Rice Krispie treats with puffed rice.  They were inedible 🙁

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I made these over the weekend, following the recipe as written.  These were perhaps the most expensive dessert I’ve ever made, as many of the ingredients (halvah, pistachio butter!) are very pricey and not available at your typical grocery store.  Thankfully, they were absolutely delicious and earned rave reviews from guests.  I’ll be making them again, if only to use up all the exotic ingredients now in my pantry!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: If you can find a Middle-Eastern grocery store, you will find the products are much cheaper there than in a regular supermarket or specialty store.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
@Kate D
That’s a good idea thank you, halva and pistachios aren’t cheap but the recipe sounds delicious

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Rice Krispies have little nutritional value.  Substituting Cheerios is an improvement. Tastes good, too.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Phil I agree that the Cheerios version of Rice Krispie Treats are fantastic!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Phil, they’re RCTs. Eating them is an act of ignoring their nutritional value.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I made these this weekend. Fabulous! The halva was incredibly good in this. I also loved the tahini balancing out the sweetness of the marshmallows. I did omit the pistachios and condensed milk.... These worked without it.  These are just sooooo good.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Surely these are delicious, if a bid fiddly for me to make.  As far as adding sweetened, condensed milk to avoid the "third day dryness," around here the original RCTs never last three days, occasionally maybe two!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Pistachio butter is just like peanut butter or almond butter. Recipe says "preferably unsweetened," which leads me to assume they just want pistachios in a food processor.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Kevin, I think that's right that you could just whip up some pistachio butter/cream/whatever you call it in your blender or food processor. I did find the stuff in the store, but it was very expensive and the recipe only calls for 2 tablespoons

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I have a pretty good sweet tooth and these rice crispy treats or just waaaaay too sweet. I think the recipe could’ve done without the condensed milk and half the quantity of halva.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
I’ve made these twice now and they’re a go to for me. 

Amendments:
Salted butter
Salted pistachios, double or even triple the amount
Double sesame seeds
All tahini instead of pistachio butter (in THIS economy!?)

THE BEST THING TO EVER HAPPEN: I bought condensed milk and came home and realized it was coffee condensed milk, Bandi foods brand. It tastes so incredible with this recipe and adds a little bite or earthiness that balances all the sugar so beautifully, I can’t recommend it enough.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe exactly as written and it was perfect. But then I sliced it into thin rectangles, layered a scoop of vanilla ice cream between 2 of them and made…ice cream sandwiches! They were ridiculously delicious! Next time I’m going to roll the edges in crushed pistachios.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Dina - I love your idea to turn these into ice cream sandwiches as you describe.  That should work well to make ahead and freeze them (with the crushed pistachio edges you suggest too) because I know Krispy Treats freeze well.  Thanks for the idea!  I'm going to look for pistachio ice cream to use and give your sandwich idea a try.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: These are so delicious! I made them without pistachio butter, as I had none.  Used all Tahini. Just yummy.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Anyone made a version of these without the marshmallows? I hate that musty taste of fake vanilla.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
@Lisa vS

The answer is to make your own marshmallows using quality vanilla. Nielsen-Massey is a reliable brand choice for pure vanilla. Alton Brown’s  “Homemade Marshmallows 2.0” recipe can be found on the Food Network site.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @French Creek Baker-- Homemade marshmallows don't melt the way purchased ones do.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Lisa vS Have you tried Dandies marshmallows? They’re vegan and less processed, pretty tasty!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Top bars before cutting with melted dark chocolate chips.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: If this is truly the recipe that Hani's uses in the bakery, these are truly one of the greatest creations ever made.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Any RKT is likely delish and never needs to last more than a day or two.  And I adore pistachios.  But I found it unnecessarily complicated for the result.  The sesame seeds were a nice easy add, and I already brown butter for a lot of baking.  But the pistachio cream is a very mild taste compared with just doubling the amount of pistachios, the sweetened condensed milk makes it crazy sticky to blend and put in pan, and despite the current fad for vanilla bean paste it’s not better than good quality vanilla extract (and the paste has other junk in it). I do like halvah but it’s already so crazy sweet halvah is not necessary.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Who still has these around on day three? I want to meet this person.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed and it was wonderful.  Next time will try to add a hint of ground cardamom or maybe rosewater.  This one is a winner.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
Why, if it was 'wonderful'???

'

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Loved these...rice krispie treats for grown ups!  Only word of advice is to have all your ingredients measured out and on hand.  Once the marshmallows are melted you need to get all the remaining ingredients in quickly.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Not sure why you need the sweetened condensed milk. I made it without and it's fine. If anything: more halva, more pistachio cream and more chopped pistachios.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: That is what I was wondering! Thanks for sharing.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: See recipe here for Pistachio, rose and Strawberry buns by Melissa Clark here and you’ll see the recipe for Pistachio Butter. I have made those and they are excellent, but must be eaten very fresh because they don’t keep well.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Tip: Pistachio butter is easy to make. Roast 1 cup of pistachios for 8 minutes at 325F. Transfer to a food processor (+ a pinch of salt!) and blend for 10–15 minutes (scraping down the sides every so often) until smooth and creamy. (It will go from crumbly to doughy to creamy, I promise!) This will make more than 2 tablespoons. Cover leftovers and store in the fridge.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: There was a peanut butter rice crispy recipe on the box back in the 70's.  That's the one I made over and over and my son will remember.  Never made the marshmallow one but I've tasted one.  Oddly, the peanut butter version seems to have been lost.  It is the superior recipe in my opinion.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Years ago when I was a librarian at a pharmaceutical research library, I was asked to find this variation (with Cocoa Krispies) as a birthday surprise for an employee. Using the Special Library Association directory I called the Kellogg library and the librarian read the recipe to me over the phone. That copy is long gone, but according to "The Back of the Box Gourmet" cookbook by Michael McLaughlin, simply "stir 1/4 cup peanut butter into the marshmallow mixture just before adding the cereal."

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I just made this and it's delish.  I left out the condensed milk because I didn't want to open a can to use such a tiny amount.  The original RC Treats recipe is just marshmallow, RC and 3T butter so this recipe has more than enough liquids already.   I couldn't find pistachio butter so I just put some pistachios in the food processor and ground as much as possible - which wasn't actually a butter texture but still worked.  I used pistachio TahiniBARs (6 bars in a pack.  Next time 9x9 pan.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: For "pistachio butter," I imagine you could just put shelled pistachios in your food processor and make a paste, like you would make homemade peanut butter. Salt levels would need to be adjusted to taste. A dribble of walnut oil might be good for texture. Just guessing here.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: This is what I did! Left out the salt in the recipe completely, as the pistachios I had were salted.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: These were a hit, and great for a crowd. I made the following changes: I cut the condensed milk by half (they are already so sweet!) and I finished with sprinkle of salt on top. This really helped them pop. In the future I will likely play with the pistachio and tahini ratio. These are tahini/sesame first, pistachio second. I want it the other way around, so more pistachio cream (in my case, it's what I found) and less tahini. But that is really personal preference.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: We doubled the tahini instead of adding the pistachio butter - delicious and no problems. Well, they were a bit stickier than my usual recipe, but I'm blaming that on the sweetened condensed milk, which I dint really think this recipe needs. I also cut the salt to 1 tsp and still found it too salty. But that's a lot of complaining for something we really enjoyed and will likely make again with our own tweaks.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I upped the pistachios and the halvah by a bit, otherwise made as written. Brought to my family thanksgiving celebration for my halvah loving cook to snack on beforehand. The rest of the group sampled it last night with a cup of tea. A real winner!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this twice. The first time I broke my own rule: follow instructions for the first time and if necessary, change later. SO, I left out the condensed sweetened milk as per other reviewers suggestions. The rice crispy treats turned out hard. And I mean really really hard. Last night I made the recipe and followed it, and the texture was much better. I would not leave out the 1/3 cup of sweetened condensed milk. I think it is important for the texture of the cookie.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
Cream of coconut can be substituted for the condensed milk.
Texture is similar and the taste is an excellent variation.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Unreal . Perfect sweet /salty combo and also wheat free !  It’s fun to make too . Great recipe .

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
Just finished making these.  Used the ingredients in the quantities listed, but put all the pistachios and halvah into the rice krispies mixture instead reserving some for a topping.  They are still cooling, but the bits left in the pan were delicious.  Will cut them into smallish squares as they are also very rich.
If you are in Canada, you can get unsweetened pistachio butter from an online shop in BC called NutJar.   The products I have tried so far have been very good.  No affiliation.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: A TABLESPOON of vanilla extract in 6-8 servings? Is that correct?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
*Dynamite* recipe. Made it several times. Guests and friends are crazy about it. 
However, having laughed at other commenters who insist on messing with any and all NYT recipes, I've found that:
* It's OK to skip the pistachio butter/cream ($$$!!). Hard to source where I live and no difference discernible. Works fine to use 4T tahini total & no pistachio butter.
* The condensed milk is overly sweet and gooey here (IMHO). It's great without that cloying, over-the-top sweetness.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
Objectively delicious. 

For those wondering about pistachio butter: If you have a small capacity food processor, take a handful of pistachios, grind/blend till it almost starts to seize up. Add a smidge of coconut oil and a splash of vanilla to bring it to a "butter" consistency (& avoid the death of your food processor's motor). Works a treat. I can also vouch for pre-mixing (and ever so slightly +ing) all additions aside from salt.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Made as written with the exception of the halvah which I excluded due to availability. I found unsweetened pistachio butter at my farmers market. The result was divine and a hit in my office. I didn't find them to be too sweet - the bitterness of the tahini was well balanced by the sweetness of the condensed milk (also adds texture). Don't skip on the roasted sesames or pistachios, they add a nice nuttiness and texture that really ups the ante. 10/10 would and will make again.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: This isn't a rice krispie treat - in the very best way!! This is SO much better than any that I've ever tried - and I've tried many because it's my husband's favorite treat! This is a total gamechanger.  And it has such a strong middle eastern flavor, reminding me of all the good things that came from my grandmother's kitchen.  I could go on but just want to say thank you!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: For those of us who are gluten free, what can stand as a substitute for marshmallow

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Fran. Most marshmallows are gluten free. You need to find GF rice puff cereal, however, as Rice Krispies are not GF.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Fran: Most marshmallows are gluten free. Just check the label.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Fran Trader Joe’s has gf marshmallows. There’s also a brand called Yummallow that’s sold at Walmart.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: If anyone can help—I would love to have a less sweet version without condensed milk? Suggestions?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
@JenBeau70
The original Rice Krispies treats does not call for sweetened condensed milk. You could play with adding additional ingredients to this recipe. The article mentions that using sweetened condensed milk extends shelf life. Perhaps, when creating a hack based on the traditional recipe that is best eaten within a day or two, the solution is to either make half a batch or invite a hungry horde to share the full pan.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I'm thinking of trying to make this but subbing out the sweetened condensed milk with coconut cream, which I would sweeten with some sugar. Anybody have any thoughts about that? Coconut cream is a bit thicker than coconut milk, though I think not as thick as condensed milk. Think it will work?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
Coconut cream hasn't the same texture of sweetened condensed milk, so it would change the textrure of the finished product. You could, however, take just over 1/2 cup of the coconut cream and simmer it down to 1/3 cup. If that's still too thin, add more coconut cream and simmer down again. 

I'd be tempted to simmer down an entire can until the texture was the same as the condensed milk, and if there is any left after using in the recipe, why, of course make another batch!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Donna in Brooklyn I have found coconut sweetened condensed milk at my local natural grocer’s. That would be a good substitute if you can find it.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @DarcieD This might work as a sub for evaporated milk, but condensed milk is different—it has a lot of sugar added.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Could this be better than the browned butter one published by the NYT in 2007/2008? Perhaps, but it is far easier and truly improves on the original with only one extra step and no extra ingredients: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/11587-caramelized-brown-butter-rice-krispies-treats

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Always interested in a new Rice Krispies recipe; I make them regularly for kids and this version is more “adult” like and probs won’t make it into my repertoire.  Shauna Sever author of Midwest Baking has a “next level” recipe that includes twice as much marshmallow as standard recipes and a bar of white chocolate —  a crowd pleaser especially with sprinkles or nonpareils.  Worth trying if you prefer a simpler recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: How about peanut or almond butter as a substitute?  Not going to go out of my way to get halvah (whatever that is) or even pistachio butter.  Fussy, many ingredients for a simple snack....however elevated it may seem.   They are still RCT!!!!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Halvah is amazing and the whole reason I wanted to try this!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I have some Ottolenghi “pistachio spread,” but it’s not sugar-free. Is the sweetened condensed milk absolutely necessary? I don’t want make it too sweet. OTOH, maybe I could drizzle melted, bittersweet chocolate on top as someone recommended? Maybe that would cut the sweetest enough

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Richard I agree with you.  Call me old fashioned, but the original simple recipe on the rice krispy box is too good to make any changes.  Sometimes I think that recipes have been created by AI - just make a list of ingredients and see what happens.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Why would I do anything with halvah but eat it? Next you’ll be giving me a recipe to make Kobe beef fajitas.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I couldn’t find Pistachio butter or paste so I used Hazelnut Almond butter instead and it came out fantastic.   I used a 9x9 pan to try and have smaller treats but it still filled the pan up to the top.   I don’t have enough Rice Krispies for another batch but thinking about doing half Rice Krispies and half Cheerios based on the comments.    Enjoy!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous! Would not change a thing. To make pistachio cream, I ground roasted pistachios in cuisinart with a little olive oil. Worked perfectly.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I love halvah, pistachios, and a gooey rice krispie treat so I was pumped to try this recipe. In the end, I was disappointed. Somehow combining all these flavourful elements resulted in an underwhelming final product.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
I'm in complete agreement. All
these awesome flavors and products just simply overwhelmed one another resulting in an overly sweet concoction instead of the divine confection I had hoped for.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
I love the concept and combined tahini, pistachio & halvah flavors. However, sadly this recipe did not work for me. My family is vegetarian and I could only find large vegetarian marshmallows. Somehow they melted lumpy instead of smooth and loose. The result was a dense mixture which did not coat the krispies uniformly. My family commented that they were ok tasting but on the salty side. Also, a pet peeve of mine are recipes that call for small amounts of different ingredients which adds up in cost and leaves a lot of opened unused leftovers. All in all, Not worth it. 

Now, what to do with a 2/3 full can of sweetened condensed milk?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I was able to find Dandies mini marshmallows at Whole Foods but I'm with you on the expensive single use ingredients. I'm balking at $16 for a jar of pistachio butter to use 2 T

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Make more squares : )  Or, some frosting recipes use sweetened condensed milk, if you happen to make a cake sometime soon.  Or, you could pour the remainder into a smallish frying pan over med-low heat, and stir slowly until it becomes light/medium brown and you will have dulce de leche to use as a filling, or to pour over ice cream.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Use the condensed milk for no-churn ice cream: easy and delicious. Here's one really good recipe: https://www.nigella.com/recipes/one-step-no-churn-coffee-ice-cream

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Having just made these, the only thing we would do differently is to make them into balls, then roll them in the halva/crushed pistachio topping. Otherwise, flavor is amazing!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Can peanuts be substituted for the pistachios? I have a tree nut allergy

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Sure, I don't see why not.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: We absolutely loved this!  It’s kitschy and chewy and far more interesting and delicious than the original treats.  Followed the recipe to the letter.  So fun.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: These were very good. Not sure if pistachio butter is needed. It is quite pricey and is overwhelmed by the sesame flavors (tahini, halvah and sesame seeds).

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: It is a time commitment. I recommend reading the recipe several times before proceeding and have all ingredients measured and ready.  I could not find pistachio butter but used almond butter instead.  No other substitutions or deviations.   I may have had the heat on the butter too low because it took twice the time to foam, settle, and brown (10 minutes).  Could not be happier with the result.  This pistachio halvah version is heads and shoulders above the standard  Rice Krispies Treat.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I made these and while they are tasty -- browned butter adds a nice caramel flavor -- they are too sweet. I used condensed unsweetened coconut milk, so that is not the cause of the excess sweetness. I also used jarred marshmallow fluff instead of marshmallows because the ingredient list for marshmallows is appalling. And the ingredients for the jarred fluff were not actually too bad. However both the fluff and the Rice Krispies are sort of naturally sweet, and together they are too much.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
I had to make these vegan for my vegan family members and it was still really good! Used Mykonos vegan butter, Trader Joe’s marshmallows and sweetened condensed oat milk (Nature’s Charm brand that I found at Whole Foods). The butter and marshmallows took longer to melt and smooth out but otherwise no problem.

For the pistachio butter I used the food processor and made it myself. No other ingredients needed. (Next time I’ll make extra pistachio butter because it was so good!)

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Would these freeze well?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: For those looking for pistachio butter, your local Middle Eastern grocer should carry it. That is where I found some to make this recipe. It was pricey, but sooooooo worth it to make these Rice Krispie treats!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I made this vegan by using plant butter (I did not try to brown it as a vegan chef on Reddit said to add tahini to get the nutty brown butter flavor and there is a lot of tahini in this recipe) and condensed, sweetened coconut milk. It was delicious decadence.  Just a note - a double batch is a lot!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Would the product pistachio cream work? Costco sells this product by the brand Nutty Fruity.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: My bad for trying to find these ingredients at an affordable price in a metro area under a million people. Even after shopping at international markets and "cheap" grocery stores I think an edible gold version would have been more cost effective. I'll be cutting this into at least 12 servings to get my money's worth.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: A note for cooks using pistachio cream instead of the specified pistachio butter.  Pistachio cream is a sweeter, silkier more dessert-y element typically has some vegetable oil and sweetener mixed in with the ground pistachio paste.  If you're using pistachio cream and then think this is too sweet, it could be that ingredient is causing it to be so.  Pistachio butter is typically ground pistachios and salt.  Also, your source matters.  Fiddyment Farms (pistachio grower CA) is a good one.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Wow - this is gonna set a trend if it hasn't already.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Pistachio Butter isn't the easiest thing to find...

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Sam: you can make your own pistachio butter in a food processor; but it's getting easier to find lately due to the popularity of Dubai chocolate (NYT has a recipe, of course!)

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I'll betcha this would be better with homemade marshmallow fluff...

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @LisavS Take it another step and make your own vanilla extract too.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Okay, now the task is to get these flavors and textures into a dessert that has, oh, 1/2 the calories? other than cutting the portion in half? Serious question for the recipe tinkerers!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Brenda I think that in the comments of this other sesame RK recipe someone mentions a vegan marshmallow brand that works: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1024985-black-sesame-rice-krispies-treats?smid=ck-recipe-iOS-share However, most people say that using the vegan marshmallows doesn’t work for RK treats. You’re probably better off trying a different dessert entirely.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Dandies makes a vegan marshmallow.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Brenda Marshmallow Fluff is gelatin free but does contain egg whites, which may or may not suit your particular needs. If using Fluff, a ~7 oz. jar is a good ratio for the 6 cups of cereal called for in this recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: If you’re making this for someone who is gluten intolerant, use rice Chex instead of Rice Krispies.  Tastes the same but a little more crunchy.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Nanci Parker there are GF puffed rice cereals. Nature’s Valley makes one, for instance.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @CV  Aldi crispy rice cereal and Walmart crispy rice cereal (Malt O Meal) are both certified gluten free.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Although summer fruit desserts are at their best right now, I was compelled to make this, and it was delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone actually Made this recipe yet?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: This was featured in "The Morning" newsletter today with the suggestion to "Bake" it.  I've looked all through the recipe and it appears that there's not any actual baking involved.  But it does look tasty and I wonder if anyone has made it using Cocoa Krispies instead.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I made these and didn't think they were much better than regular rice Krispie treats and they are way more expensive. I have used browned butter before and I do think that is worth doing but not all the other add-ons.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I made these treats and they were really delicious. Only problem was that while I followed the recipe precisely there was far too much of the mixture to fit into an 8x8 baking dish. I used a 9x13 and the mixture filled the dish right up to the top. Not complaining as the final yield was more than the 16 bars indicated. I don’t think I did anything wrong: I didn’t want to tamp it down too much, but even doing so would not have made it possible to use the 8x8 dish. Did anyone else have this problem or did I just not get all the “snap, crackle and pop” out of my Rice Krispies??

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: The timing couldn't have been more perfect. A friend just gifted me with a jar of pistaschio butter from Greece when I saw this recipe in my inbox. It definitely elevates the rice krispies treat of my childhood!  Didn't have any halva so omitted that, and also didn't condensed milk as the mixture was sweet enough without it. Gooey, delicious, and also a treat for the eyes--though to be honest, I was missing the pistaschio flavor despite the nut butter and the chopped nuts!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: These were absolutely amazing! I followed the recipe, no changes. Everyone loved them.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: One batch with drizzle of dark chocolate, the other per recipe. Both delicious treats. Was able to order ingredients from WalMart (yes WalMart, finding wide range of products with delivery faster and more dependable than Prime, no influencer or product placement fees received).

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Make it next-level with homemade marshmallows!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody:
This was delicious! Great flavor profile. Had some pistachio flour, which is just finally ground pistachios, that I added in as well. My only tweak was to crush a few sea salt flakes on the top.

Those folks who suggested some drizzled chocolate on top have a great idea. Will add that next time.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I made these Pistachio Halvah Treats and left out the sweetened condensed Milk. It was pretty hard to mix in all the parts as it set up fast once the cereal was added, but oh, Lordy, what a tasty and extra special treat! These are going to be bomber at my work retreat tomorrow!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: These Rice Krispy treats are too sweet and really not worth the cost of the ingredients. The plain old doctored ones with a touch of salt, real unsalted butter, and a tsp of vanilla are far superior. Adults and kids alike can appreciate the OG's!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: 7/2/25. Took about 45 minutes to prep. Cooled in fridge 1 hour. Very sticky on foil - spray or use parchment. Very tasty and rich, way more sophisticated than regular Rice Krispie treats. Extra ingredients are pricey though and needed to be ordered from Amazon.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Didn’t use the condensed milk, just added more vanilla and a bit of water to the tahini. Next time I’ll use less halva, which added nice texture and flavor, but added too much sugar for my taste.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: These were good but I agree they were WAY too sweet. And I am someone who usually scoffs when people say that. I ended up cutting off the top layer to get rid of all the extra halva (which, again, I love!). I just ground my own pistachios into "butter" which worked fine, and I used plain halva since it's what I had on hand. Plenty pistachio-y.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I made this yesterday and they were a big hit. Really delicious but very very rich. I would think that chocolate would overpower the pistachio and halva and you'd lose the Middle Eastern taste. Also, they're very gooey, which makes me think I'll use less condensed milk next time.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: First time I didn't have thini so I used cashew butter. They were amazing! Second time I used tahini, my husband loved them even more. I preferred the first batch so I'll have to trade off.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: 11 ingredients v. 3 for the original cereal box recipe! If you manage to find all of them, you may have to make these more than you want. It would be very helpful if recipes like this indicated which ingredients are optional or have handy pantry substitutes.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this with vegan butter and vegan marshmallows?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Yummy.  Thinking of throwing in a little cardamom next time.  I also forgot to buy any condensed milk and so just added 50% more marshmallows and butter and it came out fine.  Made a recipe and a half and it filled a 9x13 pan nicely.  Made them for a work party, but with four twenty-something’s at home, I had to leave some for the gang.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I hate that this calls for ALMOST a whole box of Rice Krispies, or ALMOST the whole bag of marshmallows. Just make a recipe that uses the whole box and whole bag!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: @Jennifer to solve for this I doubled the recipe so that it uses a whole large box of Rice Krispies and a standard 8-oz box of halvah.  It got rave reviews

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: I used a 9" square pan instead of an 8" because it's what I have -- and I liked the thinner bars that came out. Since I only had sweetened pistachio paste, I worried that using condensed milk would give me an overwhelmingly sweet bar. I subbed in more pistachio paste and tahini and omitted condensed milk.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: In response to @SBa, yes, the puffed rice from Indian groceries have a different texture.  I add it to my granola before baking just to lighten up the granola.  For these, I think Rice Krispies would maintain their crispiness better in the face of melted marshmallows.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: Can these be frozen?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: This recipe didn’t work for me. My mistake for using pistachio cream with sugar in it because these treats turned out much too sweet. Also 1.5 tsp is too salty. And, if possible, they’re too gooey! I cut a small square and while holding it in the air it started drooping towards the floor. Am usually not disappointed by 5-star recipes but this one was a miss.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Pistachio Halvah Rice Krispies Treats Recipe
commentBody: This is so addictive. I just can't get enough.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I grew up eating that as a cheap meal made with canned lentils, tomato, shallots, hard boiled eggs and vinaigrette made from the bottom of the moutarde à l’ancienne pot. 
It’s healthy and affordable and I now make it to my close ones here in the US. I sometimes add blood orange, fennel, cumin and flat parsley   mint to make it fancy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Madame_P

Definitely better when I added orange too!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Madame_P I added some chopped cherry tomatoes and avocado. Freshened it up with a drizzle of oil and some grated lemon. A perfect Neal.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Madame_P I added shaved fennel as well and loved it! Need to try the blood oranges.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If the dressing is added to warm lentils the flavor will permeate them more effectively.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @outofstate great tip! This recipe is in fact VERY similar to one published by saveur many moons ago. Core veg are beets carrots and shallots which are roasted with the lentils and thyme. The nearly identical dressing is poured over when it comes out of the oven - it is absorbed a bit but not so much that the salad dries out. So good!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @lisa - I had to look up this Saveur recipe! For anyone else interested, here it is: https://www.saveur.com/article/Recipes/Lentilles-du-Puy-with-Roasted-Carrots-and-Beets/

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Paula The Saveur recipe looks great - thank you!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Shortcut: use Trader Joe’s precooked lentils! They are delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Chef du maison I find TJs precooked lentils overcooked. If you like lentils even a little al dente cook yourself.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Chef du maison Lentils take very little time to cook so there is no reason to use a lesser quality product in the interest of saving time, and the end result will be qualitatively better.

author: Lisa Donovan
Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love French lentils and use them in salads like this one frequently. The veggies used change with the seasons:  spring asparagus barely cooked and garlic chives. In summer tomatoes juicy and bright in color and flavor. In winter, I’ve been known to add sweet potatoes or  butternut squash. Prosecco vinegar is an elegant choice with good olive oil, but plain old lemon juice works too. And herbs, dried or fresh tie it together. So folk, have fun, go to Paris or Rome or some beach in faraway Asia for a culinary adventure. Use the joy of this salad to enhance your everyday life.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!  I used sherry vinegar and left out the honey.  LOVE the tarragon!  I’ll add some roasted walnuts tomorrow if we still have any left.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @ReneI agree.  The combination of fresh tarragon, dill and parsley are SO good in this.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am amused by this recipe. It’s a variant of one my family has been gormandizing for sixty years. Think of it as a framework! It lends itself to just about any bean  instead of or in addition to the lentils, pretty much any vegetable or fruit that can stand up to a couple of days in a vinaigrette and of course, cheese and nuts.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You can replace the 1 T Dijon mustard with an equivalent measure of whole mustard seeds.  It retains the flavor and gives the salad a nice extra little crunch.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Dashathisdashathat or just use country mustard and maintain the well blended infusion.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Dashathisdashathat. I found that the mustard seeds added a bitter aftertaste to my salad. They were black mustard seeds. Could that have been the problem?  Wish I had used Dijon.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @RDJohn sorry about the bad experience! I’ve never done it with black seeds, always used yellow. Should have specified.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: THANK YOU for putting the ingredients in weights instead of just descriptions (oz vs "a bunch" etc). So very helpful!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @RudisHuman absolutely! I came here to say the same thing!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: But I LIKE the idea of fresh herbs and raddichio! and small chunks of raw carrots.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love the story behind this, and the great writing, I'm also a southerner ,living  in France, and she really captures the spirit of cooking there. I have been to many potlucks in France, and this wonderful salad almost always makes an appearance in some form, along with many other creative chopped salads that the French love. I look forward to making her version. I make a similar version of this, but served warm, and topped with a fillet of grilled salmon.

author: Lisa Donovan
