commentBody: I will use lentils here in a variety of ways, including a soup with onion, a bit of garlic, and whatever herbs I can dig up. But to give it a kick, I will cook up French lardons (small cubes of bacon) then add them in at the end. A nice contrast and more flavorful protein. For this salad, leaving it sit for a day to marinate then adding the freshly cooked lardons would work well.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Love the flavor. Took me 2 hours to pull it together, but I'm slow a cook. I can see this being a standby for summertime light dinners.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious. Anchovies instead of salt in the dressing. Added toasted pine nuts and some chevre.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Oy my dear, skip the honey. And too many herbs, just use dill. That is the everything and the kitchen sink approach to cooking these days. All dishes end up.tasting the same.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Catherine Shalen
Also, using small amounts of 6 fresh herbs that have to be bought in much larger quantities is ridiculously expensive.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @lrubin But it does create a lovely complex flavor, esp if you happen to keep a small herb garden and already have everything on hand. It is peak season for many herbs now. <3

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @lrubin. This is a recipe that really showcases those summer herbs, and may entice to use/experiment with herbs every day. I used some dried herbs which was a good substitute. Herbs bring so much to the table.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you’re cooking whole green French lentils for the first tine, know that their cooking time is more like 40 minutes than 12-20 minutes. Other than that, a great recipe and excellent with halved hard-cooked eggs .

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @latenightsolver the time is related to the age of the lentils. 40 min sounds like lentils that were harvested several years ago. This years harvest can easily cook in 12.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In step 2, « Line a pan with a towel » - does this mean a paper towel »?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Either will work just fine, it is just a question of whether you toss the kitchen towel in the wash or toss the paper in the garbage.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: 1 tablespoon salt seems like an awful lot for this salad. Shouldn't it be 1 teaspoon?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It really isn't too much.  The lentils are cooked without any salt at all, and really need it.  If you use Diamond Kosher (which is less salty than table salt), 1 TBS is fine.  If you are using regular table salt (e.g. Morton's), you might start with 2 TSP and then adjust accordingly.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I used 1 (scant) teaspoon and found that enough.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @JP I agree. I had leftover dressing and used it to dress a green salad and it is way too salty. I would use a scant teaspoon next time too.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Absolutely magnificent. I just made this as directed except I only used 1 teaspoon of salt instead of the 1 tablespoon stated in the recipe. Served with fresh sautéed cod and sautéed tomatoes. Can’t wait until tomorrow to see how this salad develops. Thanks NYT cooking and the lovely “roadside food “ article.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This looks good and similar to lentil salads I've made, but it seems too many herbs here. Rosemary, thyme, bay leaf - fine, parsley, fine. Then you really start competing here when adding in the strong taste of radicchio, AND tarragon, AND dill. I think you have to pick and choose the herbs you use or risk a salad tasting a bit confused. I'd go with the first 3, or go with the tarragon and omit the top 3, or go dill and aromatics. I feel like it's an American tendency to think more is more.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Susan

But have you tried it yet?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Triple the herbs and top with fried onions or crunch potato chips.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious and flexible!  I usually follow the recipe the first time I make something, but that wasn’t feasible.  The probability that my local grocery store would have radicchio hovers around zero.  Next time, I will wrap the bouquet garni in cheesecloth so I don’t have to pick rosemary leaves and thyme stems out of the cooked lentils.   Specific substitutions include a red onion instead of shallots and using veggies on hand instead of radicchio.  This will definitely go into the summer rotation!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @TheLifeChaotic I have taken to using a big tea ball and smash and squeeze herbs and garlic cloves inside. No picking out stems and super easy to clean out.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: When serving this salad for ladies’ buffet luncheons or buffet-style afternoon tea for 40, I present individual portions on an endive leaf, or in a radicchio leaf “cup,” on large serving platters. Everyone raves about it!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Just finished making this with TJ’s steamed lentils and the better part of a bag of Cruciferous Crunch. I added some toasted walnuts and goat cheese. Delicious and very adaptable.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@ReenieB
I find TJs cooked lentils overcooked. I like lentils with a little more of a bite.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @ReenieB genius to add cruciferous vegetables. So eat bought already sliced.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
We've made a salad nearly identical to this for the past 25 yrs when a friend introduced it to us at her home on St. Barths. The only difference- the addition of tarragon vinegar and lardons (we now use diced pancetta). She cooked the lardons slowly in olive oil to render as much fat as possible, scooped out the crisp bits, and then made the dressing (  tarragon vinegar) in the skillet in which she'd prepared the lardons as the lentils cooked. She then combined it all after draining the lentils. 
Over the years, we've topped it with crumbled goat cheese, stuffed it into fat heirloom tomatoes, and eaten it directly from the storage bowl - refrigerator door still open. It's that good.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Lardons would be especially welcome if a particularly bitter variety of radicchio was used.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Wonderful salad to make and eat.  We ate it with a lovely pasta main. We let it marinate for only two hours, but that little time really enhanced its flavor profile.  After 3 or 4 days the layers of flavor must be off the chart.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I was blown away. I never make salads (I'm a relatively newbie in the kitchen). My husband claims to be suspicious of salads. We couldn't even find radicchio in a few grocery stores here in NYC. But this was OUTSTANDING FLAVOR. I had my doubts when shoveling a considerable volume of shallots into the mix, but holy moly. I had it for dinner and just had leftovers for breakfast! Also made with Mark Bittman's whole roast chicken - pairing together is lovely!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: First formal recipe of any kind I've ever tried (Gen-X 1.0 dude, not proud).  Instructions were straightforward and clear, so thanks for that mercy.  I made 1.5x everything so it will last us awhile, though I didn't upsize the salt amount.  Just two changes due to the market being out of ingredients: dry tarragon for fresh, and escarole for radicchio.  It took me two hours because of the chopping/leaf stripping, etc.  It'll go more quickly next time, and there will be a next time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Very good. I'd like to some VERY thin slices of the raw carrots.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Use a vegetable peeler for thin slices of raw carrot. I sometimes add sultanas to this salad, and sometimes dress with yogurt, simply seasoned with cracked pepper and tarragon.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Richard  A friend once introduced me to lentils with sour cream (now I use yogurt) and Worcestershire sauce. It's an unexpected combination that is better than the sum of its parts.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love French green lentils and make several salads with them. Looking forward to this one. I just want to alert anyone who is not familiar with them that there are now imposters - lentils that claim to be French, and look like French green lentils, but do not have the texture and flavor of true French lentils. I now buy imported lentilles du Puy to be sure I get the real thing.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My Tropézien aunt and great aunt INSIST on toasting the lentils in a hot lightly oiled pot until nutty and aromatic, then add water, add mirepoix, the bouquet, and lots of salt (as salty as pasta water should be). THATS how they get as much flavor into the lentils as they do.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: 2 teaspoons of pepper looks like a misprint. Half a teaspoon was fine for me. I was picking Rosemary leaves out of the lentils. I suggest putting the bouquet garni  in a cheese cloth bag. Delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @river red. I am thinking of squishing the herbs into a tea infuser.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @river red Agreed! We unfortunately questioned this amount of pepper intuitively a moment too late after adding it. Everyone agreed that the pepper was out of balance, but the overall recipe is fantastic. I also agree with the comments about beginning with less salt and working up. It’s also lovely with red cabbage if you don’t have radicchio.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @J'ba thats exactly what I did.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you don't have kitchen twine for the bouquet garni, consider using a small tea ball to hold it instead. Then just lift out the tea ball when finished and empty it. Works for me all the time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. Popped the lentils in the freezer to help them cool more quickly. Loved the flavor and wouldn't change a thing! One of my new summer lunches!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was so perfect! I added a little bit of fresh mint because I didn’t have tarragon leaves. Was lovely. When I plated it I put a small handful of arugula leaves on top with a squeeze of lemon, drizzle of olive oil and a sprinkle of flakey salt. Was a nice presentation.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was fantastic. Checks all the boxes: healthy, wildly adaptable, and great for leftovers. I most definitely did not line the lentils on a towel to cool. Just left them in the strainer and added to the bowl while warm. Halved the radicchio and added kale from the garden. I don’t grow tarragon, so subbed for oregano. Used dry bay leaves. Topped with candied pecans for some crunch. Paired with a simple roast steelhead trout with lemon and chili flakes. 10000/10

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Half way through I regretted how involved this is but after inhaling a bowl I seriously want to eat this every day

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I thought this was delicious! I subbed in fennel for the radicchio, because that’s what I had on hand.  Also skipped the tarragon because I don’t love it. Otherwise made as described.  I see people mentioning the salt level, and I can’t speak to that - I always salt to taste, a generous pinch at a time.  When serving the leftovers next day added some crumbled feta and a spoonful of capers to change it up - totally worked!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This salad is so good. I didn’t have dill or tarragon but will try the next time I make this. I also subbed apple cider vinegar since that’s what I had in hand. For us, about 1 cup dry lentils would be enough, I like the texture of more crunch with the radicchio. The honey is an absolute must.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A pound of lentils surely needs a tablespoon of kosher salt. But whisking it into roughly half a cup of oil, vinegar and lemon juice (a challenge in and of itself) yields a sharp, salty result. Not bad, just means you need other dishes, fish or other protein, to complement and distract. Next time I’ll add the salt to the lentils when they are 10 minutes shy of being done and make this traditional French vinaigrette in the traditional way: with salt and pepper to taste, understanding that lentils may need more than a green salad. Use the herbs in any combination that suits your personal taste, but certainly keep the quantity of fresh parsley which could even stand alone. Love the combination of root carrot, chicory radicchio and generous amount of shallots. This is a versatile keeper, although I’m not quite sure how to rate it because of the salt treatment.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Lovely with sautéed zucchini and spinach, and roasted sweet potato.  I had some leftover horseradish honey mustard to use in place of Dijon.  Nice light meal

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I added a container of baby arugula to fill the salad out a bit, and it was great! I’m surprised how much flavor the garni bouquet gave the lentils.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Fresh bay leaves?!!  Never seen them in a market anywhere.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Marie In Chattanooga, our Whole Foods and Publix, and sometimes TJ, all have fresh bay leaves. I buy a bag and keep them in the fridge for as long as they last. So much more flavor than even the Penzey’s dried ones.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I have a bay leaf tree.  I bought it in the Bay Area, California 8 years ago, and it is now happily living in Oregon.  I keep it in a large pot.  I bet you can order it online.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Chatt fan you can also wash the fresh ones, let them dry fully and freeze them! Almost as good as fresh!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is far and away the most delicious lentil salad I have ever eaten--thank you so much!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is my second note.  After three days, the flavors blend and improve, IMO.  I always thought the flavors were excellent.  The texture does not change, the lentils are the same, and the carrots remain crunchy.  I will make the same general recipe again, and there is a lot of room for improvisation.  For me, the key is the vinaigrette flavor.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Love this dish, and bonus so does my wife! One note is that as written it’s much too salty for us, so I cut the salt in the dressing by half. We adapt the veggie additions to whatever is in our CSA share, and it always works!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’m regretting the 1T of salt. It’s too much!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Very tasty, satisfying, and filling. Additional vinegar and lemon juice, along with a healthy dose of salt, brightens the proceedings and boosts the flavors of the herbs and radicchio.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Found the dressing too strong for my taste.  The second day the lentils were soggy and unappealing.  The subtlety of the radicchio was lost and the two shallots used tasted very strong!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: As suggested, I added the dressing to the lentils when still a bit warm which was delicious, but they soaked up all the dressing. I would double the dressing quantity. Also the raw shallot and garlic were too hot for me. Tasty when eaten but lingering on my tongue for hours and hours. Next time, I will soak the shallot and garlic in the vinegar and lemon while the lentils cook. Finally, I didn’t find radicchio at the store so used mustard greens. They were delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Couldn’t find raddichio so I used arugula. Delish!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Just made this and served to my family alongside seared salmon fillets. What a revelation! I love the massive amount of herbs.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was terrific and easy. I added different vegetables and herbs.  It seems you can add whatever vegetables you like would work with the lentils and the dressing.  I cooked the lentils and soaked it in the dressing the day before serving, but I added the vegetables on the day I served it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is a pain but I followed the recipe and it is sublimely fabulous. It might be the most delicious healthy food I've ever  eaten.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was perfectly delicious in every way. I admit it seemed like a lot of work but I was bored anyway and had the time. I only used 1 teaspoon of salt like many other commenters. I skipped the honey because I couldn’t open the jar. I didn’t measure the pepper and used about 20 grinds. This salad created a flavor unique unto itself. Bravo,

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This is delicious!  I use about 4 carrots and cook them briefly in the microwave to soften. 
1 shallot is enough for me and I use a bit less salt and pepper. I'm not too fond of radicchio so I sub spinach or kale.  Adding the warm lentils to the dressing makes them more flavorful as another reader suggested.  Top with some crumbled feta and it's a fabulous meal!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is a LOT of salt: "In a large bowl, combine vinegar, olive oil, mustard, honey, thyme leaves, half the lemon juice, 1 tablespoon salt and 2 teaspoons pepper;" Something wrong? I used 1 teaspoon and it tasted great.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Followed the recipe semiclosely. I skipped radicchio (me no likey) and in its stead used lightly steamed asparagus and thinly sliced radishes. Superb results.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I don't quite get the reference to "chicory" in the description?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Radicchio is in the chicory family.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Beth
I believe chicory describes a bitter leaf like radicchio or endive. I meant that the bitterness of the radicchio combines nicely with the sweeter carrots, and that you could achieve a similar result with different vegetables if you pay attention to those flavor profiles.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Maybe it is just my taste... this dish a bit too bitter for me.  But because of the raves this recipe has received, Ill try making it again with less vinegar and mustard.  With that said, I agree... Trade Joe's pre-cooked lentils were wonderful and so simple!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: As french cook of Lyon i would not use sweet potatoes or honey. Besides this really, add what you have in your kitchen; crunchy red cabbage, roasted slices of garlic, shallots, arugula or any herb from the garden.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was really delicious and transformational for me…i hadn’t ever used parsley stems alongside the leafs. Even my zero-kitchen-waste Italian mom and nana didn’t use the stems fresh- they went into pasta sauce, or were the freezer and saved for soup stock. The parsley stems added a delicious boost of flavor and texture and I will def be incorporating them into more salads instead of throwing in the compost or the freezer!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Spectacular with a little leftover roast duck!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Made a dressing using Penzy’s Greek seasoning and both lemon juice and red wine vinegar.  Also added toasted walnuts and grated French Feta. Served it in radicchio cups.  Lots of fresh chopped parsley, diced the carrots. Skipped the onions/shallots. Drizzled with a bit of aged balsamic.  

This combo is so good, travels well, even through airport security.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Amazing, bright, refreshing. Perfect as written, this will be a new go-to.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Yummy and versatile. Amount and type of herbs can be adjusted to your preference. We served it with smoked sausage, a traditional French accompaniment but I can imagine it with grilled chicken as well. One warning, cooking lentils de Puy for 40 minutes as was suggested in another comment will result in mush.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was delicious, but why put any of the herbs in the boiling water except the bay leaf? You drain and rinse the lentils and the herbs’ flavor is wasted. Just add the fresh herbs to the dressing or directly to the salad. I didn’t have radicchio (used celery) or dill or tarragon (used fresh basil) or honey (used maple syrup) and it was still great.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was the most delicious lentil salad I've ever made or tasted. worth the extra steps and ingredients.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this for a dinner party and it was eaten up.  This was easy delicious and works.  I Had roasted vegetables and Salmon.   I will make it again.  I did not change the recipe, no need to it worked.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Delicious. Added sauted fennel and multicolored carrots, greens…, onion, no shallot. Did add orange as was suggested, and adding toasted, skinned walnuts. Pairs well with French green beans and crusty bread with good butter.
A keeper!! Thank you! Oh…for me the 1T of salt (K) was perfect.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this as an alternative to Julia Child's version; I found this version too vinegary. And I love a lot of vinegar. Will stick with Julia's in 'The Way to Cook'.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The combination of lentils and vegetables is great.  But, I recommend using half of the dressing.  You can always add more, to taste.  We found the salad was overdressed when I poured in all the dressing at once.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
It's a cold, rainy day here in Australia and this recipe has brightened my day up no end. 
The last of the tarragon from the garden as well as some parsley went in along with a home grown lemon (so, so many lemons this year).
It's so zesty and zingy it has to be good for you. 
Can't wait to make it in summer,  when everything (except my lemons) are in season. 
Also,  reckon it would go great with fennel... might try that next week.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Excellent! I've never cooked (cooled) lentils this way and I'm sold on the method. Great flavors and texture. Topped with toasted walnuts and goat cheese for an extra treat. This one's a keeper!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This didn’t come out well for me at all, but at least it took forever and I got to buy four separate types of fresh herbs.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Fun fact about raddichio - unless they are exposed to frost and periods of cold, along w periods of darkness, they’ll remain incredibly bitter.  I don’t buy chicories from CA for this very reason.  Chicories from the Pacific Northwest however are much more mild and delicious.  We grow them in northern MN.  Deer won’t touch them until the frost comes, then they disappear!

(Love this salad!  I sub red cabbage when I can’t find a good raddichio.)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @MFKI make this salad often with whatever veggies I have… golden beets, arugula, etc… but subbing red cabbage for radicchio is a great idea

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I made this for my weekly meal prep - subbed the radicchio for roasted beets (both a red and a golden beet wrapped in foil and roasted for ~45mins) and it is absolutely delicious. 

This will be a staple for me this summer and I look forward to trying it with different seasonal veg!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made recipe as written.  Family found it rather "meh."  Son who rarely ever complains about food found it too bitter, which I suspect was from all the parsley.  It made a lot of salad that our 3-person family struggled to get down over a couple of days.  Would not make again

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I swapped sherry vinegar for red wine vinegar and the store was out of radicchio so I used a small red napa cabbage and a couple of Belgian endives. My wife said, "this lentil salad is a keeper".

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: While I am a big fan of radicchio, I found it to be a little overwhelming in this.  I made the salad for myself, but if I were making it for a crowd, I think I'd cut the radicchio in half and add another carrot.  For a salad, it's a bit work-intensive to make the lentils as instructed.   I thought the dressing was perfect, though.  The salad has huge potential with a few tweaks.  I look forward to my 2nd serving after being in the fridge a day.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is a lovely dish as written. I am thinking of adding cooked farro for the second day, as my husband found the flavors very intense.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The salad base- the lentils- were too bland.  If I make it again, I'd double the 5 small rosemary sprigs and add more loose thyme leaves.  I'd double the carrots for more of a color pop; quarter the radicchio before slicing thinly; add a lot more, as in double, the dill and tarragon, salt and pepper.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I make a version of this using Trader Joe’s pre-cooked lentils. Makes a great salad or can be served warm over rice. For just my husband and I we get tired of one version of leftovers so I throw the lentil salad in an appropriate amount of veg broth and it makes a great soup. Such a versatile dish.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
So good! As with all NYT recipes, I doubled the time it would take to prepare because I'm not a knife whiz but it was worth it. I've made it for guests twice so far.

In step 3, the first time I made, it added 1 TBSP salt and it was sooo salty. Could that be an error?

Reduced to 1 TSP the next time I made it and that seemed more balanced.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Very easy, very customizable.  Added toasted walnuts. Not a sad desk lunch.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Just made the recipe as directed. My only addition was chopped pistachios and next time I will halve the carrots before slicing thin. The tarragon is small in quantity but mighty in flavor - don’t skip if you can. Served with grilled fish and fresh sourdough bread. Delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I recommend cutting out the parsley and instead using more dill as the greens / tarragon as the greens! I think that parsley and radicchio both contribute too much bitterness, and that just the radicchio is enough. I also used rainbow carrots for even more color! Great recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Sorry, this recipe didn't do it for me.  I'm a big fan of lentils, especially the Frenchy ones, but this tasted like eating grass.  
For those interested, Ina Garten does a nice lentil salad that is similar but without all (or most) of the green stuff.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I prefer to use less shallots because they seem to overpower this recipe. Otherwise, it is delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The intro mentions chicory but it's not in the recipe...?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Anna Chicory’s another word for raddiccio

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Honey isn't used in real French vinaigrettes but if you need your dressing sweet… Here in France, I cook my lentils with bouquet garni (purchased pre-assembled) and a few cloves stuck in a piece of carrot for easy removal, as well as chopped carrot if I have the time. Make sure not to overcook lentils so that they turn mushy; 15 minutes after the boil is my magic number. I agree with another commenter here, drain after cooking, rinse once, dress and serve warm!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Delicious! My radicchio was mild but it would still work had it been more bitter. I diced the carrots and air fried them instead of leaving raw. I also boiled the lentils in salted water with dried herbs mixed into the water, and decreased the salt in the dressing to two teaspoons to compensate for the salted water. Finally, I used two small red onions instead of shallots.

Some feta and toasted walnuts would not go amiss in this salad. So many possibilities!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: WAY too much salt in the vinaigrette. Despite the ratings on this one, I strongly believe this must be a typo - 1 tablespoon should instead be no more than 1 teaspoon. You can always add more later to adjust, but that full tablespoon was burning my mouth, as well as wilting the colors and volume of the vegetables much too soon before serving. Other than that, this is a good recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I agree with the salt comment but I prepared my vinaigrette before I cooked lentils,  letting my carrots and radicchio cure in the vinaigrette and found that my carrots were particularly soft, almost pickled.  I'm going to half the salt next time to see if I get the same result.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I used one teaspoon of salt and was enough-- I wonder if the tablespoon is a typo? Used a mandoline to cut carrot and radicchio super thin. Was perfect!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: hear me out..... roast the carrots. adds a nice little heartiness. yum!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I have been dreaming of the just-so-french lentil salad for awhile now and this met the brief. I used to subsist on the more mediterranean version from Weaver Street Market in Carrboro, NC (feta and roasted peppers). This is a perfect palette (palate?) to build on. Use any crunchy vegetables from your fridge. Don't have parsley or dill? I used fennel fronds.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you’re worried about leftover fresh herbs you can put them in ice-cube trays covered with a bit of water and freeze them. Good for throwing into other dishes later.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: After reading the recipe...(which sounds wonderful btw)...I see it calls for a whole tablespoon of salt for the dressing...I'm hesitant to use a whole tablespoon at once...asking honestly...does that much salt really work? ...Thanks in advance.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Diana H.  Once again, the recipe writer did not specify the type of salt so the answer is “it depends “.  The default salt for professional cooks is Diamond Crystal kosher salt so if you use that, it will be fine.  But if you use table salt like Morton’s iodized salt, it is twice as dense so twice as salty as Diamond Crystal and it will be too much.  If you use any other salt, like sea salt, start with half what the recipe calls for and adjust from there.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You could always add less salt, taste, and add more if you feel it needs more. That's the best way to add salt even if you are not second-guessing the recipe author.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Diana H. I was worried about the amount of salt, too, but I made it exactly as written, using 1 tbsp of coarse kosher salt, and it was delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Loved this.  Made it almost exactly as written, but based on others’ comments, I added the lentils warm to the dressing before mixing in the veggies and herbs.  I added toasted pepitas.  Really delicious, will definitely make again!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Where does the chicory come in? It’s in the first sentence of the description but I don’t see it in the ingredients list…

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Am I missing something?
Raddichio is also known as Italian chicory. It belongs in the same family as Chicory

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Needs more garlic. All herbs need to be fresh and fragrant.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this as written. It is 5 stars. I noted all the add ins and changes. I might include them in the days to come. I LOVE this! Got my lentils from Amazon. Got the last head of radicchio at my chain market. Made the sweet produce guy go look in the back for tarragon. The bouquet garni put the lentils over the top. A serious keeper.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Agreed! The dressing is outrageous.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There are few things more delicious than this a serving of this salad (warm) next to a glistening piece of roast chicken or this salad (cold) next to grilled salmon and slices of lemon.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: First time made it as is. Great flavor. Dressing is perfect. It's a beautifully looking salad if you serve it right away. (On day 2, the lentils were soggy, radicchio lost its color, and the salad did not look appealing.) Second time, I tweaked the quantities to my liking (cut the shallots to 3-4oz and parsley to 3oz, and upped the carrots to 10oz) and served after two hours. Added radicchio before serving. Had some candy cane beets and used them on top as garnish for extra cheer.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This salad is SO good and also made us very... bloaty. ;) The recipe doesn't suggest it, but I highly, HIGHLY recommend soaking the lentils in advance as I now know that will help remove much of the oligosaccharide sugar that causes the gas. Also wouldn't recommend eating this as a main dish! I'm hoping soaking will make these more digestable because this is so so yummy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: One tablespoon of salt in the dressing is WAY too much!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made with a few modifications. The radicchio I had was very bitter( note to self taste it before adding!) we don’t favor tarragon so subbed oregano with delicious results. Used Melissa’s cooked lentils. Added tomatoes, cucumber, golden raisins  and feta cheese. Also added a splash of Cara Cara orange balsamic vinegar at serving. That tamed the bitter radicchio and it was great!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was very a very tasty and hearty salad. I also added 3 strips of bacon, crumbled, into the salad to help season it a bit. Leftovers were perfect for lunch and dinner. Salads are the way to go this time of year.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: As others have said, this was even tastier after sitting in the fridge for several days. I was liberal with the parrots and also added some sliced radishes. Ate it over arugula with different toppings for lunch throughout the week. Very much liked a big dollop of full fat cream top yogurt (like creme freche and already in my fridge so no extra shopping needed) one day, a side of cottage cheese with my favorite lemon pepper another day, and the last day, a small avocado and a cubed nectarine on the side and salted pistachios on too.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: With all respect to the people who gave this 5 stars, we found this bitter and disagreeable.  I love lentils, but the combination of bitter radicchio, lemon zest and mustard was very unpleasant.  Also, it was too salty, and I only used half the salt called for.  I won't be making this again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Wait a sec! How are two medium shallots equal to 6 ounces?? Shallots confound me these days.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: 3 medium shallots or 6 ounces….this seemed like a huge difference in quantity. I weighed 3 medium to large shallots and it was 2 ounces. Tripling this seemed a bit much. Loved the flavors and would make again!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Is this not the best potluck dish that is low key vegan and gluten free, too? Double the recipe. You'll thank me later.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I soaked the radicchio in ice water for about 10 minutes to help cut the bitterness, and used all the fresh herbs except for dill, which I just didn’t have. Didn’t miss it…The salad was crunchy, flavorful and delicious. Mine turned out very peppery, probably because I used finely ground pepper instead of fresh ground pepper. Next time I’ll go lighter on the pepper but otherwise wouldn’t change a thing. Thank you for this delicious, healthy recipe!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Chef du maison, another way to save time is to make more lentils than you need, and freeze the extra in containers with enough water to cover. That way I can make quick individual grain bowls for lunches without worry of spoiling.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve made this prep several times.  The first time, I prepared as written and did not care for the radicchio.  The second time, I added pancetta and sliced cherry tomatoes but sans radicchio.  The third time, I added blanched asparagus, a mistake.  This fourth time, I added pancetta, sliced cherry tomatoes, and some sliced green chicory.  This method is my preferred method of the four tries.  Rice stars.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @drgonzo777 five stars

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Very tasty. Made as written 2 days ahead of an office party. It was very wet and the colors were not as vibrant so it didn’t look very appetizing by the time I brought it to the party, despite the recipe saying it was better when the flavors had a chance to meld. I thought the flavors were brighter on the day it was made. It also could use more lentils. Overall a good recipe just wouldn’t make ahead again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This looks fabulous and I'll try it, sans the honey. I'm not a fan of added sugars in whatever form.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Sounds pretty good. My suggested variations: 
1- adding pomegranate seeds to this salad and using pomegranate paste instead of lemon juice and vinegar would be a good twist. 
2- Replacing all or part of olive oil with tahini.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've made this with canned lentils and whatever cabbage and other crunchy veggies I have on hand. Since I don't cook the lentils myself, I thrown in a small amount of fresh or dried herbs - whatever strikes me. I've made more of the dressing to keep in the fridge so it is easy to make in small batches for lunch.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this for the third time, today with golden beets, radish and arugula. It is a lovely salad but there is no easy way to get around the extensive chopping. I use 1 cup lentils, do not rinse after cooking, and dress them while warm before combining the other ingredients. The only ingredient that I have not liked is the radicchio.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Mary Young I personally love radicchio, but also really like your addition of golden beets!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: One small head of raddichio ended up being way too bitter for my taste. Love the concept, but mine didn't turn out particularly well balanced.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
TOO MUCH SALT!!

The flavors are great, and I would make this again, but I would cut the salt in half.
We use coarse flake kosher salt, and it was salty to the point of mouth-burning, unable to finish a serving.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Robert completely agree!! I gagged when I tasted the dressing. Had to add more oil, more lemon, more honey over and over to reduce the salt ratio. A whole tablespoon?!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The lentils in the photo are not french green lentils.  Green lentils are smaller, rounder, and much darker green.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Love this recipe. I’ve made it as is, and most recently, with radish instead of radicchio and after realizing I didn’t have enough lentils, used half farro half lentils and it might’ve been even better.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For those who meal prep consider cooking double or triple the lentils, adding the (also doubled or tripled) dressing and, when completely cooled, freeze the portioned extras in flat zip bags or whatever works for you. The next time you want to make it grab a bag, thaw and add whichever veggies suite the day. Toss it and you’re done.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Annoyed about this recipe. I thought 1 Tablespoon of salt sounded like a lot, but I followed the recipe. It took a long time to prepare - and then I had to throw out. So salty it was inedible.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I don't see 1 tablespoon of salt listed in the ingredients? Did I miss something?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Found the 1 tbsp of salt in the instructions. I'm glad I have been warned about the salt.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I wondered about it, too, but used 1 T. table salt and loved the results (though I do like my food salty and cooked the lentils from dry, without salting the water). It is a bit surprising they don't specify what kind of salt to use, as I believe some taste saltier than others. Sorry your first batch didn't turn out!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The rosemary in my bouquet garnished fell apart and I had to pick through the lentils to get it all out. The contrast of bitter radicchio with dill and tarragon is very pungent, combined with rosemary and thyme and parsley, I found the combination of flavors to be unappealing. My partner agreed. Considering the amount of time it took to prepare this, and the specific ingredients involved I personally wouldn’t bother making this again. On the plus side, it’s a healthy and hearty salad, just not for me.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I used French lentils cooked in my Instapot and it worked great. Instapot timing is 8-10 minutes on LOW pressure. I also subbed a packed of fresh “poultry blend” herbs. So herb mix was thyme sage and rosemary plus the parsley.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My husband said this was his favorite lentil salad. It's mine too. The perfect balance of flavors and textures, and pretty. Usually I eat radicchio only in cold months. No longer. This is a year-round pleaser.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made a version of this with some small modifications based on preferences / what I had and it was excellent! I subbed in fennel for radicchio, sherry vinegar for red wine vinegar, and used lots of tarragon and a few bay leaves in lentils but no other herbs. Will definitely be making this again!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My lentil dish is 1 cup lentils, a bunch of carrots and unsalted, roasted sunflower seeds. I have low blood sugar, and this dish helps manage that. The seeds provide crunch, the carrots sweetness, and the lentils lots of protein and fiber. After seeing the ingredients for this French dish, I gotta try it!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is very good and added French Feta, olive oil and a squeeze of lemon on top of each serving. Agree with the comments to add the lentils to the dressing while they are still warm.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I should have listened to my instinct about two teaspoons of honey. Instead I ended up with a big bowlful of oddly sweet lentil salad. Really? Does everything need to be sweet? I like my savory dishes savory. Will not make this again and I’ll listen to my inner chef warning me next time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this 2 days ago. Followed the recipe mostly, but left out shallots and garlic because we can’t do raw alliums. Added Penzey’s dried onions to the dressing instead. Half the honey and sherry vinegar. Didn’t have tarragon and used brown mustard seed instead of Dijon. Used dried dill. It was wonderful and even better the next day. Definitely will do it again so I can have healthy and easy lunches. Am planting a tarragon plant and picking up dill at farmers market for the next batch.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: So delicious! Will have a place on my regular rotation. IDK what kind of lentils I was using, and I used the fresh and dried herbs I had. Turned out great. Guest worthy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this for a potluck yesterday and it was absolutely delicious. I used the herbs I had (parsley, and little bits of thyme, basil, and mint) as well as garlic scapes instead of garlic. I also cooked the lentils the night before in the instant pot (7 minutes on high, natural release), and it was pretty quick to put together the next day.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious and hearty. I divided the 1T of salt, adding some of it to the water when cooking the lentils, to start infusing them with flavor, and putting the rest in the dressing. I didn't find it too salty, as some reviewers did and am not sure if this is why. I used belgian endive instead of radicchio, and ran some radishes across the mandoline along with my carrots. I can see adding other veggies as they are in season. The lemony, garlicky dressing is divine and I will use it on other salads.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Definitely a keeper ! Have made it several times now this summer. The family loves it!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My friend and I both made this independently and she forgot to put the mustard in and it tasted so much better when I tried hers. I would leave out the Dijon. It’s too overpowering.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
my adjustments
- lavender white vinegar
- used sage, rosemary, bay and garlic for bouquet garnish
-used veggie bouillon for water
- used red cabbage
- used the entire bunch of parsley, chopped up
- added some green onion in addition to shallot
- used mint and basil as fresh herbs
- used lime instead of lemon
- DID NOT USE USE A TABLESPOON of salt in the dressing. Seemed like a mistake.

Was a hit!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this last night. Yum. Usually make a curry mayo to top lentils. Loved the vinaigrette. My husband said he liked it more than the curry mayonnaise.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe was good but not great (many NYT recipes could use a few more rounds of resting). The lemon was overpowering — next time I’ll do a vinaigrette (w balsamic or red wine vinegar). The lentils were bland — I will cook them w salt next time. The raw carrots were weird. I would sautee them a little. I would add chunks of feta too (I used to buy a lentil salad from a deli that did this). Text time. Some people love this recipe but I will heavily revise it if I make it next time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Adding diced celery (about 2 stalks) to the carrot mixture adds extra crunch.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I cut the carrots in tiny dices, barely bigger than the size of the lentils. You may add bits of fried chorizo of the ame size.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this and it was a big hit.  I didn't have radiccio so at the end of the lentil cooking I put in the carrots (i prefer them cooked) and swiss chard chiffonade.  Several guests asked me for the recipe.  The vinaigrette is so good (I also used dijon mustard with tarragon as well as fresh taragon). It's a keeper!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I had to make it without the garlic and shallots because my guest was sensitive to both, and it still turned out fabulous!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You must put herbs in a bag. I just tied them together and the rosemary leaves were everywhere and too punget to eat. Had to throw away the lentils😢

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe works equally well with quinoa and couscous.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Really liked cooking the lentils with this bouquet garni - great smell and flavour

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I cook the little French green lentils all the time to add to green salads. This looks delicious.  But I wonder if a TABLESPOON of salt is a typo!?  When I cook 1 cup of dried lentils and salt them at the end, I use probably less than 1/2 a teaspoon!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this pretty close to as written. I held back on the salt & pepper as others recommended but after a few hours in the fridge added a bit more. 😋 Parents loved it. I love the taste of the salad, and oh so healthy and satisfying. I used true French Lentils ordered online and I think it’s worth it. I used ALL the fresh herbs and froze the leftover chopped herbs. I think I will add Feta cheese tomorrow. Yum.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Lentils become a complete protein when combined with rice, nuts and seeds - This recipe can easily be modified for the protein craze crowd.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: THUS IS AMAZING!!!  Served it along with cold cooked quinoa and brown rice. Just outstanding!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hi found the dressing way too much and too vinegary, didn’t work for me (think I’m the only one!)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Linda agreed, I had to play with it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Tried this and loved it!  Had only plain green lentils, so not as pretty as the picture, but still delicious.  I didn't have fresh rosemary or thyme so put dried herbs in the bouquet garni.  It seems to work.  I also added salt to the lentils and didn't use quite as much in the dressing.  Definitely a make again

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I had to throw this out. The instructions said to use 1 Tbsp of salt. I did and it was too salty to eat.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I wrote earlier that the flavors did not meld when I made this lentil salad.  After 48 hours, it was very, very good.  But, it took all of 48 hours to get there.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: 6 oz of shallots seemed a bit too much. 3 medium to large shallots weighed 2 ounces. That said lots of onions with all the veggies and beans might be tasty.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious!  I've cut corners by using Trader Joe's steamed French lentils and a package of shreaded carrots.  Sliced radishes add a nice crunch.  I know I'll want all of the lemon juice and zest added, and I used lemon thyme.  A crowd pleaser.  It's a bonus for Vegans and GF people who rarely have something to eat at gatherings.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this exactly as instructed except I only put 1/2 the amount of tarragon (I’m just not a huge fan of this herb) and added a little more fresh dill and a about two teaspoons less salt and it is absolutely perfect! You will be fighting over the leftovers!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was a hit with everyone at a recent potluck.  I made it a day in advance, exactly as the recipe outlined*.  Doubled the recipe. Surprised by the amount of salt, but I bit my lip and dumped it in.  It was perfect.  Of the 9 guests, ALL asked to take some home, leaving us with none, but we will make it again!  *One omission: tarragon, because I couldn't find it fresh.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I added feta and roasted crushed pecans. I couldn’t find all the fresh herbs so substituted for dried ones. No radicchio in the market so substituted with arugula from what I read. I also pan fried the shallots and garlic. The rest I followed the recipe and turned out beautifully and yummy!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made a mashup of this recipe and the Saveur version linked below - roasting two small beets, the carrots tossed in olive oil, thyme and salt and pepper. Added to the cooled and dressed lentils (this recipe) with herbs and raddichio, and topped with crumbled chèvre. Chef’s kiss!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Such a lovely, lovely salad. I find if I don't have radicchio, a bagged salad with sturdy lettuce or something like kale works as a great replacement. (Leaving out the dressing, croutons, etc., of course. Use them for something else.)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For lentil lovers (I am one), this is great. As in the recipe, I used carrots and radicchio, and added thinly sliced fennel, which added more flavor dimension. Next time, I will add more vinegar and lemon zest to help the flavors pop. It keeps well in the frig for a couple of days (doesn't get mushy), but I needed to add more olive oil after it had been sitting in the frig.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Threw some roasted & salted pepitas over the the end product just to see what would happen. Added some really nice crunch and texture (especially on the leftovers)!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Wonderful recipe. Such a fresh and easy little salad. I couldn’t locate radicchio and subbed arugula. That was delicious, but I think you could make this with lots of different additions. The little green lentils are key. This one is going on the reg.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I cooked the lentils with herbs de provence as that's what I had on hand.  Really flavourful and wonderful dish!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A perfectly balanced, substantial and delicious salad. I have been enjoying it for lunch with some marinated goats cheese and walnuts. So happy to have found this recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe is nothing like the Saveur recipe that a commenter mentioned.  That makes lentils that are dark and roasty, while this makes lentils that are bright and fresh.  I've made them both and both are good, but they are quite different from each other.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Wow! Super delish. I skipped the tarragon (rosemary, thyme, dill, parsley, and bay leaves seemed like plenty of flavor, and I’m not crazy about tarragon), and apparently the Kroger and Publix by me aren’t carrying radicchio anymore, so I used … purple cabbage! It was a great substitute, I have no regrets! Great vegetarian dish for a potluck.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Maureen I came here to see if anybody used purple cabbage, and I’m thrilled to get this bit of advice. Thanks.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My own fault but friendly reminder to read the whole recipe first. I added all the lemon zest and juice to the vinaigrette before noticing it’s half the juice and add the zest at the end.  The salt and honey balanced it out though - so still super tasty.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious and versatile...I can see this being prepared with so many variations!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This will definitely not last in the fridge for four days. I just made it and it was so delicious I promptly gobbled some up. It will probably be even better tomorrow after the flavors have time to meld. The only change I made was to cut the honey to 1 tsp. I think some sliced castelvetrano olives would be a tasty addition, and maybe some crumbled feta.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve started putting bouquet garni herbes in a paper coffee filter and tying it closed in recipes like this.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Although green lentils don’t strictly need overnight soaking, it makes them easier to digest. They contain phytic acid and oligosaccharides (raffinose-family carbs), which can cause bloating and reduce mineral absorption. Soaking helps break these down before cooking.
Even if you cook lentils until soft, those compounds aren’t fully removed by heat alone. Soaking also helps them cook more evenly and a bit faster.
That said, if you tolerate lentils well, you can skip soaking and just cook them.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Loved all elements of this salad except the ratio. First time trying that and I had no idea it was so extremely bitter. Next time I will substitute cabbage or maybe kale. Everything else I will keep exactly the same!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Insanely good and perfect for summer! I made a portion with endive but didn’t add it to the entire batch; gets soggy after a day or two. It was a good addition but personally wouldn’t notice it missing either. 

I do agree with another comment about adding a little mint, and will give that a go next time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Substituted purple cabbage for radicchio, and it's delicious hot or cold! The recipe is generous, so you can eat on it for days.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: delicious! a French chef once recommended adding a clove or 2 to the bouquet garni for lentils and I have to say it's a winner. I urge you to give it a shot, but just 1 or 2.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I would really dial back the vinegar and/or lemon juice in this. The acidity overwhelms pretty much everything else in this salad, and I’ve eaten two servings more than 12 hours apart so the acidity doesn’t moderate on its own.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Very tasty and easy. I had forgotten how good french lentils are all on their own. I added fresh spinach in addition to the carrots and radicchio. Will be making on repeat.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is a superb recipe! I couldn’t find radicchio, so I used dandelion greens and that worked well.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Really fabulous salad. The bitterness of the radicchio and the sweetness of the fresh herbs (you can't have too many of them) make for a lively see-saw of textures and tastes. I also cooked up some farro and added that to the salad for a full protein offering. Good the second day too...we'll see how long it lasts (and I doubled the recipe!).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: 20250510 1st time. Got this in my NYT Food email this morning. I had all the ingredients, except radicchio. I subbed in Red Cabbage. I made it before lunch and mixed in a bit over half the dressing, then tossed, covered, refrigerated. Right after mixing, before chilling, it tastes so good. I shall serve it with Grilled Salmon and hot rice.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I liked this but did not love it... definitely would add some grilled vegetables to this recipe or perhaps serve it warmed

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Didn't change a thing and loved every bite.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Prepping all of the herbs took me much longer than the author, but it is a very delicious salad. My grocery store didn’t have radicchio so I substituted lacinate kale cut up in small strips and massaged.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've been making this (variety of herbs) for about 20 years. It makes a great bagged lunch. Filling and healthy and DELISH!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Eating this for lunch this week. Don't love it. It came out kind of soggy. Taste is pretty good, but the texture just isn't doing it for me.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I added farro to this to give it some extra substance and texture and it worked really well

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I substituted the radiccio for kale, left out the tarragon and added radishes. One of the best salads I've had in a long time. It also holds up good to make in advance for lunches for a few days

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. Two years ago I enlisted my family in making legumes a bigger part of our diet, but lentils have largely been relegated to soups. For this I used French lentils, skipped the dill (just didn't feel like trying to track it down) and went a little heavier on the tarragon (which is simply magical in this dish). I also used "mild" treviso radicchio since it was available at same cost. My 15 year old son declared he'd "eat the (love) outta (this salad)."  Even better on day two!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Thanks for tarragon comment - a favorite flavor.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love this dish! A bit of chèvre sprinkled on top along with some olive oil is a great addition.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I don’t understand what everyone sees in this salad.  I thought it was over-vinegared.  Has anyone tried it with lemon juice instead of vinegar?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: "Standard" vinaigrette proportions are along the lines of 3 parts oil to 1 part vinegar, so you are correct in your assessment of this recipe.  I like things dishes like this a bit "over-vinegared, though I like a leafy green salad more in line with the 3:1 standard.  Generally speaking, it's useful to know one's personal preferred basic ratio and use that as a guide, from which you may (or may not!) decide to depart on any given occasion!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I used red lentils as that’s what I had, and turned out delicious. Omitted tarragon as I didn’t have it on hand, kept everything else the same. Gives such a fresh summery flavour, and loved the texture of the starchy lentils with crunchy carrots and radicchio.  Paired well beside steak, potatoes, and asparagus. It is impressive how inexpensive these ingredients are for the elevated flavour and sophistication it delivers. A keeper salad.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
delicious, I served with beets and tangerine salad, and a roasted coho salmon, heavenly
will do over many time, thank you NYT!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I kept the basic proportions but modified the ingredients slightly because of what was on hand. Dried thyme instead of fresh. No fresh tarragon. Hot honey. I didn't have radicchio but used a comparable amount of chopped Belgian endive. After slicing the carrots very thin (at an angle) I par-cooked them in boiling water for about a minute and a half then plunged them into ice water. They were still crisp but the texture was a little more compatible with the whole creation. I agree with others who said but this is a flexible recipe. Great flavors and textures. I will make it again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Too much salt, but great, especially the next day.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this exactly as written. Flavor was pretty decent (you were correct that it improved over time) but the lentils were a soggy mess.  My daughter referred to it as "compost." I may try again with the TJ's precooked lentils.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this for a potluck recently and folks loved it and asked for the recipe. I will definitely pull this into rotation at home and for parties.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delightful. Mods I tried and liked: Added a 1/2 bunch of lacinato kale, shredded and massaged, with the radicchio and carrots. Also, I boosted the quantities on the vinaigrette, saved 2/3 cup for the salad, and used the remainder as a marinade for a flank steak, which I did sous vide and served sliced atop the salad. Big hit.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Great for weekday lunches. Used French green lentils, cooked until just starting to get tender. Added walnuts to make complete vege protein. Skipped the lentil drying out part & just patted dry in the colander then added to the bowl. I put all the lemon juice & rind in at once, & added thinly sliced shallots only to taste to avoid overpowering the salad. Salt to taste, not 1 tbsp. Only 1 tsp honey. Couldn't find radicchio so sub'd arugula (keep separate until ready to eat). Herbs are perfect.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This looks like a good recipe and similar to on I make without measuring anything but eyeballing it. I think one can simplify the shopping and preparing of some of the ingredients by substituting dry herbs:thyme, rosemary, dill and tarragon. It may be that expending the extra effort of going to the store and procuring the fresh herbs may produce a more flavorful dish, the savings in time and effort suggests it is not worth it. I also question how important it is to cool the lentils on a pan....

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made it without cooling and drying the lentils. Still was good. Also, used dry thyme and dill. Left out the tarragon. Still came out great.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This salad was delicious. The dressing was excellent. Next time I make it, however, I will not rinse the lentils with cold water. It made too much of a mess spreading them on kitchen towels, for one. Number two, if you double the recipe, like I did, you end up, needing a lot more space to spread out the lentils. I didn’t have that space so the lentils never really dried and as a result, the dressing was watered down. It was still incredible though and will make this again and take my chances  With not rinsing the lentils in cold water.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was tasty but took way longer to make than the recipe suggests. Not sure I’d make it again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this last night and it was a hit with both grown-ups and toddlers.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious! Variations: I did not rinse the lentils with cold water to keep more flavor (and saved the lentil-herb cooking water for a separate broth), substituted already-in-my-fridge red cabbage for hard-to-find radicchio, and omitted the parsley (since I had accidentally bought cilantro). Guests and family complimented and had seconds! I plan to make this dish again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Don't be aftaid to improvise and use what you have in your fridge. I didn't have carrots or any of the fresh herbs, but I did have apples and arugula. Very tasty result!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: To my taste, the recipe calls for too much dressing.  If I make this again, I would use half the dressing and save the rest of another salad.  The dressing itself is good, but masks the flavor of the other ingredients.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious, versatile, and I enjoyed the comments!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This is a great recipe and very adaptable to what you have on hand. 

One of the comments mentioned was to put the dressing on while lentils are warm and more is absorbed. This makes sense to me based on cooking similar recipes over the years. Is there a legitimate reason to rinse them? Or to let them cool? This seems a bit high maintenance and I doubt this is done in a French kitchen. 

People here also mentioning removing every piece of rosemary leaf. Why? It’s delicious and just adds flavor. 

Also,

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love recipes that leave room to improvise.  I didn't add lemon zest (used it in ravioli filling as I was meal prepping).  Didn't add dill as I don't like it.  I only had a little parsley as it hasn't taken off in my garden.  Added some crumbled feta and massaged kale the next day and it's great!  I wanted to add some fennel but didn't see any at the store.  Citrus would be good but it's July.  I'll add walnuts or chicken sausage tomorrow.  This recipe is a keeper!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I think that adding diced sweet potatoes would be even better and more nutritious than carrots!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I suspect that one or two people reading this recipe may not have access to "fresh" thyme or rosemary. Methods for using the versions of herbs and spices that are actually available to those   few unfortunates could, as in just might, be helpful in all these recipes. Recipes that require unobtanium do not get tried very often.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Maximus fresh rosemary and thyme are quite easy to grow in pots, so I suspect that actually a fair number of people do grow them. Regardless, there are plenty of us who want recipes that include herbs such as them.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I'm feeling radishes, celery, and tangy chèvre with this.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe is so adaptable to whatever veggies are in season, but I think carrots are a must - that crunch and the flavor of in-season carrots are just great. Also, a bouquet garni will let the rosemary sprig needles escape, which are too big to be anything but unpleasant. I suugest wrapping the herbs in cheesecloth and tying with string.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I learned a simple very delicious lentil/onion salad recipe from a person who was French.
Cook lentil gently with aromatics.  Drain lentils when al dente.  Put them in a well seasoned vinaigrette with sliced onions and/or shallots when still warm.  Add herbs like parsley, thyme and summer savory when cool.

author: Lisa Donovan
Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Absolutely superb, made as written. One of the best salads out there, and one you could vary seasonally.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I added Craisins to mine for a chewy tartness. Pretty good.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was delicious.  Don’t skip the bouquet garni.  The layers of herbal flavors put this recipe over the top.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: While this was delicious, it was one of the most unappealing dishes (from a visual standpoint) that I've ever made. I used Rancho Gordo French Style Green lentils and had high hopes for it, but it ended up a brown, muddy mess. I may try it again with yellow or other light colored lentils.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: What a disaster! After 18 min, my green Puy lentils were a total mush-fest! So much for holding their shape... I don't have time to make another batch, so am forging ahead with the remainder of the recipe and will serve French lentil dahl at my luncheon.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this. expected it to be great. it was very vinegary in taste (and I love vinegar). just wasn't great.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Gave it a 3. I found it rather bitter, probably the radicchio.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Add less vinegar

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Love this lentil salad. I have riffed on it each subsequent time I've made it- shredded carrots, arugula, dried cranberries, roasted eggplant, feta or goat cheese, sliced grape tomatoes. An adventure every iteration.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was so good (even though I overcooked the lentils)! I was skeptical about the honey, but the balance of the vinegar, honey, and lemon juice worked so well. I also added toasted walnuts -- grasping at any excuse to add toasted walnuts.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is wonderful but it makes a realllllly large amount.  It also takes a lot of prep to do all those different herbs and I’m not sure there was enough of any single one to distinguish it, especially with the flavor of the radicchio, so if you don’t have them all, don’t fret, just do the quantity of the sum with the ones you have.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Radicchio makes it very bitter, although it got better after a day of marinating. Did not really enjoy this and it did make a lot.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I followed the directions exactly as written except I used dried tarragon instead of fresh. I found it to be very bitter. I will not make it again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If it was the radicchio that made it bitter, I suggest slicing the radicchio and letting it sit in water for about 30 minutes. It takes the bitterness away.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is a very nice salad when served immediately after making it. But mine, at least, did not keep well. It quickly became watery, after just a few hours. I made this exactly as described, no alterations.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Very forgiving! I must have been sleepwalking through the grocery store because I forgot radicchio, Dijon mustard, and parsley, and then discovered I was out of sherry vinegar. I substituted chopped snap peas, dried mustard, and a 2:1 apple cider vinegar/balsamic vinegar mix and skipped the parsley. Sprinkled pomegranate seeds on top. Delicious! The orange carrot, green snap peas, and bright red pomegranate seeds made for a very colorful dish.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this with Trader Joe's lentils to save time, and it was delicious. The fresh herbs really make it. Especially loved the flavor of the tarragon and dill with the radicchio.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I read all these nice reviews, love and cook lentils often, was prepared to love this salad … but I found it to be just okay esp. for the time and effort

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: 1 tablespoon of salt ?  Is this regular iodized table salt ?  Morton’s kosher or Diamond Crystal kosher ?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I read they use Diamond  Kosher.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was just okay, took an incredible amount of time to make. Wish the lentils were salted, and the shallots diced. Lots of leftovers that aren’t necessarily better the next day.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Flavor explosion- made this exactly as written. Perfection!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Really good. I thought the addition of honey to the salad didn’t make sense, so I made it without. It seemed to lack something so ai added it. Perfection!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Start with 1tsp salt and adjust up from there if needed.  Agree with other commenters that 1TBSP seemed far too much and might be a typo.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Really tasty and great to also add to spinach or arugula…but the dressing was really salty!! Added more lentils to compensate. Next time I’d add maybe a tsp or less of salt, keep the pepper!  Also I used red onion as I didn’t have shallots and equally tasty!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Use the frozen green beans

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Love this recipe and in a pinch I’ve substituted dried tarragon, dill, and thyme along with precooked lentils from Trader Joe’s.   It turns out great!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Excellent.
 
Lesson 1 - While I used fresh dill and parsley, I used dried thyme, rosemary and tarragon.   The tarragon wasn't an issue, but the dried thyme and rosemary are sticks in the dressing.  So I put the dressing with the dried herbs in the Magic Bullet and that worked beautifully.  
Lesson 2 - Next time I make this I'm going to leave the dressing in the Bullet and whiz the parsley and dill into it too before combining the dressing with the carrots and shallots to save time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Feta and tinned fish are great additions.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Do you really need to dry the lentils on a towel? Hoping to skip that step.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Reader if not a towel, you may at least want to allow for extra “air-drying” in the colander…i do think extra moisture will alter the efficacy of the vinaigrette, and possibly even cause the lentils to lose their firmness  after a day or so.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I use Trader Joe’s cooked lentils and add balsamic vinegar* and crumbled feta cheese — for the sweet/salty zing — to evoo, diced cucumber, jicama, fresh corn, halved cherry tomatoes and a soupçon of lemon juice. Serve over greens misted with seasoned rice vinegar. For effect at a potluck add nosturtium blossoms on top. 
*White balsamic works, too, and keeps the ingredients colorful.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was so so so good.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I roasted the carrots and roasted some sweet potato as well.l which added a nice autumn/winter feel. I also served some grilled sweet Italian chicken sausage alongside this dish (or mixed in) for added protein. If you planning on eating for a few days, I recommend preparing and keeping the dressing separate.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is an excellent salad with lots of flavour and the possibility of infinite variation. I’ve been eating it this week and adding different elements each time - chopped cherry tomatoes and cucumbers; feta cheese, mixed seeds and walnuts. I could also see adding a cooked grain and maybe roasted or steamed veggies. Thank you for a great base recipe!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this exactly as written with the addition of pomegranate seeds for a pop of color. It’s a bit time-consuming, but the result is worth it. Delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: And I used black beluga lentils, because I couldn't get French green lentils.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I trader Joe-fied this and really love it. I so look forward to lunch the day after I make this for dinner! My swaps: 

- Used TJ's cooked lentils from the fridge section
- Replaced the radicchio with a bag of TJ's shaved Brussels sprouts (just a preference)
- In the dressing, Instead of 1 tbs salt, used 1 tsp (it's way too salty with 1 tbs, maybe on account of the pre cooked lentils)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In France green lentils are Le Puy type of lentils. Here it seems to be what in France is called lentils blonde. Which one is it in the original recipe?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Arne Jonsson the recipe calls for green lentils and in parentheses she says French which as you noted are two different lentils. Le Puy are smaller and darker green in color. I have access to every type and color of lentil you could imagine. A real treat for sure!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The intro mentions chicory, but I see no listing in the ingredients! Am I missing something?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Susanna yes.  Radicchio is a chicory. So is endive and a whole bunch of other things, including what we refer to as chicory.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It's great. I loved the raw radicchio and just the plentiful vegetables in it in general. I shaved the carrots into ribbons since I like that texture. It's worth getting most or all the herbs for this (there are plenty of other things I can use what didn't go into this throughout the week). I'm not sure what the fretting about the salt recommendation is all about but you can just salt to taste.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! It's also great with black lentils. I added feta and toasted walnuts. And served leftovers on the bed of arugula and tomato salad. Seriously, the kind of deliciousness you want to eat every day. And I think I will!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Whoot!  Finally was able to feed the 17-yr-old vegetarian lentils!
Quote: "That was good.  Keep this recipe"
AND he chose the leftovers for lunch!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A TABLESPOON of salt? What is the sodium content per serving, please?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Dee
Way too much sodium and not necessary.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: good but highly acidic (imo, even without the addition of more lemon juice) and needed some nuts to add flavor, texture and nutrition to this salad

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I wonder if it would have been more helpful for them to given a volume of lemon juice, since citrus sizes vary. I used a Meyer lemon and didn't have acidity issues at all. Agree about the nuts! Added a small bag of slivered almonds I had and liked the crunch (and extra protein/healthy fat).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This dish has now become a regular in my household. I love that it is easy to make, can be done in advance and lasts a couple days, specially for those busy weeks where we don’t have much time to cook and look for something healthy to eat in the evenings. Goes very well with hard boiled eggs on the side. Sometimes we also eat it with grilled sausages on the side as well

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Insanely delicious! I used regular red cabbage instead of radicchio…had it on hand and easier to find in “any” grocery store. Also cheated with the Trader Joe’s lentils.  It could use more than one package of those. Maybe 1-1/2? There’s plenty dressing. With leftovers I’ve been adding goat cheese and/or orange wedges.  So good for such a simple dish!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Amazing. I’ve never used fresh tarragon before—it is superb! What a delightful salad. This was main course for dinner tonight and satisfied completely. I can’t wait to try with some blood oranges cut in.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: So, we made it and it was a rousing success, the tarragon is the magic touch. Just amazing!!!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: What a nice dish. Easy to make with minimal prep time. Economical, especially if you grow your own herbs. I used some of my homemade red wine and garlic mustard. Tastes even better the next day.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Had high hopes for this salad because I love lentils but found it to be a lot of work for an average tasting salad.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made the dressing as directed but with 2 teaspoons of Diamond Crystal Kosher salt (instead of 1 Tbsp.) and half a lemon juiced plus the red wine vinegar and found the dressing very acidic and strong. I feel maybe it was too much lemon juice. It is very tangy.  I used 2 cans of Trader Joe's lentils and they were perfect. Next time I will make a milder vinaigrette.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I don’t like the bitter taste of radicchio, so I subbed in red cabbage instead. It was delicious, crunchy, and beautiful! I enjoyed this dish for days

author: Lisa Donovan
