commentBody: Delicious but so fussy and time consuming to prepare

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I used 2 cans of Trader Joe's canned lentils (drained and rinsed), just parsley and basil (because that's what I had), added crumbled feta cheese for a salty bite, and thinly sliced endive instead of radicchio (again because I had some in the fridge already and which I think are pretty similar). Served it at room temperature as a side dish for roasted salmon. Easy and tasty.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is a great salad. I wouldn't change a thing.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We made this last night and it was delicious!  This is a great way to use the herbs we grow.  I make soup weekly during the fall and winter, so this will be a great summer substitute.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Too much salt and pepper (I reluctantly followed the recipe). Wish I had consulted the comments first. Otherwise delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I just made this and served it with some grilled arctic char.  Four stars.  I tasted this along the way.  First I thought too much mustard, but the lemon zest balances things out.  I like tarragon, and, over time, the tarragon blends with the other flavors.  I don’t think the radicchio is necessary.  That is your call.  As one reviewer noted, I think this prep would benefit from the addition of lardons or bacon.  Again, your call.  I may make this again.  I’d like to try the basic idea with beans rather than lentils, just to trey something different.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: How many servings does this make?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @RonC The recipe says it makes 9 cups of salad using 1 1/2 cups of dried lentils. I generally figure 1/4 cup of most dried beans and grains for a serving.  If you use it as a main course, it would be about 5-6 servings. It’s also great served with roast chicken or fish or fried halloumi, in which case it might stretch further. Since the proportions can be flexible, I’ve also made it with just 1 cup of dried lentils when that’s all I had, and it served 2 main courses with enough left over for side dishes for 4 more. It’s good hot, warm or cold, so I enjoy the leftovers.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @RonC It seems to make 4 servings if you want 20g of protein. That’s a decent amount of bulk though, about 2 cups.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Superb and health maintaining

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: What’s your source for the imported ones?  I’m generally happy with what I can buy here but would love the real thing!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: SECOND COMMENT. After three days in the refrigerator, the salad is still good.  Nothing is mushy.  The seasoning, if anything, is more interesting.  Please see my prior comment for a full review.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Probably the best lentil salad I've made in a long time.  Prepared as noted, could not be happier with the results.  AND, yes, the flavor does improve with age if you don't consume in one sitting.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: One more comment - I second the earlier commenter who recommended Trader Joe's pre-cooked lentils! Really wonderful if you're short on time or energy!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Is it possible to substitute for some of the fresh herbs? I haven't seen fresh bay leaves ever! I am growing thyme but it isn't large enough for the required amount.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I'm not a fan of recipes that cook legumes and then throw away the cooking water. So much nutrition and flavor is being tossed in favor of texture. For that reason, I just choose to make something else.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Former Chef 25 years

Two questions:

Do you think we could just use less water so we don’t have anything to drain?

Also, how do you use the leftover water from cooking things like this? What are some examples?

Thanks!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Beth

Soup is all I can think of!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
So so so good!

Made as is with a few exceptions….

- used chicken bone broth to cook the lentils and also seasoned them in the water with the herbs
- added them to the dressing warm so they would take on the flavor so much more
- added roasted Japanese sweet potatoes into the whole dish. 

It’s going into the monthly rotation for sure!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Does anyone have any good substitution ideas for radicchio?  I’ve never been a fan…

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Surfandturf. Have not figured out yet?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Surfandturf Radish and/ or arugula. I can’t easily obtain radicchio or endive.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: FWIW, Treviso radicchio is a bit milder, so that might (or might not) be of interest.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: How could two shallots amount to 170 grams? I took 3 and they weigh 80 g… (everything is bigger in the us etc., but something must be wrong here).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Flavor explosion, so good. I'm adding feta cheese to mine and maybe less shallot next time. Yumbo.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made as indicated. Served alongside  roast pork loin w/ fennel. Excellent. Will make again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
"Line a sheet pan with a towel ... "
What kind of towel? Dish? Paper?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've always loved eating warm beans, and now lentils, with crisp, cool salads. The weather was cool here, so that's how I ate this - it was very good. The next day, it was exceptional!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made it for second time, today with leek, radish, carrot, arugula and lemon thyme, basil and parsley. If you dress while lentils are warm, you need to reserve some dressing for later. I am thinking about a gently roasted red pepper or a slender grilled eggplant for the next time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Even my rather…umm…particular spouse liked this, though we skipped the tarragon as neither of us cares for it. I would also use a little less salt in the future.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious! I will be making this in repeat! I followed the recipe precisely with the one exception of not including tarragon, my loss, I’m sure!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: 1 tablespoon of salt in the dressing seems like waaaaay too much.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I doubled the recipe, including the dressing, and save half, using only 1T of salt. Deliciously salty but not overly. 
Also add half the dressing to half the lentils and freeze without any vegetables or herbs. I added them, along with the herbs, zest, and olive oil, to the second batch of (defrosted) lentils before serving. Makes for a quicker prep the second time around.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It seems like the weight amount of the herbs is off, too much. Am I right?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Looks like a great recipe. Most of the ingredients are on hand except for the thyme, rosemary, bay leaves, dill and tarragon. Shame that once I buy and use these ingredients the 90% that I don’t use for this recipe will end up in the trash.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Stone Fish Maybe you could find a way to use those things instead of wasting food.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Freeze what you don't use, so that you have it available for another recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Stone Fish they can be frozen

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: What is the point of the bouquet garni if you rinse the lentils and toss the bouquet after cooking? It's lost of me.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Ouidaart the lentils absorb the flavor through the cooking liquid, kind of like risotto. I found it very pronounced.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hearty and delicious on its own or as a side (we enjoyed it with steelhead trout). I didn't have all the herbs specified and didn't miss them: Parsley, dill and a few thyme sprigs do nicely. Instead of radicchio, I used cooked French beans left over from another meal. They worked beautifully. The dressing is so good, I made extra for other purposes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I need to try this again.   My lentils were way too mushy and that ruined the dish. I'm going to use canned lentils next time, as per some of the comments.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: FYI, store-bought French lentils took over an hour to cook.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: lovely

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This dressing is an awesome base for a French-inspired lentil salad. I didn’t have radicchio or shallots but I did have beet greens, fennel and red onion. The beet greens needed to be cooked, so I sautéed them in a little olive oil for 10 minutes. After they were cooled, I added them and the other vegetables (the red onion in place of the shallots) and the fennel and beet greens instead of the radicchio. Delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I just made this (substituting madly) and it's delish. ... as for the recipe's intro paragraphs: appealing enough but feels like AI...

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious. I subbed spinach and arugula for the radicchio, as I don't care for the bitterness. Added an extra shallot, dried cranberries, feta cheese, halved grape tomatoes. It was a big hit, so I will be making it for a family bbq this weekend.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The carrots—no matter how finely sliced—and the radicchio made this salad annoyingly chewy. Will try again with red cabbage and bell peppers.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve made this twice now - it’s perfect for meal prepping for the week and keeps its crispness beautifully. I couldn’t find radicchio and made this with red cabbage and I have no complaints. A bit too light of a lunch for me, but is perfect with some baguette or crackers on the side for some carbs. Love that you can make it in one bowl as well. This recipe inspired me to become efficient with my kitchen knives. :)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was outstanding!  When I make it again I’ll reduce vinegar by 1TBSP, can add later if needed.  I’d also add an extra TBSP of fresh dill.  Maybe throw the parsley, tarragon, dill, in a food processor to reduce prep time, can also slice the carrots with a slicing disk to speed that up too.  I sautéed 1/2 the shallots in some of the olive oil to tame them a bit.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Just an aside-my bouquet garni did lose many rosemary and thyme leaves into the lentils. If this will bother you, wrap your bouquet in muslin. I didn't want the extra rosemary leaves because I was worried they'd impart too much rosemary flavor, so picked them out.
This salad was a huge hit!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. I like a little chew to my lentils so cooking for 15 minutes was a bit too long for mine. Also the garlic had a bit too much bite, possibly my cloves were too large so will adjust next time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Fabulous.  Loved the fresh herbs, very adaptable

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Super easy and packs a lot of flavor.  It's a keeper!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made it exactly according to the recipe. Two things: 1) The carrots are not added early enough to allow them to cook completely. 2) The stew drys out - Either the 2 cups of stock is not enough, or the pot should be covered instead of partially covered.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It is a salad, not a stew.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I liked the dressing. I didn’t have dill or tarragon and it was still a nice balance. It was easy, flavorful and healthy. I think adding goat cheese would have been good. My only critique is that 2 shallots is way too much for me. It sounded like too much but I followed the recipe anyway and it was too much. Next time I would only add half a shallot rather than 2.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
No coconut water at end
1.5 cups lentils
Curry spice mix

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Awesome recipe, used 2 of Wegman’s microwaveable packs of lentils. Super easy and delicious. Fresh herbs a must.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious and versatile...I can see this being prepared with so many variations!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is sooooo good.  Highly recommend!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I'm obsessed, even with rushing! I put rosemary and thyme directly in the pot with some Rancho Gordo green lentils...used an almost empty dijon jar and eyeballed some agave in place of honey and shook it up...this allowed for a bit of extra vinaigrette to be poured over once served. Did not have fresh tarragon.
Next time, will follow to the letter for the sake of comparison, but once I tasted the vinaigrette, I knew it was going to be good... I ate it for dinner AND lunch--wish I'd made more!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Great fiber-rich recipe! Very tasty!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I really enjoyed this as a recipe to make ahead and bring to lunch through the week, mixing up add ins as the days went on to keep it interesting but always adding a drizzle of olive oil and a sprinkle of salt. Some of the add ins I really enjoyed with this were chopped almonds, avocado, roasted beet and Japanese sweet potato, goat cheese, mandarin segments. I also used red cabbage instead of radicchio. Love love love I will keep this one in heavy rotation.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: 2 1/2 hours including clean-up. When I get my own test kitchen and minions, it'll probably take 1 hour and 10 minutes as stated. I added another half recipe of dressing.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is one of the best salads and dishes I’ve ever made. It’s off the chain!  Instant entry on my greatest hits list.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I thought it was barely edible and did not improve with age.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Good recipe, I didn't have tarragon but used fr. basil. This is a good big batch salad. The radicchio adds a good element (worth it).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Can it be made with red lentils?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Shane Brown I wouldn’t. Red lentils break down so quickly you’d likely end up with mush.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Simple, delicious, nourishing.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Scaling recipes is a great feature, but this recipe doesn’t print at 1/2 scale

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The recipe calls for "shallots", Does it mean green onions (the long ones with white bottoms and green tops, like miniature leeks) or "échalottes" (the French bulbs that look like a large pink  garlic clove)?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Phab. Long skinny green onions are SCALLIONS.   Shallots are shallots as you describe them.  I have never seen them sold in the US with the French name.  any ordinary supermarket has them as Shallots.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Question: I have been only been able to find green lentils that carry a warning for “traces of wheat/gluten”. Will it be a disaster to use brown lentils, which carry no such warning? Suggestions? Thanks.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The longer I've cooked, the more boldly I substitute. I definitely wouldn't use red lentils, but brown ones would be fine, as long as they don't melt into each other. For what it's worth, green lentils might be different from French green ("de Puy") lentils. I couldn't tell if you were using shorthand or possibly seeing a different type of lentil. French green ones hold their own more when cooked — probably one reason she uses them here. I think beluga/black lentils do, too.

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Recommendations on a substitute for the mustard? Spouse is allergic

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Mustard tends to add tang, so I'd probably increase the vinegar and/or lemon juice. For some reason, I'm wondering about adding some nutritional yeast, too. In Indian cooking (which also uses a lot of mustard), I've seen chefs like Suvir Saran talk about amchur (mango powder?) and/or lemon juice. Tamarind adds another distinct and sourish note. Pomegranate molasses might be another interesting thing to try, as it has a bit more body. Hope one of these ideas helps!

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My lentils needed a 2 quart pot (not the 1 quart I used) and much more than 20 minutes, but came out very flavorful. Added kale + some mini bell peppers and left out the dill (forgot) and parsley, tarragon and radicchio I didn't have. Still delicious. Will definitely add this to the rotation, as I like non-wilty salads I can make in bulk. I'm not sure about using blood orange AND hard boiled egg, per @madame_P's popular comment, but two eggs for a hearty bowl of the salad was very good.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: oh my gosh! made this in CA and I was appalled by how bitter it was. thanks for the explanation and substitute recommendation! @MFK

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I doubled the recipe for a potluck, and kept half for my lunches this week. My local Piggly Wiggly didn’t have radicchio, so I subbed a chopped, boiled potato. Delicious and endlessly toffabke! Oh and one day I added some fried halloumi. It’s

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this a few days ago; stuck to the recipe except for substituting Belgian endive for radicchio due to availability. It was even better the second day. I can see us making this a staple, varying which vegetables included with the seasons.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I drain the lintels, spread them directly on the sheet pan, without a towel, and add the vinaigrette while hot. This allows the lintels to absorb the vinaigrette while cooling.

Serve with a nice green salad.

Chevre is a great accompaniment. Feta would do well too.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious. Easy. I made with canned lentils and don’t regret it. Lentils were not mushy as some have complained. I made it the night before, so the flavors were really pronounced and wonderful.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love this recipe and have made it several times as written.  For a change this time around, I made some farro and, after cooling it, mixed it in with the lentils. I liked the result even better! It wasn't a 50-50 mix -- the lentils are still the star -- but the addition of a grain cuts a little bit of the acidity and makes an already healthy meal even healthier.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @ALKA. ALSO. The tablespoon of salt isn’t listed explicitly in the ingredients, it’s just in the text. Normally that amount (as opposed to “to taste”) would be a listed ingredient not just pop up in the directions. So I’m going with the assumption that it’s a typo and should be a teaspoon and you can add more if you have to. Tablespoon is wild.

commentBody: 17 ingredients for a salad that includes three fresh herbs? I think not.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Drop the rosemary, substitute Brussels sprouts & apples for the radicchio, and it’s heaven.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve now made this twice in the last month, modifying slightly each time and each time it was wonderful.  I used green French lentils, adding some pearl couscous the first time bc it was in the fridge and needed to be used.  My carrots were less than ideal so I roasted them with the shallots along with some fennel that also needed to be used.  We loved it with the roasted veggies and will likely keep making it that way.  As for the herbs, I only used the bay leaves to make the lentils and put a few pinches of dried thyme in the dressing.  Added copious amounts of dill and parsley chopped fine.  Salt and pepper to taste; dried mustard powder bc my mustard was too old.  oh and the second time I didn’t have radicchio so subbed arugula.  Was a wonderful light dinner in this brutal heat wave.  I get that I changed things around; however as with many NYT Cooking recipes it was easy enough to do that with a good road map. And for me reading the notes is key—folks out there are excellent cooks and have helped my cooking over the years.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made exactly as the recipe instructs, with all ingredients and herbs fresh from the market that day.  Found the salad bland to the point of having trouble eating it. I would not make it again without changes. We added some feta on top, which helped. I also suggested adding capers to provide a bit of energy, and my guest agreed that would probably improve it.  Since I have oodles of leftovers now, I can test the capers, and/or maybe a bit of anchovy, as one reader suggested, and/or more lemon or vinegar.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This version of the cake is unfamiliar to this Southerner, but it's an excellent recipe.  The Swiss buttercream is far better than the usually over-unctuous American buttercream.  I still prefer a lighter frosting for coconut cake.  For those hesitant to use the sugar, choose another cake.  Coconut cake is traditionally very sweet, and it fades if it's not supported adequately by sugar.  That said, the 2 cups of sugar can be reduced by about 1/3 cup without doing real damage.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Now if you really want to celebrate and gild the lily you could take all those leftover egg yolks and whip up some lemon curd to dollop on the side.🍋

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Have a cardiologist on standby.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My southern mother made a coconut cake every Christmas. I remember my dad grating fresh coconut for what seemed like forever. (I don’t think there was ever a cake free from his dna) Mother iced with old fashioned boiled icing, which required the cake to need stabilization with small dowels. Her family recipe has been passed down, and continues to be made for Christmas dinner. Not always beautiful, but always delicious. (And thanks to modern day shredding tools, no dna!)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @KTS yes, my mother also used boiled icing. And the middle layer was orange filling, not frosting. This was my father’s annual birthday cake

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Melanie please share more info! I've never heard of orange filling, is it like lemon curd?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Katy Lou. I only make boiled icing. It is fabulous!!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Came here to roll my eyes at the inevitable cardiology comments.  Oh the sugar, oh the butter!! It’s a cake ya’ll. Those of you who bake or look at recipes know it’s pretty typical to have this amount. I bet you can get way more than  12 servings out of this too. Enjoy!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I know! Don't make a cake if you don't want to eat cake! There are no surprises with cake. It's...cake. It's meant to be all that it is. And I wish commenters wouldn't try to guilt bakers into not baking a cake and cake-lovers into feeling guilty about eating the most delicious dessert creation there is. (I am a cake person.) You don't want it, don't eat it, and can I have yours?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I just this morning happened to make a coconut cake for the first time, from the Women's Home Companion Cook Book, dated 1944 -- my go-to for cakes.  A much simpler recipe than the one herein and quite delicious.  But the cakes were flat.  I see in the photo accompanying this recipe that the cakes are also fairly compressed (despite the egg white), and I am going to chalk that up to the moisture in the coconut in the batter.  It's good to have this coincidental reference.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe is A  I made it immediately after seeing it NYT Magazine. It’s a labor of love and worth every bite. Don’t be fooled by the amount of sugar the cake is sweet but not overly sweet. All of it is perfect will definitely be making it again. Also I didn’t have coconut extract so I left it out and it was still delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Tini thanks for sharing about the coconut extract. I’m trying to use up a couple of bags of shredded coconut and didn’t want to have to buy a specialty ingredient for this cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’m sure this is delicious but i just can’t bring myself to make it using 2 cups of sugar. Wondering how using less sugar would affect the cake’s texture?

commentBody: It ends up with three and half cups of sugar and a pound and a half of butter, along with the sugar in 5 and 1/2 cups of sweetened coconut for 10-12 slices. That's going overboard.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Kathie It’s a great cake! Just cut the slices smaller.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In Atlanta, a dinner featuring the multi-layered coconut cake from Rich's department store bakery was about as fancy as you could get without being at an actual wedding.  Long after the store was bought by another chain, the Atlanta newspaper ran the Rich's recipe - but there was an error (gasp!).  The following week, the paper printed the corrected recipe.  It was a big deal, and I still have both those clippings in my recipe box.  Someday I might be ambitious enough to give it a try.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A Southern chef who made a delicious coconut cake shared with me her (super easy!) frosting recipe. She mixed a half a cup of sour cream with a half a cup of heavy cream, whipped. Then she whipped in confectioners sugar to taste, spread it and sprinkled it with sweetened coconut, and voila! Five minute frosting.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I would pay someone to make this for me! I had the best slice of coconut cake from Erehwon last week; I might drive back to L.A. for another.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I have long sought a coconut cake recipe that would approach that served me in the 1950s/60s by my Virginia grandmother (born 1893). The mystery for me, as I've searched many recipes, is how she used  the coconut water in the fresh coconut she would always crack open in my presence. I remember the cake being so moist, it was almost dripping. Any thoughts what she was up to?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The best coconut cake I've ever had was from Paul Prudhomme's restaurant.  There is a recipe for it in one of the cookbooks and it does use fresh - fresh from the coconut - water.  Sounds like a lot of work, but believe it does make a difference.  Just a thought if you're inclined.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: With the whisk attachment, whip the buttercream on medium/high speed until it is white, fluffy, and doesn't taste buttery, this can take 8-10 minutes. To counteract the yellow in your buttercream, add a drop or two of violet food color gel to make it whiter. Then switch to a paddle attachment and mix it on low speed for 15-20 minutes to make the buttercream very smooth and remove air bubbles. This isn't required, but if you want really creamy frosting, you do not want to skip it!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is an excellent version of coconut cake, though it's unfamiliar to this Southerner.  The Swiss buttercream works far better than the heavy and overly-unctuous American buttercream.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I always reduce the amount of sugar in dessert recipes because that is my taste. I usually cut by as much as 25% and my dinner guests beg for seconds. If you want something less sweet, make it less sweet. That goes for southern cakes too.

Happy baking

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Nancy I absolutely agree, but readers, please be aware that reducing sugar in baking recipes can sometimes affect the texture of what you’re making, not just the taste

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This cake came out great! The coconut flavor is there but not too strong (you will not feel like you’re eating tanning lotion!). And I liked the very slightly boozy flavor of the frosting. The cake is all butter and so is a bit dense—you’ll want a small piece. If you don’t know what to do with all the leftover egg yolks, you can instead use pasteurized egg whites from a carton. Despite what you may have heard, they will whip into meringue—just add some cream of tartar to help stabilize them. I just did it to make this cake and frosting yesterday, and it worked fine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Just want to say that I saw other comments suggesting you can’t use pasteurized egg whites to make meringue. That isn’t true, and my proof is I just did it two days ago! Just add some cream of tartar, and you’ll be fine. One caveat: you may need the power of a stand mixer. I wouldn’t try it by hand or with a handheld mixer.

I have made vegan buttercream frosting with earth balance sticks with much success! Just not great on a warm or hot day as the melting point is lower than regular butter.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you don't want to re-mortgage your house for all the eggs used in this recipe (or go through the hassle of making a swiss buttercream), I highly recommend using a cooked flour ermine icing in place of the swiss buttercream. It's the only icing I make anymore. Light, airy, not too sweet, yet still buttery tasting.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Ermine icing is my fave as well! Every time I make it, people go crazy for it! So mucg better than a standard buttercream and easier  - and cheaper - by far than Swiss Meringue Buttercream.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I haven’t had a homemade coconut cake since I moved north. Scrumptious. If you love coconut cake, look for a recipe for its close relative—the Hummingbird cake. And for those of you who are concerned that there is too much sugar or butter—move on to another cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I made this cake for a birthday/Easter celebration. It turned out looking good and more importantly tasting great.  The textures of cake and frosting were balanced and light. Turns out the effort was worth the results. Definitely a special occasion cake
It was a lot of work and I had my doubts of the outcome while baking. It is a special occasion cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is my first NYT Cooking note! I have been using this for years, and I sometimes enjoy the comments more than the recipes. I made this for Easter. The 11 leftover yolks felt like a decadent discard in the age of Avian flu. The cake is delicious, tender, and, yes, sweet. One of my layers was a bit underdone due to eyeballing the division of batter in each pan. The point is this: three layers is a lot of cake, particularly when the cake is this rich. My two-layered cake is better!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
My favorite cake recipe makes three layers. I always frost two and freeze the third, because we're too small a family to eat a three-layer cake in any reasonable time frame. I also make extra frosting and freeze that too. Then I have a quick fancy-ish dessert without baking a whole other cake. I would definitely do that here. This cake looks really big.

Maybe not for a fancy dinner party, though. Then, the three layers should get the wows they deserve.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is a great recipe and people will rave. If you know how to make Swiss Meringue Buttercream beware! This recipe has a boost of powdered sugar which affects the normal swiss temps of 160 F stove top and 70 F before adding butter.  I slapped a couple of bags of frozen peas alongside the bowl to cool before adding the room temp butter.  It happens quickly so don’t stop before the miracle happens.  Be sure a charge ahead by following the steps through. your cake will be superb!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Our Mississippi family has always preferred boiled icing or seven minute frosting. Purists insist using on fresh coconut, typically when labor is not an issue and somebody else is doing the heavy lifting.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe makes a DELICIOUS cake. I followed the instructions to the letter, EXCEPT I slowed the mixer to the lowest setting when adding the powdered sugar to the frosting (so it didn’t dust up all over the kitchen). I then mixed about 1 cup coconut into the frosting. The frosting was still quite runny, so following the Tip, I put the frosting back to the mixer on a higher speed, about 7. It firmed up nicely within seconds. During final assembly, I found that I didn’t really need another 3 cups of coconut for the finishing touches. I toasted about 1 cup coconut for sprinkling on the top. It was yummy and a big hit with my guests.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Delicious! I made it into a bunny for Easter Sunday a and proceeded with a few tweaks:
- 1/2 cup less sugar on the cake and on icing - in my opinion I could decrease sugar a bit more
- unsweetened shredded coconut 
- 1 1/2 cups of butter in the icing ( as opposed to 2)
- had to skip coconut extract Bcz I could not find it at the store 

Huge success! 

Huge

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Totally delicious and a total hit at the dinner party. It's very rich, very decadent. I've been a little wary of coconut extract as it usually makes everything taste like sunscreen, but this is a small amount and it worked well.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I’m not sure what I did wrong but when I made the frosting the egg whites   sugar never formed stiff peaks like you usually get with a merengue. I’ve never whipped egg whites with sugar before, but I did use the mixer for almost 10 minutes - longer than the recipe suggests. I also had no problem with the egg whites that went into the cake.

What did I do wrong? 

I ended up moving forward with the rest of the steps without getting peaks in my egg whites and ended up with runny frosting.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made a gluten free version. It took about 4 hours plus the time to frost it the next day. I could not find gluten-free cake flour so I followed a substitution that I found online where you replace two Tbsp of flour per cup with 2 Tbsp of cornstarch. I also replaced 3/4 cup of flour with 3/4 cup of coconut flour. I followed the King Arthur suggestion of letting the batter sit for 20 minutes before baking (for GF batter). I did that right after I mixed the liquid and dry ingredients with the butter. I left out the leavening at that point. After the 20 minutes, I added in the baking soda and baking powder and then folded in the egg whites. I had to bake it just over 30 minutes. It only rose to the the top of the pan. It was also gummy to touch. Since the icing was runny, I put everything in the refrigerator overnight and that gave the cake a chance to dry out a bit. However, the icing broke and I could not restore it. The texture sort of looked like shredded coconut so I just went with it. It would not stick to the sides of the cake, so the cake had naked sides. This turned out to be one of best tasting cakes I have ever made in my life! Everybody absolutely loved it and nobody knew any better.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made the cake as directed. I did use three round cake pans, but I probably could have used just the two as the layers were quite thin. Nevertheless, it looked beautiful when finished and tasted great. I did not think it was too sweet, even though there is a lot of sugar and it is loaded with butter and eggs. Nevertheless, it’s not like you’re eating it every day and a small piece will satisfy.

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Lovely! I chose this recipe because we had a lot of egg whites leftover after using the yolks in prune ice cream. Bring the egg whites to room temperature since the peaks form from protein (unlike cream, where cold fat forms the structure). The richness of this cake makes it easy to serve a crowd — easily 16 pieces. If you’re concerned about too much butter or sugar, choose something else!

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I am on a lifelong quest to make a coconut cake as good as my Nana's and this one came the closest although I used a cream cheese frosting instead. I also basted each layer with coconut milk before frosting.

I usually find the back and forth horror at the amount of butter and sugar in a dessert and the eye rolling of DUH it's a dessert funny. But I made the mistake of looking at the nutritional info for this cake and it is ONE THOUSAND CALORIES PER SLICE. Anyway, it's obviously incredible.
