author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Interesting complaints about the sugar - I was disappointed with the results of this recipe in that the cake was not sweet enough!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: While it's beyond my skill to make this cake, it looks amazing! Kudos to any cook who can!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Can you use unsweetened coconut or does it affect the cake?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Reminds me of Lane Cake that my mother used to make, except Lane Cake was more elaborate and was flavored with liquor.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I followed this recipe to a T…. Worst cake I’ve ever made. I’m not sure where I messed. Batter barely rosed during baking. No chance of getting egg white peaks in 3-4 minutes with my standing mixer.  An hour of beating the egg white & sugar mix and still couldn’t get firm peaks. I finished the recipe. It is edible and especially since the total price of all ingredients was high, I’ll finish it. If I can get good advice, I may be tempted to try this recipe again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
ouch! As far as your eggs go...You have a few options. Cream of tartar, salt, or sugar...just a teensy bit! It could be do TEMPERATURE (room temp only!), contamination of something greasy (don't use plastic bowls!).

I am just about to pop my cakes in the oven. Fingers crossed!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Tom Hambury Review...............Now THAT made me laugh out loud.  Thanks for the humor

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hurrah! It’s so good to see a real coconut cake recipe rather than those ‘coconut’ cakes with coconut- dressed tops and sides alone (sometimes with coconut essence added to the cake batter). My family created a recipe strikingly similar to this in frustration.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Quite a recipe: thorough and clearly written and the resulting product is as impressive as it is delicious.  I kept reaching for more butter and egg whites as the baking proceeded...at least three cups of butter and nearly one dozen eggs. I think a stand mixer is almost a necessity. Kudos to the author and pastry chef.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this for Easter and it was fantastic! I may have left my butter get too soft as my frosting was very soft, but I popped it in the fridge and it helped. The cake is so moist and tender! I didn’t have any issues with mine not rising, so not sure what the other commenter was struggling with. It’s coconut cake. It’s going to be sweet. It’s worth making as is :)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This was delicious! I only had unsweetened coconut, do I used that both in cake and mixed into the frosting. As others said, it was already pretty sweet. But for the portion applied to the outside, I toasted half, then tossed it with about 1/4 cup confectioners sugar before pressing onto the cake- before that, it was a bit bland.

The cake was HUGE though, could have served 12-16! Next time, I’ll reduce the volume and make just two 8” layers.
In the meantime, I’m wondering if I can freeze a portion of the cake, possibly pre sliced. Does Swiss butter freeze well?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Hockeynut I agree that this cake is huge! Most people just want a small slice, so I think it could be as many as 18 servings. Definitely possible to freeze individual layers for the future if you’re just serving a small group.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Hockeynut …but I don’t know about freezing the frosting. Sorry :(

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This cake was a big hit among my coworkers. I understand the hesitation making a cake with so many eggs and so much butter especially with how expensive groceries are lately, but this is definitely a special occasion cake that people really appreciate. Next time I make it I want to add slightly less baking soda, spread coconut into the layers of frosting and toast the coconut flakes before I press them onto the top and sides of the cake. Happy baking!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Great flavour, the cake is rather stodgy though but it's to be expected with the butter/sugar base, I reckon oil would have made a lighter crumb.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Could you make this with unsweetened coconut?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Well the icing was a fiasco.  I followed the instructions faithfully and it look great until I incorporated all the butter, and other ingredients. Then it was just a runny drizzle consistency.  The recipe says “don’t over beat” but I couldn’t figure out how to thicken it up even after I added a lot more powdered sugar.  Threw it out.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We made it....and loved it. Yes, I too clutched my pearls at the butter, sugar, all the things. If you don't love all the butter and sugar, you could try to adjust or reduce, or just skip this one. For those that want to (and are able to) live dangerously, this cake is worth it. The crumb is moist and and the frosting is fluffy and full of coconut flavor, not heavy but rich. It came together very well, fairly straightforward to make. We had smallish slices and devoured every crumb, and will make again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this exactly as written. It was excellent - guests raved. So I made it again for another party the next week. This is now my favorite cake…

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A lot of work, a lot of bowls. Not worth it.  I cannot imagine a new baker even attempting this.  It was hard for me and definitely not worth it.  I’ll keep looking for another recipe

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This cake is a star, but it is indeed a lot of work. My husband and I tackled it together and even shredded fresh coconuts for the recipe. We served it at our monthly supper club gathering. It was a huge hit. I do think that the icing could be subbed with classic Ermine Frosting (add a little coconut extract). That’s my plan for next time. Gotta get my strength back though.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Is there a way to save this if the frosting breaks after adding butter?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
far too sweet for  my liking. not sure why the egg whites never got to stiff peak level even after 1/2 hour of whipping.  because the price of eggs are so high I went with the egg-whites-in-a-box. maybe that's why???
but, for me, far too much effort for far too sweet.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I put egg whites in the freezer for a few mins before whipping into stiff peaks…that way they always firm up during whipping.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Does this use run of the mill buttermilk or the thicker cultured butter milk?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Aaron all buttermilk is cultured.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Excellent recipe.  Great cake with superior frosting.  It was a big hit at a recent get-together.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There are a lot of walk down memory lane comments in here that have nothing to do with this recipe, which isn't helpful for a girl baking a coconut cake for the first time for her mom's 71st birthday. So here are my tips for THIS recipe: the sponge was incredible! I did cut the sugar by half a cup. Definitely use coconut extract, it's worth that extra flavor saturation in the cake part. I made it in two layers, not three. And I did take a helpful tip from one of the commenters deep in about making a 5 minute frosting: half a cup of heavy cream whipped, half a cup of sour cream, confectioners sugar and dried coconut. I forgot the extract in the frosting and it was fine. I'd double the frosting recipe for even two cakes and maybe 2.5 it for three cakes. But the frosting flavor (even frosted thinly) was delicious and I received a lot of compliments. Mostly, happily, my mother was content. Thanks for the great recipe! I'll make it again!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this exactly as written, including the frosting. It was perfect—it tasted delicious and looked exactly like the picture. Nice crumb, not overly sweet, fluffy but buttery frosting, perfect amount of coconut flavor. I made the cake the night before and then frosted it then next day. Then I refrigerated it for a couple of hours so that the frosting wasn't too soft. I took it to a party where it sat out for a couple of hours, so it was the perfect temperature when served. It was a big hit!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I agree with Tom about this cake i.e. have your AED handy.  
Sure it's cake but how often do people use that excuse to have high caloric, high fat desserts?
"Oh I'll just have a piece for lunch and a small one for dinner".  This is why so many people in this country are obese and that is increasing.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You can just heat the oil in the skillet while you preheat the oven. That’s how my mom did it. It is definitely a must to heat the oil, though. You’ll taste it if you don’t.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My family, going back three generations at least, always uses bacon grease, instead of the vegetable oil.  And yes, a tall glass of cold buttermilk with chunks of cornbread in it is a delicious treat, especially on a summer evening.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Step 5: You've just removed a hot skillet from the oven. Why would you bother to turn on the burner for a second while you add oil then turn off the burner?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Confused It’s because the 1/4 cup of oil cools the pan down (to 290 when I tried it.I pr

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
this is a fully gluten (and sugar free) version i’ve been making for years. letting the cornmeal hydrate over night make a huge difference. 

combine: 
45 grams (1/3 cup) cornmeal with 1/2 teaspoon each: kosher salt, baking soda, baking powder. set aside.

combine: 	
85 grams (2/3 cup)cornmeal, 1 egg , 200 grams (7 ounces) plain full fat greek yogurt (or buttermilk). refrigerate overnight.

preheat oven to 450-degrees and melt 4 tablespoons butter, vegetable oil, or bacon fat in an 8-inch cast iron skillet. combine dry and wet ingredients, add melted fat. pour batter into hot skillet. lower oven to 425-degrees and bake for 20 minutes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @lynn forgot to mention used an 8-inch cast iron pan.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@lynn
I made your recipe just now and that will be my staple cornbread recipe going forward!!!! Thanks for sharing!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My Memaw called Appalachian style cornbread “a poor girl’s milkshake”. And she always tossed in a tablespoon of fresh churned butter into the cast iron skillet before placing in her wood fired oven. And sometimes after hog killing, she added in rendered hog fat called “cracklings”. Now that’s about as Southern as you can get!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @GHH this is the best thread ever - learning sooo much and the stories are great!! Thank you all for sharing!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Get good cornmeal—stone-ground, if possible. Makes a big difference!
I agree with others re: heating the oil (melting the fat). Put it in the skillet and heat it in the oven at the same time.
But I have never understood putting sugar or any other sweetener in the cornbread itself. This recipe only calls for 2 tablespoons, so it’s probably barely noticeable. But I’ve had corn muffins that might as well have been cupcakes, they were so sweet!  Do yourself a favor and try it without sugar!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There were sweet cornbread families, and no sugar cornbread families (like mine) where I grew up in Appalachia. I knew no family that made both. I was taught to eat whatever was served and say nothing, which was easy enough because of course I liked the sweet cornbread better when I was lucky enough to get some.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@C. Hughes
Same in northeast Louisiana! My family is decidedly a no-sugar cornbread family. (Same goes for vegetables--no sugar) But, I enjoyed "cake" cornbread at friends' houses!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Sugar can intensify the flavor of corn. Sugar also holds moisture so it can help keep the cornbread from drying out so easily, much like sugar in enriched breads. The amount in this recipe is hardly enough to impart a sweetness to the bread. I'd try it both ways and simply compare. Much like salt is used in breads for structure, color, and to regulate the function of yeast, you don't always taste it.

Also, I make a similar recipe that uses sour cream instead of buttermilk.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Never thought I would see the day that skillet cornbread was featured in the NYT.

Cornbread and milk (as described in the tip at the bottom) truly is a staple of life in Appalachia.  My grandfather used to eat it almost every day.  It's what we called "poverty food" that could always be made with the ingredients at hand (milk and cornmeal) even during hard times, and filling enough for a long day of work. Another suggestion: Serve with apple butter (also a poverty food).

author: Lisa Donovan
Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is very good and is more or less the same as my Tennessee great-grandmother Gaga's recipe, although she never made it sweet. (I don't think Southerner's back then liked sweet cornbread). She used bacon grease instead of vegetable oil which I prefer to vegetable oil as well.  Be careful not to over bake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My grandma from North Carolina taught me to make my cornbread in a cast iron pan, however, she insisted that you use butter melted in the hot skillet before adding the batter, not oil. It makes the most delicious crisp edges!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Mine used bacon grease whenever she had it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Lovely piece by Lisa Donovan. She adds a grace note to all she does. And boy did it make me miss New Orleans. This weekend and I’ll think of things that stay with us even as we leave them behind

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I like to char some sweet corn kernels and chopped jalapeno in a cast iron skillet first. Let it cool and fold that (and some good cheddar) into the batter just before pouring into the skillet.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I rarely have buttermilk in the house, and our market carries only a large container, much more than needed.  Plan Greek yogurt is a good substitute.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Alice: I’ve read numerous suggestions stating that buttermilk freezes well.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I don’t know what eating cornbread in buttermilk has to do specifically with Appalachia. My parents were both from Oklahoma and they did it. No sweetener of any kind though.
Plus, always use a heated cast iron skillet, preferably one that belonged to your granny.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Your family may have migrated to Oklahoma from Appalachia, as did so many.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Does anyone here remember the late Lee Bailey, a food/lifestyle writer who preceded the current tsunami of such writers by a few years? He too was from Louisiana, and his trick for getting cornbread with the maximum of crispy crust was to pour very thin layers into *two* preheated cast iron skillets and proceed from there. Works for me. And although I'm a member of the no-sugar camp, I'm not going to start a war over it. Kudos to Mr. Johnson for all his good works.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You sure do complicate things. Both my graduate school educated, Southern parents made skillet cornbread regularly from the fifties until they died at 89 and 95 and used bacon grease,  no sweetener.  One bowl. No need to turn on the stove. You melt the bacon grease in the oven in the skillet you're heating. That skillet will be HOT when you pour in the batter and the hot grease will brown the crust. Why complicate something so simple?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I have a similar recipe but the ratio of cornmeal to flour in mine is 50/50. It also uses butter in place of the oil swirled in the hot skillet. Any reason not to use butter? It makes a nice crunchy crust.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In N. Wilkesboro, NC, we use bacon fat in the pan (don't pour it into the batter) and sprinkle a few tablespoons of dry corn meal in the fat before the batter to prevent sticking. Otherwise a proper deep south recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A key is using GOOD ingredients,  quality cornmeal as well as buttermilk. I'm from the unsweetened cornbread please neighborhood but did try it with a touch of honey - nice depth of flavor addition. Occasionally I like to add a bit of grated cheese also.  Have made it sourdough style and also with a mixture of greek style yogurt and buttermilk. For a totally different take try the browned butter cornbread recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I hope this is allowed.  I don't work for White Lilly, but their Corn Meal is super!  I make cornbread at least once a week, so I want a corn meal readily available at the grocery store down the street.  I know their products aren't available on the west coast.  Used to live in San Diego, so my mom mailed me White Lily flour and corn meal.  It's that good! That was before Amazon . . .

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @LisaJ googled it and not finding it anywhere but amazon which i dont use - where have you found it? It is not a brand that my local store sells. Thank you!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Alexandra Todd
I buy in NC at Food Lion. It truly is the best.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am surprised that no one has mentioned the baking powder-baking soda issue.  I have always made cornbread from my Alabama granny's recipe. She always used soda (also no sugar), and her contention was that soda makes a more moist, and, somewhat paradoxically, lighter, cornbread.  I have tried both, and I prefer the soda.  I believe this is also why so many of these recipes use some kind of acid like buttermilk which is necessary for the soda to perform its leavening qualities.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Ampexona do you use the same amount of soda as powder?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Same question as Ampexona - I made this recipe - but am still looking for the one that tastes like my neighbor - Mama White - used to make every noon when we lived in Alabama.  I’d find excuses to run an errand to her house every day around the time the cornbread was coming out of the oven.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You can surely bake it in an 8x 8 pan - glass or metal, but @jj is right, the crust won’t be the same.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: drizzle 'hot honey' over the butter and corn bread... trust me.. you're welcome

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My first wife, a deep southerner, told me, a northerner, that if god had wanted us to put sugar in cornbread he would've called it corn cake. Readers' thoughts?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Heat the oil or bacon grease in the cast iron skillet in the oven until smoking (Marta commented here too). Then there is no need to heat on the stove top. My family, 4 generations of southern cooks, would not use sugar in the cornbread batter.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I place the skillet in the oven while it preheats. Then I bring it out and put the oil in. That is hot enough for me. However, my grandmother always heated the skillet on top of the stove with the oil in it, and then she scattered a little cornmeal in the hot oil before pouring in the batter. This yields a much nicer crust but I am okay with the payoff from my easier method.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love this recipe already, even though I haven't made it, because I try to avoid animal fats in my diet.  However, I'm not sure the advice about "preferably with milk sourced from small farms" is what I look for in a recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Would you rather the milk was sourced from a big factory farm?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The flavor comparison is worth mentioning.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Nothing better than a good cornbread. Sometimes I'll add ¼ cup fresh squeezed lemon juice, lemon zest, and sub lemon Greek yogurt for buttermilk. It's a nice refreshing riff that reminds me of a good lemon pound cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Other people might like it, but I'd leave out the sugar. I'd also only use one egg. Your mileage might vary.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is the same as my family’s cornbread recipe although we use finely ground white cornmeal. I’ve made it with both and I prefer the texture of using the finely ground.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: So funny - my recipe also came from a photographer - Sally Mann. My husband became close with her making two documentary films and while he was having dinner at her home once, she made cornbread and he raved about it. She not only sent him home with the recipe, but with a big bag of fresh ground cornmeal! It makes me so happy every time I pull out that recipe card. The recipe is almost identical except 1/2 the baking powder and bacon grease instead of vegetable oil (the secret ingredient!)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Maybe it was because of the cornmeal I used, but this cornbread had an unpleasant mealy texture, and sort of a flat, bitter taste.  Handling the hot skillet was intimidating,  and really not worth the effort.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I was kind of disappointed by this one. The taste was good, but it came out super oily.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I wanted to make this vegan and gluten-free so that everyone in my family could eat it. Instead of eggs, I used 4 tablespoons water plus 2 tablespoons ground flax seed (mix and let stand about 5 min.). Instead of buttermilk, used soy milk plus 1 tablespoon apple cider vinegar (also mix and let stand about 5 min.). And for the flour, used gluten-free rice-based flour. It turned out so good! It had a delicious, substantial crust on the outside, moist and good "crumb" on the inside. Yum.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Real cornbread is savory, not sweet. Omitting that, this recipe fits the bill.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
If you can only find larger containers of buttermilk, as someone posted, it does freeze well.
Or you can do what I did -- bought a quart of buttermilk and then looked for other recipes to use it in. Dinner tonight includes this cornbread, Lidey Heuck's buttermilk coffee cake, and Samrin Nosrat's buttermilk marinated chicken. Buttermilk all gone! (Yes there will be green vegetables served as well. No buttermilk there.)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: DO NOT use oil in this recipe. It ruined my cornbread with a terrible chemical taste (I used canola). Use butter or bacon grease as others have suggested - anything but oil.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am from Arkansas originally (now in Brooklyn), my family never makes cornbread without throwing some bacon grease in the skillet while it heats and then pouring the remainder into the cornbread batter. Seriously, it is delicious--whether you prefer a bit of sugar or a can of jalapenos in the mix.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: No flour and no sugar of any kind. Very fresh, stone ground meal has a sweetness on its own.If you want a little more, add fresh or frozen corn kernels to the batter. Bacon drippings in the bottom of the skillet as it heats will give a crunchy crust.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mom used to cook bacon in a cast iron skillet when making cornbread: the skillet got good and hot, the bacon grease seasoned the pan, and we got to enjoy a few slices of bacon with our cornbread and white beans. Highly recommend!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This skillet cornbread was a bit dry for our tastes.  I can understand now why in Appalachia they would crumble it with honey and buttermilk the next day.  It did look pretty in the skillet however!  Personally, I’m going back to my age-old proven recipe from NYT 1994.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Really good texture, I thought it needed just a little more sweetness to highlight the corn flavour.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this exactly as written & it was divine. Served it with salted cultured butter & honey since it isn’t a sweet cornbread (which is a good thing!).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Why not heat the skillet on a stove element rather than in the oven? It is more convenient, faster, and just as effective.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This tastes so good! Every morning I eat blueberries and homemade yogurt for breakfast. I used to add my own homemade granola but now I crumble the leftover cornbread in it and have the tastiest ever breakfast. I use coconut oil instead of vegetable oil.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Modifications for high altitude(5,000-7,000 feet)?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mother always left some bacon grease in the cast iron skillet after combining it with the cornmeal mixture.  Then she dusted the hot skillet with about half a handful of cornmeal before she poured in the mixture.  About 10 minutes before it was done, she inverted the cornbread and cooked another 10 minutes. It always came out with a nice dusting cornmeal on top.  She always used white cornmeal.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This sounds yummy, but I only have a 12” cast iron pan. How to adjust for it?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Invest in a 9” skillet

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Multiply ingredient amounts by 1.44 (precise) or 1.5 (close enough).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You can just bake the unadjusted recipe in your larger skillet: lower the baking time to compensate for the thinner/flatter spread of batter in the larger pan. This actually will work to your advantage, if you’re a fan of dark brown, crispy crust - you’ll get more of it! Similar to the strategy “A. Marie” notes

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I use 1/2 the sweetener 
I’m not a fan of sweet cornbread

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Flip it in the heavy cast-iron skillet? I sense a disaster in the making!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: How do you flip this in the pan?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Adding oil to a hot skillet is going to spatter and make a mess on your stove top.  Better to heat the oil/bacon fat in the pan in the oven and pour the batter into the hot pan to get that lovely crispy bottom.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Rather dry

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve made a Texas version (No Sugar! And melted butter instead of vegetable oil)  since I was in junior high school.  No buttermilk?  Just add a tablespoon of lemon juice or white vinegar to a scant 1 c of milk (fat free is ok but just add a little extra melted butter) and let it sit for 15-20 minutes while oven (with oiled skillet in it, of course).  Leftovers make great crispy croutons.  I spray them with olive oil before toasting them slowly in a low oven.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
...and I've used homemade coconut milk kefir for the buttermilk in other recipes.
Will try that in this recipe...

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Excellent recipe, and I am from a long long line of Appalachian corn bread makers. I do not use sweetners in mine, but will put honey on it for a treat.  And yes, definitely eat it with milk in a bowl.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Ugh! Way too salty and generally flavorless. And step-5 is seriously vague! Just how hot should the oil be when one dumps it into the batter?!? I'm reasonably sure if one got it too hot, one would have deep-fried chunks of cornbread inside the baked cornbread, no matter how vigorously one stirred as one added the oil to the batter!

My finished product popped right out of the skillet and looked gorgeous but that was the end of the positives for me....

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Yes, WAY too salty, like a pretzel. I think ½ teaspoon of salt would be plenty. Easy to make and except for the saltiness, it was tasty. But after one slice of this salt bread, my wife and I agreed to slide the rest into the trash.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Graveyard Stew: leftover cornbread soaked in a glass of milk or buttermilk, honey optional. My mother commented often about this Depression era fix through her youth of Missourian poverty.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Overall, I like this recipe. However, I grew up in the South, raised by my grandmother, and no one I know ever put ANY sugar in the best cornbread I've ever consumed. Sugar in cornbread was referred to as Yankee cornbread.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Doesn’t mean it’s not delicious

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The late Danièle Mazet-Delpeuch, former cuisinière to President Mitterand, regularly accompanied sweet American-style cornbread with foie gras from her farm on the Dordogne. A delish fusion combo

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I make cornbread 10-15 times a year. I just  this recipe and decided to try it. It came out perfect! This will be my go-to.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve cooked many cornbread recipes, but repeat few. I will cook this again. Heating the oil with the pan, cast iron in my case, worked well. Next time I’ll keep but decrease the amount of sugar.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This turned out dry, mealy, and tasteless, despite following the recipe closely. I did double the recipe and made two skillets' worth of cornbread because I thought it would be a hit, but no one in our group of 20 enjoyed it. Will not be repeating.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I didn’t care for this recipe at all. Flavorless and incredibly dry. Followed directions as written. Win some, lose some.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this with anticipation, but the recommended size of skillet is way off. I have an 8 inch I normally  use for cornbread, but followed the guidance and end up with thin, dry, overcooked “bread.” Might try again in smaller skillet, the batter tasted good.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This was good! I reduced the cornmeal by 1/2 cup (what I had) and increased flour by the same. 
I also didn't have buttermilk so added 1 1/2 Tb vinegar to whole milk. I let the mixture rest for 30 minutes before baking.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I added 1 cup of corn kernels.  Very delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Highly disappointed. This mostly tastes like baking powder.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious. Perfect crumb, moist but not sticky, wonderful corny flavor.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: No oil. Butter.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I used sorghum syrup, Possibly the best southern skillet cornbread ever.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I saw the Skillet Cornbread title and was hoping to find out how my mother made cornbread perfectly in a skillet on the stovetop. She used bacon grease, no oil, no butter, no sugar. I know she put a lid on it and flipped it halfway through. Does anyone know how to do this?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Adjusted recipe for 9X9 dark pan.  REALLY, REALLY GOOD.  Added 1/2 cup Flour (vs 1/4); 1 3/4 - 2 cups Buttermilk (vs 1 1/2); 1 TBSP Maple Syrup + 1 TBSP Sugar (2 TBSP Sugar); 4 TBSP Butter, melted & browned in oven before adding batter (vs 1/4 cup Oil)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: How can I bake the cornbread without a cast iron skillet?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In my experiences in baking CB, a cast-iron skillet is a MUST for that sizzle & crunch.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You need something heavy that absorbs and holds a lot of heat - to get that deep brown crust the picture shows. I bet a Dutch oven would work.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @JJ I used a Dutch oven and it worked fine. However I slightly overcooked it so it cooks very fast when you preheat the pan. I need to take it out at 20 minutes not 25 minutes

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I agree.  Why put the skillet on a burner?  It's already hot!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Love cornbread. Hate buttermilk.

Has anyone tried this using regular milk?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I used bacon fat instead of oil. I just put the skillet in the oven to melt the fat. I used medium grind stone ground cornmeal (Bob's Red Mill) which had a great flavor.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Note reminds me that my mother, who grew up poor in Ozarks of Arkansas in a family that had migrated from Appalachia in 1850s, told me she often only had cornbread with buttermilk for supper. She loved it her whole life. Agree on bacon grease— since they rendered their own fat and no commercial oil to purchase, it was that tradition.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Instead of the oil, add 1 stick of butter & 1/4 cup of honey to the cold pan prior to putting it in the oven.  Mix as directed & pour over honey butter. You're welcome.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe is not all that different from the one I grew up making in my family except for the added sweetener and we use bacon grease. This one is okay but I prefer it my way.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I don’t know…. Sugar is usually considered questionable in cornbread, at least traditionally. It is not cake. I did note the 5 stars so I may have to break the ironclad rule and try it!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: When we poured the hot oil into the batter it didn’t sizzle, it exploded! Drops of hot oil were all over the kitchen. Fortunately no one was hurt. What did we do wrong? As for the cornbread, it is still in the oven. I’ll get back to you on whether or not we like it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It is better if you cook bacon in the skillet first and use the bacon fat instead of the oil.  Really no need to use flour or sugar or honey - unless you want a sweet cornbread.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Used Nueske’s bacon fat to grease the pan, and I’m pretty sure that’s why it tastes so very good on the second day. FWIW, swapping in WWF for APF wasn’t noticeable. I rarely make cornbread with such a high ratio of meal to flour, but I loved this and will make this recipe again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Cornbread and milk may be an Appalachian treat, but my mother grew up on a farm in the north country of New York State and ate it regularly. She called it Johnnycake and milk.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I just made this, and it was fantastic. Super quick and simple to make. I made two substitutions. I have celiacs, so switched the flour for Bob’s Red Mill Gluten Free Pancake Mix - which is great for GF cakes etc. And I switched the vegetable oil for olive oil. Will definitely be making again. Thank you, NYT, for another great recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Quarter cup of oil is a heckuva lot of oil. I’ll probably cut it by 25% next time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I thought it was too much oil in the pan; next time I'm going to try it with half the amount. And my oven needed less that 20 minutes cooking time   My husband thought it wasn't sweet enough but I did. I used honey so next time I'm going to go with sugar, maybe dark brown.  However, despite all my comments we loved it and it is fast and easy to put together!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Good recipe; I first made it when Melissa Clark put a brief ingredients-only version of it on fb not too long ago. There were no directions so I just made it as I've made other cornbreads. I heated my 23cm Le Creuset pan on the stove with a tablespoon of the oil and added the other 3 tablespoons to the liquids. (I did think the pan was small; it overflowed a bit. A true 9" or 10" would be better.) Photo looks like yellow cornmeal; I used white finely ground. Thoughts?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I heat the iron skillet with vegetable oil while the oven is preheating to 425F. Over the years I’ve learned to use ample oil such that it reaches the top of the skillet after adding the batter. This ensures a delicious crust.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I'm dying to try this recipe. One thing, I've used a special homemade coconut milk kefir (almost as thick as Crisco) and have used that in place of buttermilk in recipes -- King Arthur's classic sourdough waffle recipe, for one.  I WILL try that in this cornbread recipe, too. 
The kefir certainly provides the "fat requirement".

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Buttermilk substitute: Add one tablespoon of white vinegar per cup of whole milk and let rest for 10 minutes. I used half and half because we don't use milk. Using the half and half made the mixture resemble the texture of real buttermilk. I have read that you can also use lemon juice instead of the vinegar which I did try. I feel that using the white vinegar yields more of a buttermilk like flavor.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: i didn't have enough cornmeal so I used a cup of cornmeal and a cup of masa, and I didn't have milk so I used kefir. Delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Way too salty and not tasty - followed the recipe exactly. Maybe I just didn't know what 'authentic' corn bread is supposed to taste like?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Just made this.  Outstanding.  I don't put sugar in cornbread, and then just saw the comments for making it last.  With cornbread like this, it won't last because you'll down it when it comes out of the oven.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Excellent results.  I used some bacon grease from breakfast amended with butter and baked on convection for just about 15 minutes. Stone ground cornmeal from Maine Grains. It is not the least bit sweet (I used 2T honey). I finally have a go to cornbread recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The instruction to heat the oil in the skillet, and then add it to the batter was confusing, especially hidden so far down in the instructions. I assumed I was pouring the batter without oil into the oiled skillet… Only to find out after doing so that I missed a step. Would be helpful to have this called out earlier in the recipe. Anyone else have this issue?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I made mine in a Dutch oven and it works fine too but it does heat up very fast and I almost burnt it so keep an eye on it -- cooks sooner than you would think (the minimum time versus the maximum). 

Perfect served with chilli.

The next day leftovers were perfect lathered in butter and drizzled with real maple syrup. I liked this even better.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
tasted a little bitter, perhaps due to a poorly seasoned pan?
Next time I would omit the flakey sea salt on top. Crystals were too big.
But altogether a big family hit.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Oh-No Nancy Mine also tasted a little bitter, but I thought it was just that it wasn’t sweet enough for me. My skillet is plenty seasoned. I followed recipe to a T except to use butter while heating skillet instead of oil. I think the recipe just fell flat for me.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Excellent. Used 1 cup buttermilk and 1/2 cup Greek yogurt and 1/2 bacon fat 1/2 butter in place of oil. No sugar.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made for Superbowl. Awfully dry, porous and tasteless.  Threw away half uneaten.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Dan Donovan I recently made the Brown Butter Cornbread and it got raves, although I think I burnt the butter a tiny bit, truth be told. Thought I'd try this one next. Not sure my skillet or oil was as hot as intended but we'll see!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I baked this as directed, with the cornmeal the recipe suggested, and I found it to be unusually gritty, not sweet, and honestly even under salted. Maybe I’m used to a different style of cornbread, but I was surprised by all the positive reviews. It smelled amazing though!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The crispy crust and fluffy cake were perfect and this recipe was easy. I think I learned from this recipe that I do like a sweeter cornbread so I would add more honey/sugar/maple syrup next time. But nothing that wasn't solved by a drizzle of honey post-bake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I found this too dry and too salty.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This was really tasty.
I make my own corn meal by putting popcorn kernels in the blender. It really adds an extra layer of complexity. That and bacon grease in the pan.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Great recipe but way too much salt.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mother’s family made cornbread very much like this, though with sugar (jam or honey) ON, not IN. We offer tips for a good crust: When pouring hot oil from the skillet into the batter, leave a bit in the skillet. After stirring oil into the batter, sprinkle a half tablespoon of cornmeal into the hot skillet, swirl, then add the batter. Second, when taking the cooked cornbread out of the oven, flip it onto a plate and slide it back into the skillet. Return to the oven for another 4 minutes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've actually had Pableaux's cornbread with his RB&R. RIP to an amazing person.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The cornbread was flat. Is it supposed to be soft before entering into the skillet.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: THIS is so much like what I grew up with (minus the sugar/honey)....and then I got to the note about the buttermilk. Whoosh--I'm 9 years old watching my dad crumble skillet cornbread into a LARGE glass and fill the glass with buttermilk. Not my cup of tea, but, wow, that sure brought back memories!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This sounds amazing.  With the egg whites I would make meringue kisses to serve on the side or top the pudding with instead of more cream as suggested.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious!  I added a 1/3 c of 2% milk since I only had a pint container of heavy cream.   Recipe made 5 small glass jar servings.   It really does set up beautifully several hours later but best overnight.  Yes, I made this at 9pm and had 2 spoonfuls at breakfast today.  It's a pot de creme.  Meringue kisses are a great use of the whites.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: What does it mean that the milk is "steaming"? Any bubbles?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Given how hard it is to find eggs at present and how costly they are, it would be a good time to offer a cornstarch thickened pudding instead of one that requires 4 egg yolks.  Context matters.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @DCNative  "Steaming" means exactly that, i.e., steam is rising up from the milk.  If they wanted bubbles, there are several ways to describe bubbles in cooking, because there are several ways/sizes to achieve them.  "Steaming" is a basic condition in cooking whether the substance is water or milk or juice or, 60 years ago, Dr. Pepper heated in a pan with thinly sliced lemons tossed in right as DP steams & is removed from heat.  But also, see "steaming hot," which could be beef roast or a cook.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is toe-curlingly good.  Added 1.5 Tb butter before pouring the custard over the chocolate.  It only made 5 4 oz yogurt jars so double if you want more.  Really not difficult if you read over the process a few times before attempting.  I had invested in an instant read spatula ( about $15 on amazon), which was very helpful in monitoring temps.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am curious if preparing puddings in this manner heats egg yolks to the 160degree threshold required for food safety. I've made similar puddings and flourless tortes many times in the past using this technique. But use of a thermometer might be recommended given the current concern over bird flu.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @JL Wedell you’re cooking the egg yolks for at least 2 min to 180 degrees. They are for sure fully cooked.  Did you read the recipe?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Just in time for Super Bowl/ Valentine’s Day.  I had all the ingredients and just made this- it hasn’t completely set yet, but licking the bowl and spoon was chocolate nirvana.  Wow.  I can hardly wait for tomorrow!  Lots of dirty dishes, but you can’t make killer custard pudding without breaking some eggs!  If you find them, use them to make this!

commentBody: You can reduce the dishes to 2 if you melt the chocolate in the microwave and also make the custard in the microwave using a 4 cup pyrex measuring cup. Stir the custard into the chocolate and you are done. Also much faster! I make almost all custards and cornstarch thickened puddings this way since I read about the method in an NYT comment!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hm. It might be a good time to feature more eggless recipes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Lemon even at $9/carton, it’s 4 eggs which is 1/3 of the box, so $3 and you can reuse the whites for something sweet or savory. Worth the eggs, IMO. :)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @A thought all these has eggs for $1.99 a dozen!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this today. Recipe directions are explicit and accurate. By "steaming," it means that you see steam coming up before it boils. That's when you take it off the flame. (I also used a Thermopen; recommend.) I used a high quality dark chocolate. The pudding are sublime!!

commentBody: @Cynthia I have 100% great quality dark chocolate. Do you think it’d be too bitter if I used it? I received a few bars as a present and honestly I can’t eat them as they’re way too bitter for me.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe isn’t complicated at all!   Make a basic custard and pour it over some chopped  chocolate.  It’s wonderful and EASY!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This sounds like an easy version of chocolate pots de crème that doesn’t require baking.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Very good. Pretty straightforward. Keep in mind that you’re using 4 eggs for 4-5 couple ounce servings so these days it’s a luxury recipe

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Yes eggs are expensive right now.  It’s a luxury.  Enjoy every bite.  Cornstarch won’t get you this texture - it’s a whole different thing.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Came out great. I’ve never made custard before but found this simple and easy. My temp got much higher than the recommended, but it still came out perfect. Used a 72% chocolate and tasted well balanced. Will be a new valentines tradition.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Only a half cup of sugar?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: That's a quarter cup of sugar per cup of whipped cream.  With the chocolate added, each of the 4 servings would be around 2/3 cup, and each of these would contain about 2 tablespoons of sugar.  To me, that seems to be more than sufficient sugarwise.

author: Lisa Donovan

commentBody: @CFXK Agreed. I just made it and I find it is too sweet ( or too chocolatey) for my taste.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Followed recipe exactly and found it very clear to follow. Used distilled Mexican vanilla for the taste rather than a bean. Don’t skip the sieve step, it matters in order to get that silky mouth taste. Very good valentines dessert.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Absolutely delicious, easy and perfect for a lazy, easy to advance-prep yet glamorous, grown up dinner party. Even just for one!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: prep time 5 minutes?  The directions allot 5 minutes just to heat the cream, then there's all the other steps.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Didn’t think about the 1/2 cup sugar until seeing the yield. So sweet ,we can’t eat it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I thought it was a little too thick, as much like ganache as pudding. Would using light cream rather than heavy give a looser result?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It only makes 5 servings.  the pudding is delicious specially if you use high quality bittersweet chocolate 70% cocoa It is a worth adding the toppings suggested because it tastes and looks better.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: So - do you pour the hot custard over the sieve on the hard choco chips?  and then it all melts together ?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Wow this was delicious! Will definitely make again and not too difficult. I also used the suggestion to make the leftover egg whites into merengues which ended up being my husband’s favorite part.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this exactly as written. It’s delicious, but much more like pots de creme or even chocolate mousse than like pudding. I’m not sure how to thin it. Maybe half milk half cream, or maybe one fewer egg yolk.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @TH  - read the recipe once or twice and have all the ingredients ready. Make sure the chocolate is finely chopped.  Slowly heat the cream, then add a few ladles of it to the egg so it doesn't scamble, then add that back to the cream. Use a thermometer to slowly cook the cream/egg mixture to 178° then pour it through a strainer onto the chopped chocolate.  I put the chopped chocolate into a large flexible rubber measuring container so it's easy to pour.  It's very delicious and you can use the egg whites for breakfast.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I assume if using vanilla extract there’s no need for the sieve..? Or am I missing something?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Candice There are random egg solid nubbins that the sieve removes.  You won’t be sorry you did this step😊

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Candice. I often see straining custard as a waste of time. I think people are freaked out by bits of milk or egg but if you’re not vegan (I am not) then you’ve made your peace with eating lots of parts. I think straining is just for the smooth texture.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Will the warm custard really be hot enough to melt the chocolate? Especially if using chocolate chips? I didn’t see a step of melting the chocolate first.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There is no thickening agent on the recipe, e.g., cornstarch.  Will the pudding actually thicken?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: egg yolks are the thickening agent. the chocolate also helps thicken the custard when it is cold.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is not thickening. I tried this recipe twice. I am coming out with a chocolate creme anglaise.   The first time, I overcooked it waiting for it to thicken and ended up with a grainy texture. The second time, I was religious about stirring and not letting it get too hot and it is soup. What gives?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Liz thickening happens when cooking the eggs with the cream. You can go a bit hotter but what I do is take it on and off the heat. Like right when steam starts coming out of the mixture, take it off the heat but continue stirring like crazy. And, if you have a stainless steel pot that’s got more surface area on the bottom (think: bigger) even better. Once the steam goes away, put it back on the heat. However, little curds will form, but keep repeating this process until it looks like thick pudding. The curds are just the start of the process, eventually the whole thing forms into “one big curd” that is thick and delicious. It’s very similar to making pastry cream so if you look up a video on that, it will help you.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Made this today with Ghirardelli’s 100% unsweetened cocoa baking bar.  Because that’s what you get when you send your spouse to the grocery store with instructions to buy nothing less than 70%.
I added an extra tbs of sugar to make up for it.  And used 20 gms of semi-sweet chocolate chips    as a substitute for 20 gms of unsweetened chocolate.  The rest was exactly as the recipe indicated.  It was sweet enough!  But it was much less a pudding/custard and more of a dense ganache texture.  Delicious, but much too rich.  We could only split one serving.  I will keep this in mind as a dessert to serve in very small portions, such as double-shot size.  Or try again with a 70% cocoa bar to see if it makes that much difference…doubtful though.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: absolutely delicious. cut the sugar by a third and added a sprinkle of (decaf) espresso powder to the chocolate. used country crock plant based cream to make it non-dairy (and used same cream to make non-dairy creme fraiche). highly recommend!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Justine
Can you order hominy from Rancho Gordo (based in California)? If so, I highly recommend it because rehydrating and cooking it are easy - and your kitchen smells like a fresh corn tortilla. One bag yields 7 cups, and cooked hominy freezes well. I just used a year-old bag and soaked it for 24 hours for a red posole. Cooking instructions are on the Rancho Gordo site.

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@LaBolilla

I did not know that.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @LaBolilla No Cumin PLEASE! Authentic Mexican food rarely uses this spice, and if it is called for the quantity is a pinch, never a teaspoon or God forbid  more.  Also, with all the milder green why Kale?  This recipe needs various modifications—besides what has already been mentioned, substitute the Kale with dark green of romaine lettuce, and add a pinch of epazote.  Órale

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @LaBolilla Depends on the region. In Guerrero cumin is used in a variety of dishes. It is used in most much anything made with chile guajillo, including cuche en salsa roja and chorizo. On the other hand, pozole verde is very popular in Guerrero, and I’ve not had it with cumin…nor kale for that matter.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This is in no way remotely similar to an authentic green pozole. Authentic green pozole has only pork meat and hominy corn in it. Once made you add chiles onion radish and lettuce. The broth is made with pork meat and pipián (a green paste made from green pepitas) 
I have no doubt that this must be delicious but it is misleading to call it pozole.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Vio
It’s a vegetarian pozole and it’s pozole because it’s just a different preparation than you’re used to. Not everyone eats things exactly the same way and it’s OK.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:

The pozole verde I have had in Mexico is made with turkey or chicken, not pork.
