commentBody: @Matt I found that adding a little sugar to this recipe nicely moderated the acidity.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Easy to make into a vegan meal but beware that the prep time is vastly understated. I would suggest 30 minutes before the oven and another 15 after.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Authentic pozole does not use cheese or cream, you can eat it with tostadas slathered in cream though

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe is so insanely good. I’m not kidding, this is in my top ten meals I have ever eaten and definitely in my top 3 I have ever cooked. Absolutely delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Looking through some of the notes below, be sure to use the condiments--sour cream, cabbage, radishes, Queso Fresca, avocado, etc. They are integral to the final soup, not just an optional add on. Without, yes--it's pretty tangy. With? Sublime. SO GOOD.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Very disappointed in this recipe. As some others have said, it was so bitter & sour that it was  inedible when made as written. Maybe the tomatillos were unripe, but my sense is that 2 pounds is just too much. The roasted lime also imparted bitterness, I think, and its juice overpowered the other flavors. I managed to salvage the posole with more broth and seasoning, and since I didn't have more hominy, I added corn. It's edible now, but still very sour. Proceed with caution if you make this!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Very tasty and I'll make it again (with more peppers!), but no way that 15 minutes suffices for prep time. It took me that long just to separate the stems and leaves of the cilantro!  With all the chopping and whatnot, I'd say an hour of prep time is more realistic.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I like pozole as a rule but this is awful. Characterizations as inedible sour are correct. A waste of expensive ( I cooked my own dried pozole) ingredients.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I ended up throwing the whole pot away. It was insanely acidic and tart and no amount of anything would take that away. Followed the recipe to a “T”

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Shred or thinly slice the cabbage.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've made this twice, super delicious both times. I skipped the extra steps with the poblanos and just pureed them with the other veggies both times. I did leave out the roasted lime the second time--I think there's plenty of acid just from the tomatillos, and you can add lime juice at the table.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Matt Here in Guatemala tomatillo vary greatly in acidity. The larger ones are usually very sour while THE smaller ones(grape sized) are less so. I go slow, adding only a fraction of what the recipe calls for, and add more after tasting.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Very good recipe, especially with homemade veggie broth (included a Serrano pepper).  The kale and cilantro stems are totally not needed or advised, getting way too far from traditional posole.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Followed the recipe almost exactly, but the flavors were not well-balanced. There was a bitterness to the broth-possibly from the roasted limes? The sour from the tomatillos/limes was too much. It also needed more umami- the bites with hominy, cheese, or avocados were the best. Overall, a lot of work for a mediocre stew.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Totally agree!!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Close to a disaster.  Unbelievably sour.  Added salt to counter the sourness.  Made it both salty and sour.  Finally, in a last ditch effort to salvage something, added sugar.  That finally blunted the sourness.  The result was edible but not at all notable.  Two pounds of tomatillos was obviously way too much.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I thought this was a great recipe. I had to use canned tomatillos because that was all I could access and it turned out great. Also used spinach instead of kale. This is a great vegetarian pozole recipe!  Will make again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: 15 minutes to do all the prep is completely preposterous unless you’re a professional cook. Why so unrealistic?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I have never felt so swindled by a recipe.  This was  inedible.  I followed the recipe exactly, lost a wonderful quart of homemade stock in the process.  
Many thanks to those who suggested sugar to balance the extreme acidity—it did help, but not nearly enough.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @EEC It depends on the size and acidity of your tomatillos.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Cooking this in fall, it wasn’t possible to find tomatillos, but I substituted with salsa verde and roasted some green bell peppers with the veggies and it was absolutely delicious. I opted for more protein by adding diced chicken thighs and using bone broth vs vegetable broth. Also used fresh oregano. Excellent.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Oh. My. Goodness!!! This is spectacular! I used half a bag of rancho gordo hominy and soaked it 12 hours, then boiled it a couple hours, and used that instead of canned hominy. I used my own homemade veggie broth. The chilis were hard to peel, so I chopped them up after roasting and waiting, and just used them. No problem at all. I wondered about roasting the lime, but it was perfect, and the whole soup tastes so limy! We all just loved it. I HIGHLY recommend this recipe!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you are vegan, Vialife feta is a decent substitute for queso fresco in recipes like this. Before the omnivores come for me, 1) if you’re not vegan ignore my comment, 2) I know it’s not identical, but it’ll serve the same salty/creamy purpose.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In experimenting with the basic Posole recipe, I have had good success with using diced, roasted hatch green chilies in place of the others.They add a complexity that enhances the broth.Also I matchstick cut the radishes, and sometimes use chayote and/or zucchini matchsticks as well, adding these raw veggies plus cilantro into the bottom of the bowl before ladling in the broth and topping with green onions, lime juice and a big handful of crushed tortilla chips. For me, hominy is the highlight.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: What a spectacular recipe! I used local New Mexico green chile rather than the poblanos since it’s already roasted and in the freezer! Read the instructions through a couple of times. Lots of steps and well worth it! Don’t skip on the toppings as they add taste and texture!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: whoops!  i've a family of meat eaters and I added the smoked chicken package from TJ's and it's great!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious! Subbed a can of salsa verde for half the tomatillos because I wasn’t able to get enough. Also made with half hominy and half chickpeas. Otherwise made as written and it came out delicious!! This is a very hands on recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love this dish, but it is NOT 15min prep. For someone who cooks regularly but still considers themselves an intermediate chef, this takes a while to get ready.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It took me two days to make this delicious dish, and I agree it’s the thinly sliced radishes, the queso cheese, shredded cabbage and avocado that elevates this posole. Full disclosure: I don’t care for kale — soooo I roasted and shredded an organic chicken, substituting that protein for the kale. It made a delicious broth. I served the posole with chips although homemade tortillas would’ve been quite fun to make. On a cold winter’s day, this posole brings warmth and freshness to your family’s table. I served limeade with the posole. This dish was made with love for the family that I love.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Speaking of Rancho Gordo, I used their hominy, and cooked up a bunch of their Santa Maria Pinquito beans and added those in, along with the cooking broth, largely because I just love beans with my hominy. It might sound like it's a lot of work, but it's really not. A few bits of prep, and worth every step.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Might substitute corn if hominy is difficult to find

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You can order hominy (called Cacahuazintle) online from Masienda. You have to nixtamalize it, but that seems easy. Reviews say it is outstanding

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I wonder if fresh spinach would work as well as Kale (or in addition to)?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @JB spinach is softer so you get less texture. But if you want to bulk the soup out with a nutritions item, spinach does the job nicely!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I would use Swiss chard rather than spinach if you want to avoid kale (it’s my go to kale replacement). It won’t disintegrate the way spinach would.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Jarred salsa verde works just fine for this recipe. Shredded chicken is also good in it. Garbanzo beans can be substituted for canned hominy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Ingrid thank you!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was not good - total fail - way too much acidity.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this pozole for my picky family and everyone loved it. It had depth from roasting veggies, great citrus tang and the canned hominy worked perfectly. I added a cup more of stock as it cooked down and subbed red cabbage for green, because I had it on hand. Perfect for a winter night with a roast chicken.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Justine, I am allergic to corn, but I will make this soup with beans instead of hominy. It won't be authentic, but it is as close as I can get!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I tried this last night, it was amazing. I pressure cooked 1lb of pork shoulder for 3hrs, pulled it and added it plus its juice instead of kale. I also used chicken stock instead of vegetable broth. It does require more salt than I’m accustomed to using to balance out the acids.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I make it this way all the time and it's always a hit on cold winter days.  My only quibble is why the lime, when the tomatillos have so much tang and acid?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Really excellent recipe! I added chicken thighs after letting the onions sweat for a few minutes, then shredded those at the end. Worked well!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Really incredible flavor explosion. Next time I will omit the extra steaming and peeling of the poblanos to save time and gratuitous dishes. I only roasted half a lime per the other critiques on acidity levels and it was perfect. A bright and welcome antidote to any seasonal blues!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Very good overall. I added a pound of chopped, cooked pork to the dish. The amount of lime I believe could be halved. A whole lime was a bit too much. The toppings make the dish pop. The kale is a nice addition, not overwhelming. Ole!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A bit salty for my taste. Will use less when I make it again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Why oh why ruin the dish by putting kale into it?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Sort of like the Hollywood Handshake, but from my husband. Added picked rotisserie chicken, subbed chicken stock. Delicious and perfect on a cold January night.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Incredibly delicious! Made as written and revised after reading all the notes. No roasted lime. Instead I squeezed half a fresh lime into the food processor. Also did not roast cilantro stems. Sautéed half with the onion and garlic, and added half raw to the food processor. Did not steam and peel the poblanos (they just went into the food processors with their cousins). Also: y'all scared me off the kale. The soup without garnishes doesn't have enough body, but I was generous with the garnishes!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Try with the kale. Not a fan, generally, but this whole concoction worked SO WELL together!! And the garnishes are integral to it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Wow yum!   Toppings essential.   Tangier than I expected but not in an unpleasant way.   Will make again!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I was eager to make this. Looks delicious! But...after reading through the directions just now before buying the ingredients, I cannot see how this would take a mere 15 minutes of prep time! Anybody?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I prefer more smoky, roasted flavor. It's better to roast tomatillos whole, and let the skins brown, before you blend them. This will give a deeper, smoky flavor- otherwise if you chop up the tomatillos and then roast, they just become watery. There's a lot of  hominy in this soup, which is nice, but I also love poblanos. Next time, I'll add an extra poblano, blend half of the poblanos with the rest of the roasted ingregidents , and chop the other half and leave as is in the soup, as instructed.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Fantastic. What I changed - added extra oregano, added 2tsp of chili powder, used vegan "chickn" broth cubes, kept the skins on the tomatillos. Turned out perfect, not overly sour at all. Still tart but I think the chili powder really helped with that - I pulled the idea from another pozole recipe. 

Topped with Chihuahua cheese, Greek yogurt, guac, cilantro, and radish/carrot pickles.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Added one small shredded pork loin.  Posole is a staple in our house. This one is very tangy, farm to table rustic. Delicious. You could add some agave to cut the acidity if it bothers you, but it is mitigated with the crema and cheese.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Substitutes for hominy:
chickpeas, sweet corn, barley, brown rice & wild rice, polenta and regular grits, buckwheat grits, samp (South African corn), or adzuki beans.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I also found this recipe very acidic.  There was also a slight bitterness to the dish.  I surmised that the bitterness came from the lime peel.  Not sure it needed more acidity from the lime.  We added some crushed corn chips to try to mask the bitterness.  

I think I would make it again with some modifications.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Did you include the peel in the soup?  The instructions say to discard it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Phenomenal flavors. Next time, before adding hominy I will simply immersion blend everything including the kale and poblano.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Loved this. Stuck somewhat close to recipe. I had 5 poblanos so roasted them all and blended 1 with the puree.  This base is so delicious that I almost regretted adding so much broth. It would make a great sauce for enchiladas.  Next time I won’t hesitate to roast more vegetables. The kale I bought was a pre cut bag so the woody stems weren’t cut out. That made it slightly unpleasant. I’m sure just about any green can be used here. I will make this again and use a bag of frozen collard greens.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’m allergic to poblano chili’s. Any substitution?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Tasty but not like the pozole verde I know.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I was disappointed in this 5 star recipe. It took longer than I anticipated with those fiddly steps, and at the end lacked a depth of flavor that, imo, it should have had. And I didn't use those dreadful cans of hominy, I prepped it using dried posole.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This has a lot of steps.. is there a
More straightforward set of instructions?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was kind of complicated and messy to make, but so delicious! The broth! Next time I'll use less kale though.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I Enjoyed this recipe a lot with some lite tweaks based on the amounts I had available. I added the kale towards to end so it didn’t get too soft . Just a few minutes in the finished soup and it was great. Poblano skins were fine left on , saved time and dishes

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In my experience, prepping the veggies takes much longer the stated 15 minute prep time. Tips for efficiency?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Didn’t bother with peeling poblanos. Would eliminate the lime in the roasting process as it makes the pozole bitter. Doubled the broth and added frozen corn kernels to cut the bitter.  Consider leaving some seeds in the peppers for added heat. Will probably add some chopped chicken for the carnivores. HNY!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This recipe is super bitter and leaves a lingering bitterness in your throat even after eating it. I suspect it's one of these 2 culprits (I skipped the kale so it's not that)
-roasted cilantro stems
-overcooking the lime juice

I would skip roasting the cilantro stems and add lime at the very end.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This recipe is very special. I’ve made pozole many times red and green and even white, but never with these interesting flavors.
I didn’t peel the veggies- I put tomatillos in whole. Baking them on a bed of cilantro stalks may be the magic. Will make this again and hope it turns out as well. Used whole cumin not ground. 
Served it with warm bolillos. Thank you!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
MAKE THIS! For context: I’m white with Irish background and live in Canada. I have zero expertise with Mexican food! So- TAKE THIS AS YOU LIKE! ;) I only made changes to the recipe cause (1) my kids don’t like spicy (see note above re: white!) and (2) I’m in Canada and authentic Mexican ingredients aren’t as readily available. 
Changes made: I used about a quarter lb tomatillos cause that’s all I had; I didn’t use a poblano or jalapeño and subbed two green peppers (so sorry if this makes anyone who is Mexican reading this cringe! I cringed but it is what it is!); used basic curly kale instead of lacinato. I agree to give yourself closer to 2 hours. I DID find hominy at my local Safeway (shout out Burnaby Heights Safeway) and it was so delicious. Others have talked about replacing with white beans… sure but it’s not the same and I loved the texture and consistency of the hominy. I didn’t have quesa fresco so I used feta instead. Also added pickled jalapeño on top, in addition to other toppings cause they weren’t in it. IT WAS DELICIOUS AND DELIGHTFUL!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I added 3 blocks of tofu to give it some protein and make it more hearty. The tofu soaks up the flavor nicely. It seemed a little over complicated with setting the poblano aside. I tossed all the sheet pan into the food processor and it turned out great. Something, maybe the lime neutralizes the spice because it was quite mild, despite a lot of peppers. Could have used some more hominy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I loved this! This recipe strays from tradition, but nonetheless, the flavors were wonderful—bright, complex, earthy. To keep this plant based, I skipped the dairy and found the avocado was essential to bringing it all together. I added a can of pinto beans for extra protein and added a few cups of water at the end because my soup was incredibly thick and concentrated. I think cooking hominy from scratch would take this to another level, as the texture and flavor of canned was something to be desired. I’m wondering if adding fried corn tortilla strips or really good/fresh tortilla chips would be a quick way to impart that wonderful corn flavor that was somewhat lacking in the canned hominy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
If available add nopalitos (cut up fresh cactus paddles)’. I prefer the Safeway Signature salsa verde to Herdez. 
Let me add, Mexico has many variations on virtually every dish. Some add jitomate (red tomato) to guacamole, others insist that’s forbidden.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Stunningly delicious! I used one 16-oz can pozole and it was a good amount. (Next time will try dry pozole, which needs to be soaked overnight first.) Per another comment, in lieu of tomatillos, I used one 7-oz can of Herdez salsa verde. For the amount of effort and time (both moderate) this dish packed so much flavor! I would serve this to guests. Leftovers are wonderful.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @LauraB a time saver!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was delicious! I made it a couple months ago and I am craving it again. I ended up roasting the chilis and tomatillos and limes the day before I made it (for logistical reasons), and that worked out fine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious. Took a noob like me 1.5 hrs.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Poblanos weren't available so I subbed for Anaheim Chilies and turned out amazing. Highly recommend. Still need to try it with poblanos.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was fantastic. Added chunks of seared pork loin near the end for my meat loving husband and kept mine vegetarian. Everyone loved it. Took much longer than 15 minutes of prep time, however and my daughter and I are trained chefs. Just prepping vegetables, roasting them, cutting them, etc took quite some time but enjoyable. Also no need to remove the skins from the poblanos/vegetables, left them on for fiber and everything tasted amazing.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was restaurant quality.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Pozole verde is traditionally made with diced chicken. If you want to keep it vegetarian, I suggest adding white beans like cannellini. Also, this dish was traditionally made with dried hominy, called...pozole. Cooking it is similar to cooking dried beans, with a presoak and long cooking times. Using canned hominy is a lot faster. However, it is packed in water and gets a bit soggy. Drain it, pat dry, and toast it in a 425º oven 15 minutes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There are so many pozole recipes.  For me, within the Mexican tifecta of red, white and green pozoles, the true pozole verde from Guerrero uses the beautifuly aromatic Hoja Santa which adds a anise-fennel like aroma to the broth.  All over the state, on Thursdays, pozolerias pop up everywhere to serve it.  Yum!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: No instructions on what to do with cabbage. Thinly slice??

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Recipe states: shredded.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Jonathan Gage

Shredded, it’s a garnish, I am Mexican and that’s how it’s supposed to be served, enjoy!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Shred and use on top as a garnish. See picture.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This soup is delicious! The only change we made was to omit the jalapeno. We made half the recipe and still had enough for the two of us for dinner and lunch.  I highly recommend.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Watch the amount of lime. It’s a little too acidic with an entire lime and all the tomatillos. I put in a teaspoon of sugar to tame it a bit.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: At the risk of being one of those NYT cooking commenters who totally changes the recipe... Any reason why you couldn't just sautee the veggies and use an immersion blender? Roasting first makes me waaay less likely to make this, particularly during the week.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've found hominy in small Filipino groceries in Nova Scotia so I have to think BC has something similar.  It is essential to Pozole.  Call around to small ethnic groceries and I am sure you can find it because you will never see it in Sobey's et. al.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: walmart canada has Hominy....try superstore too

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I really can't handle cilantro (yes. I have that genetic marker -- it is not a matter of learning to like it). What substitutes for that would work in the broth the best?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
To answer Justine, below, I came across this under the heading of substitutes for hominy: "Polenta is very similar to hominy and can be used as a substitute in cooking. Use polenta as a breakfast or side dish.

It has a firm, creamy texture with a taste that is similar to light corn. In Italian cooking is usually used as a replacement for pasta sometimes. It is a suitable replacement in most recipes and you can always combine it with dumplings, tortillas, or if you are making a stew."

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: How does this end up with so much sodium per serving?  1234mg?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I halved the recipe. This allowed me to use the 4qt airfryer for easier cleanup. I layered cilantro stems, onion, tomatillos, and peppers on top (taking out the blistered poblano peppers at 12 minutes).  I also substituted 2.5 cups of chopped nopales for the kale for a heartier final product.  Absolutely delicious with queso fresco, radishes and cabbage...perfect way to eat a lot of veg!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: What can be substituted for Kale?  I detest the stuff even in soup.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Merla I would just leave it out, which is what I will do when I make his again. The taste was fine but the chewy texture of the kale almost ruined this dish for me. The hot spicy, tangy broth was perfect with the fresh veggies and cheese and the kale didn’t seem to belong.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Lovely. Followed exactly the recipe with additions only. Sazon, epezote, and chile powder to onion garlic mix. Fried a drained can of garbanzos to toasty brown in olive oil sprinkled with a heavy hand of smoked paprika to add a zang of wood fire. Then a shot of tequila. Well, two. One for me and one for the soup. Bueno!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
It took me 1:45 to make this dish, not 1 hour. There's a lot of chopping, peeling, roasting, processing, preparing the garnishes, etc so that although its "easy," it is involved. 
Also, I should've known better to wear gloves; all my fingers were painfully burning for a day afterwards from handling the chilis, and I had trouble taking out my contact lenses. A word to the wise!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love a version of this that, instead of kale, uses a variety of sautéed mushrooms. Perhaps a little more pork-like. And my cheater version uses Trader Joe’s green salsa instead of fresh tomatillos, still delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Nope. I made this as posted. The kale didn’t work— not a good add. The tomatillos made the soup too acidic. Not gonna make it again

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Excellent flavor made mostly  as directed. I only had two poblano chilies and it was still amazing. The tomatillos were from the garden.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: First time making any sort of pozole. Had a bag of Rancho Gordo hominy so decided to try it. Pretty much made per the recipe but food processed all the roast veg together, and used some frozen turkey stock (b/c had to use it up!). Delicious and hearty.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: easier: roast veggies, cook onions and aromatics at the same time. take out veggies and separate poblanos to steam. Add roast veggies to aromatic pot, add a little water, puree with immersion blender. then add the rest of the ingredients to the pot, with the kale at the end

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Absolutely delicious! Acidic and hearty, perfect in wintertime or anytime! We all have enhancement ideas, so here are my three:
1. Other than the jalapeño garnish, nothing in the recipe is particularly spicy. To add a little kick, had a half dozen dashes of Tabasco. 
2. Add a teaspoon of roasted chicken flavor Better Than Bouillon to the simmering soup. Adds delicious (non-vegetarian) umami!
3. Include some sautéed ground chorizo in the toppings for a hearty non-vegetarian meal-in-a-bowl.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Terrific dish. A few points: 1. If you just get most of the skin off the poblanos, you're okay. If the poblanos aren't blistered you might want to put them back in by themselves after the other veg are out, though. 2. This is more of a stew than a soup - it's very thick. I like that but some people prefer brothier pozole. 3. It's already pretty acidic. If you want to save ten seconds of slicing and fifty cents, don't bother with the extra lime at the end (but do roast the lime as instructed).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: mine came out way too tangy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Any tips for making this less acidic? We're not enjoying the flavor as is.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I don't like corn, so I made exactly as recipe but substituted Progresso canned cannelini (white beans) for posole. This was delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Don't see how this is rated 4  stars when it must be doctored up significantly to be somewhat palatable.   I added 1/2 cup of bulgar and 4 cups  of vegetable broth and it was still too acidic.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this tonight and while it was pretty good, the stated cooking time needs to be adjusted to more like 2 hours total time: even just using the math stated in the recipe... in step 4, you roast for 25 to 30 minutes, then you do a bunch of steps (which take time) and then in step 10 you simmer for 20 to 30 minutes.  Also, roast the poblano peppers separate, no way those pepper skins could get roasted enough to come off all crowded onto one pan.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made it, by the recipe, and it was an absolute hit at my house. Made enough for two hearty, satisfying meals for three hungry adults. My husband didn't miss the meat at all! It was as delicious as advertised.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Listening to the other commenters who said the pozole was too sour, I did not add any lime or liquid from the pan. It was still sour but enjoyable.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hominy in Canada - try Walmart if you can't find a Latino grocery store.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was pretty good, but the one whole lime squeezed into the mix was serious acid overkill. I'd use half, or maybe just a small squeeze and serve slices at the table for individual taste. Also not sure you need the second can of hominy. But it's a good recipe overall. We added chopped radishes and cilantro, with a spring salad with avocado. The roasting vegetables worked really well.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I think it’s just from having a cold for months, but I thought I needed chicken and chicken stock in here. Also, I didn’t have a lime to roast, so I had to just use lime juice. Next time, I’ll make it correctly because it was really good even with the I’ll-advised substitutions.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Justine - Walmart appears to carry hominy. Did you check there?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I added chicken- browning it first then adding it to the step where you add the broth. I also didn't have enough tomatillos so I roasted some cherry tomatoes and regular tomatoes as well. It was delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Added a couple extra tablespoons of lime juice at the end for an extra zing and accidentally roasted birth, but otherwise

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Would this freeze well?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @KS I have not yet made this recipe, but the salsa verde (the roasted and puréed veg mix, freezes brilliantly.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: 8/21/25 - This is so healthy and EXCELLENT! I live in South Korean where some of these ingredients are not available. I couldn't get poblanos, so I used green bell peppers- still great. In an attempt to make it "lighter" reduced the oil by about half, but it was a bit too sour. I also can not get fresh tomatillos, so I used two 28oz can of tomatillos, drained. ABSOLUTLEY STUNNING! MAKE IT! :)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made my own hominy from dried heirloom corn (Hopi Blue) and it was a huge flavor payoff.  A fragrant, toasty concentrated corn aroma and flavor. It's not technically difficult, but it is time consuming.  You just need dried corn, water, and pickling lime, and follow a recipe for nixtamal/nixtamalized corn.  I'll never go back to canned hominy, ever.  I also grilled the vegetables over a fire (because I already had the grill going) and added leftover carnitas meat to this.  Delish.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For me, this dish got even better as it sat in the fridge as leftovers!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Not a lot of liquid — more like a stew. Was also very salty. I think with a few modifications it would be very good.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Weeknight version: use an instant pot for sautéing everything, substitute small jar of Herdez green salsa for tomatillos, pressure cook 10 minutes and when done add kale to soften. Non-vegetarian version: add boneless/skinless chicken thighs to pressure cooker. :)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Substituted white beans / cannellini since I couldn’t find hominy at my local grocer. Turned out well!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Added two cans of beans and used 1lv swish chard instead of kale and no cumin!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Also.. added 3 T total for oil. Plenty. 13 WW pts per pot! And added corn too

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I was excited to try this recipe, as it

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This posted before I could finish it - oops! Came back to a regular keyboard today rather than my phone and finished my comment.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly. IMO not worth the time and effort, but maybe it’s just me. I do like pozole and have been cutting meat but this just didn’t work for me.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I was so excited when I was smelling the vegetables in the oven.  But I would have to say that the Kale made this so bitter and I was so disappointed…

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: 25 minutes was too long to roast the vegetables. The pan was scorched earth and I lost all the juice from the tomatillos and lime.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Holy smokes this was so acidic it made my toes curl. I followed the recipe carefully because l typically make posle with pork. I think there must be a season for tomatillos just like tomatoes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Love this recipe it’s sooo yummy. I will say it always takes longer than an hour for me, it’s a pretty labor intensive meal. But so worth it. I enjoy the craft of it anyway

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is a wonderful recipe, but the prep took me nearly an hour. It requires a lot of chopping and dicing. I loved it but will be making it again only when I have time to spare.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: How this is a five star recipe is beyond me. It takes an hour just to wash and prep the vegetables. You’ll need a slow Sunday to pull it together. I added a tablespoon of honey to counteract the sourness at the end. I also put in more water, because it was very thick. I’m no expert in pozole, but I did not like this and would not make it again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: As others have noted, this is far too acidic, even when generously salted. Definitely leave out the roasted lime, and check the acidity of your roasted tomatillos before adding all of them to the pot.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Subbed the jalepeno and poblano Chiles for leftover salsa I made from Pasilla and guajillo Chiles. Was great

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Can this be made a day ahead of time?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Does anyone know how this recipe could be done in a (small) instapot?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We left out the kale and didn't successfully pull all the clear outer skin off the poblanos. Still SOOOOO good! Will be making this again, for sure. Enjoy w tortilla chips.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is delicious. I served it with the NYT Jicama Salad and they went great together!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I was excited to make this in rainy California but the acidity of the lime and tomatillos made it impossible to eat. Even with a teaspoon of baking soda to try to neutralize the acid (good organic chemistry work up skills), we still couldn’t force ourselves to eat it. Adding the lime later should be in the “helpful” comments! I very rarely compost a recipe, but I did today.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Decided to make this when I ran across a package of Rancho Gordo hominy in a local grocery store. Followed Rancho Gordo's prep instructions for hominy. I did everything else according to the recipe, except roasting the tomatillos (did roast all other veggies). I couldn't find tomatillos that looked even remotely ripe at this time of year (February in CA).  I happened to have a mostly-full bottle of Herdez roasted green chile salsa in the fridge, so I used that. Turned out really delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Kudos to the team of sous chefs sof a 4 star Michelin restaurant that can prepare this in 15 minutes.  There is no way that the rest of us can accomplish that task.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In Mexican groceries and markets in California they usually already have the nixtamalized, and cooked hominy for sale.  This simplifies the process of making pozole a lot!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I live in New Mexico and the difference between canned hominy and soaked and cooked pozole is huge. I would never make Pozole with canned. The rest of the recipe looks good.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
So, yes, this is a DELICIOUS soup. Even if all of the veggies were prepped and chopped, the prep would still be more than 15 minutes. 

I started making this at 5pm sharp. It was on the table at 6:45! That’s a lot of time for…soup.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I loved this! I added roasted chicken since we're not vegetarian and it was wonderful. The roasted veggies and drippings make this! the broth was fantastic. I like cumin so I used it and I'm glad I did.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Why not roast all the garlic with the veggies in the beginning and save some for the soup as the veg come out of the oven? I blitzed the roasted poblanos with the rest of the salsa verde because I didn't care for larger chunks of pepper. This is a Rancho Gordo quadruple threat if you have access to their pozole, oregano indio, NM red chile powder (4x this:1x cumin), AND a bean... the RG buckeye bean and broth added flavor, depth, and nutrition. Love this as a playful base, as suggested :)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is delicious. Made a second version where I added pork tenderloin (I seared cubes with the onion and garlic, then braised it all in an additional 4 cups of broth; followed all other steps). Vegetarian recipes often have such well-developed flavors - and so adding pork was just a bonus layer of flavor (and added lean protein). This pork version was pretty darn good - family loved it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Came out great. I put in a lot more hominy and i think it benefited from the addition. Loved the roasted lime flavor and great idea to roast the cilantro stems. I didnt bother to skin the poblanos. Didn’t even notice that difference.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Good recipe, but not "easy and efficiently made".

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I was not successful with this dish. The cilantro stems were a disaster ... they came out all limpy from the oven and resisted the blade of the food processor ... they were an ugly-colored stringy mess that needed to be picked out of the dish. The kale was also a big fail for me. I did not have access to Lacinto kale and used "regular" kale, which cooked to an unappealing color and lent an unpleasant bitter taste to the dish. I struggled to peel the poblano peppers. :(

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I add roasted ground pepitas to mine, gives it an earthy flavor.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Typically the pozole i have had is quite brothy and the thickness comes more from hominy and protein in it. I think this has way too much of the tomatillo salsa added and was way too tangy. It feels like you are just eating a bowl of tomatillo salsa. I would next time half the amount of tomatillo salsa added

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: As a native New Mexican, I’ve made biscochitos every Christmas for the past 45 years. I use the Public Service Company of NM (PNM) recipe. No orange. No masa. I did make the inauthentic move to butter to make them vegetarian-friendly. I agree that these are often skipped over on the cookie plate at parties (especially when we lived out of state) - my family is thrilled - more for us!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @LA woman totally agree with no masa! And I also sub for the original lard (heck, even my mom did). However, since I never have brandy in the house, I often use orange juice for the liquid.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In the first sentence (above) of Ms. Donovan’s introduction to this cookie recipe, the words “New Mexico” are underlined. Click on the it and ypu will be taken to the official New Mexico State Cookie recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You would be hard pressed to find a New Mexican home that didn't a have a platter of these alongside a stack of tamales and a pot of posole around Christmas. Lard is sold by the tubful in all our grocery stores for this reason, but I don't mind the substitution of butter to make these more accessible. The cornmeal and orange though are hard "no"s. Some people really don't like the anise in these cookies, and to those people I'd offer a hug while I ate their share.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Says it makes 8-12 servings. What is a serving size? I normally see cookie recipes with number of cookies made and a cookie or 2 seems like a recommended serving. Does this only make 2 dozen cookies?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Friends although this is a good cookie. This is not a Biscochitos

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Excellent cookies, but I changed a bit on my second batch -  roll, cut, add cinnamon sugar while they are at room temperature - freeze then bake.    The cinnamon sugar has a hard time forming a nice coat if you add to the frozen dough, so it does not look  nearly as good. Highly recommend this recipe, though, a true winner!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Also a native New Mexican, and although this recipe isn't the most traditional, I would definitely want to try it. I know many families put their own spin on the biscochito; I've known some who add rum, or red wine to theirs. My grandmother preferred vegetable shortening to lard. Some  prefer the traditional anise seeds, while others may opt for the extract (my mother does both). One of my favorite things about food is the way everyone adds their own touch to a common recipe!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Someone says in the notes that fennel and anise are the same seed- they are NOT! Please do not substitute fennel for anise.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Lauri I made both versions because at first I only had fennel. Strangely I actually preferred the fennel seed.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Substitute for ground anise seeds. Fennel? Star anise? I have never heard of anise in the UK lol

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Name required- Fennel would be an ok substitute

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Name required,  you could also try liquid replacements such as Anisette, Pastis, Ouzo, Galliano, or Sambuca.  Or if you decide to try the star anise, cut the amount in half.  It will be more herbaceous with star anise.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Fennel IS anise, and the liquid options are made from the said plant. Seeds are widely available in south Asian and middle Eastern stores.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I thoroughly enjoyed the story behind the recipe.    
 The cookies are good if you like a plain, not very sweet cookie with tea. The prep is way more than 10 minutes.   Between weighing, beating , chilling,  toasting seeds, rolling out, and cutting out, I  spent well over an hour before the first batch went in the oven. The recipe made 36 cookies.  
They were crispy; I used lard as the recipe suggested.  If you don’t have anise seeds I would suggest using almond or anise extract .

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
2 cups lard
1 cup sugar
1 egg yolk
1 teaspoon aniseed
6 cups flour
1 teaspoon salt
2 teaspoons baking powder
½ /2- ¾ cup water
½ cup sugar, for topping
½ teaspoon ground cinnamon, for topping
Stir in just enough water to hold dough together.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: As many others have noted this is not the traditional recipe. If you want traditional use the one linked to in the note on the NM state website, it’s the same as what’s in the 1978 copy of New Mexico Magazine Best of NM Kitchens cookbook that I snagged from my parents. For that recipe, if you don’t have anise seeds, you can easily substitute some anise extract (if I remember I also throw in some vanilla extract too). I’ll also use whatever plain, un-peaty whiskey I have if I don’t have brandy on hand (you want some of those caramel, vanilla flavors).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious twist on the classic, which I have had many times in NM. I do not eat lard and I loved the addition of the corn meal and the orange. Extremely tasty. 10/10.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made with one change (1/2 crisco, 1/2 butter). Delicious, super interesting cookie that even my Colombian/Lebanese husband likes. Toddler approved as well. Layers of flavors that keep on giving.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I'm from Arizona,  my mom from Sonora.  These are called bizcochuelos here.  Her recipe calls for lard, crushed anise seeds, and anise extract.  She rolls about a tablespoon of dough into a thin log and meets the ends in a circle.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There is a link to the authentic New Mexico recipe in the text that precedes Lisa Donovan's recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: New Mexican biscochitos do not use corn meal. Also I make the dough the day before (or several days before and refrigerate) in a ball, not sure what the advantage of rolling it out before cooking is. Then I take out portions and warm up on the counter for 20 min or so roll it out, cut the shapes, bake and then dip in cinnamon and sugar while still warm…. Doesn’t matter if you use butter/lard/shortening—the key is to whip it up with a mixer before you add the sugar.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I grew up with Italian Christmas cookies, which are drier and less sweet— essentially made for dunking—than what a lot of people expect, so I was excited to try these. I followed the recipe exactly and they turned out perfectly. I found the dough very easy to work with. Half the recipe (stuck the other half in the freezer) made 23 decent-sized stars and half-moons. Love them, but if you find biscotti and the like “too dry,” this is not your cookie.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Follow-up note: dough does not freeze well.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I always made the PNM recipe until I tasted my friend Christine Trujillo's recipe.  Leave out any liquid, and lightly crush the anise seed.  Certainly no masa.  It makes for a more shortbread texture.  Just made 4 dozen today!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @LoriB - Hi! Sounds delicious! Would you please post the recipe?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I'm typically not an anise-flavor lover, but these are wonderful. The masa adds nice texture. They are not -- as many commentators have said -- traditional biscochitos -- but the author has created a biscochitos-ish new recipe that is a keeper.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious!  Somewhat fragile, as I discovered when I tried to decorate them a little.  Just laid a stencil of a basketweave design on the cookie and sprayed a bit of edible luster.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
We use Grandma Salazar’s more-than-hundred-year-old recipe.
You need lard and whiskey (for flavor and texture). No masa or orange. 
Also, FWIW, she spelled it bizcochito. 
We only make them at Christmas, and it’s not Christmas without them.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hi Mrs. Lopez - in a huge NYC law firm, Italian legal sec. made twice yearly (b-day/x-mas) for her crabby old boss.  Boss and legal sec. edged up to my desk one day and in a whisper said, "Hey, wanna try the best cookie ever?"  Melt in your mouth. They were rolled out, and shaped by hand into a fleur-de-lis, as instructed by legal sec.'s Italian grandma.  Which is why I assumed these were Italian!  Lard was the key ingredient, brandy and anise seeds.  No masa or orange.  Utterly delish.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@mrs. lopez

Can you share the recipe? I’ve been looking for an authentic recipe for decades. I’m from Juarez and we had a family friend that made these. I had no idea they were traditionally New Mexican. She always made them in stars and moon shapes. I don’t remember them tasting like orange.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious. I just ate one with a bite of dark chocolate and it was divine. Next time I will try dipping them in chocolate and then letting that set after they’re done.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: These cookies were a fail.  Dry and tasteless.  The recipe for the New Mexico version has twice amount of fat (lard in that recipe).  If you want to give these a try, I recommend doubling the amount of butter.  Now, what should I do with a batch of cookies no one wants to eat?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I only have Diamond Crystal kosher salt. I know it is different from Morton, but I forget in which direction. How much should I use? (Important: I loathe salty cookies.)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Never mind, I found it: Diamond Crystal = 2 X Morton, so 1 teaspoon (or less) Diamond Crystal salt.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I just made this recipe and I have never had this or the original cookie before, but am not a fan of the masa, I don't think it serves a purpose in flavor or texture.  I won't be making these again. I think I may try the original recipe to have a comparison.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Yum I had excess masa to use so this was appealing and also forgiving- reminded me of snickerdoodles.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Just baked off a few to test for my cookie boxes and I am wow-ed! The subtle corn flavor from the masa and delicate orange notes with the anise is great! I know some are saying these aren’t the traditional ones- but I am glad I stuck with this recipe! I didn’t have the liquor to add to the dough so I just used half n half to help the dough come together. Yummmy!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Anise is common in NM groceries, now in Kentucky it is rarely found. I use shortening, no masa or orange but still add brandy. I use a cookie press to make small cookies, although the shapes blur when cooking, then roll the fresh from the oven cookies in the cinnamon sugar.  Tender and delicious

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: These were potentially good cookies. I love the anise flavor and they were nice and crisp even though I used butter rather than lard. However, the flavor is dominated by the masa and I don't like it. I considered whether to use masa or corn meal and it was the wrong decision. I may try again, with or without cornmeal, because I think they could be good.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Nancy Gertner - I used masa and did not have that problem. Perhaps the masa was old or stale?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I loved the flavor and texture of these cookies.  Even better the next day.  It is important to use the fine corn meal or you’ll have corn grain left in your mouth.
I did not cut these out with cookie cutters. I rolled the dough in a rectangular shape, refrigerated, put on a parchment lined baking sheet and sliced into diamond shapes.  I did not separate the shapes until putting them on the cooling rack.  This process made it go much quicker.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: These may not be absolutely “authentic” but the are yummy, not too  sweet, and not too heavy.  Difficult to stop eating them!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you compare this to traditional viscosities, not enough fat. I made these and it was very difficult to get the dough to form. Added extra brandy but when compared to the original this should have had twice the fat as written.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I couldn’t find anise seed, so used a lttle over a teaspoon of star anise (2 pods finely chopped in a spice mill). Wouldn’t have wanted more.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Grew up in New Mexico making my Biscos with Crisco because that's what my mother would do. I switched to lard as an adult but also made with butter which got rave reviews from other family members. I am anxious to try making these with masa which is not a thing that I know of but I am trying to use more anti-inflammatory ingredients these days of which corn flour is considered to be. However, the refined nature of masa marina is not a plus.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious! I didn’t have anise seeds so I substituted star anise. I understand from the other comments they’re not authentic at all, but I must say I enjoyed the orange and corn in these cookies. The orange added a nice flavor (taste testers thought it was lemon) and the corn gives them an extra crumbly texture which I enjoyed. To rub salt in the wound of inauthenticity we decorated these with royal icing flavored with bitters. Overall I loved these and will be making again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Left out the anise as no one in my family likes it. They were delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love a crisp, dry, not-too-sweet cookie (really!), so this really fit the bill. Also love the anise/orange flavor. Not sure I love the flavor of the masa in these...it provides a kind of strangely funky background note (imo). I would make these again. The measurements for the topping were way off for the number of cookies. 1/2 cup of sugar? If you used 1/4 cup, you'd still have some left over. Also, # of servings is meaningless in a cookie recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Really liked the look of this recipe since I bake with masa for its gluten-free value though I did use the wheat flour this time. But in step one I got the idea we could use alternatives to a standing mixer, which I don't have. Please don't try this without one; the dough becomes so stff and crumbly I had to hand knead it for each cookie! An hour? Say rather an afternoon spilling into evening. But I'll experiment to make this in gluten free mode; thank you all for the other recipes!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Anyone have any suggestions for wheat flour substitutions? I can't have regular flour but these cookies sound amazing.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Ok. Make thinner.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I really like these cookies. The anise and orange flavors are there but subtle enough not to be overwhelming.  The only issue I found is that the dough was really just a little bit dry. Even though the rum and brandy are marked as optional, I think the dough really needs that extra 2 tablespoons of liquid.  Next time I will try adding some brandy or other liquid.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I was excited to try these cookies as I love the flavor of anise!   I only had ground anise so toasted that and used in equal measure to the seeds.   Perhaps the ground had already lost flavor compared to seeds as the orange dominated.   It’s a nice cookie, but perhaps a little too plain for me!   So on one tray, I added a simple drizzle of icing and then the sprinkling of cinnamon sugar on top and that provided a nice variation.   Thanks for the recipe and the look at a culture and tradition different than mine!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: These are not biscochitos. The author should clarify that these are far from traditional. They would disappoint any New Mexican.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am from Europe so I am not very familiar with masa - is the yellow masa in this recipe masa harina or something else? And if it is something else, can I use masa harina in its place? thanks!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The Golden Crown Panadería in Albuquerque makes blue corn bizcochitos that are my favorite. And I have a bag of blue corn masa. I’m going to try it. Doesn’t have to be traditional!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: No mass or cornmeal please, not in biscochos!  And you must use lard!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: What? No lard in biscochos? Masa, no way! Put in the lard and take out the masa.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I have made these cookies as hostess gifts all over the world.  They are uniformly loved.  Even the French devoured them!  My favorite recipe by far is from the Santa Fe, NM, Pink Adobe restaurant/cookbook (sadly closed/out of print).  Easiest recipe ever.  I substitute lard for butter and whip the butter/sugar/egg until really light and fluffy.

Pink Adobe/Rosalea Murphy
2 cups lard (I use butter)
1 cup sugar
1 egg yolk
1 tsp aniseed
6 cups flour
1 tsp kosher salt
2 tsp baking powder
1/2-3/4 cup water
1/2 cup sugar for topping
1/2 tsp ground cinnamon for topping
Preheat oven to 350.  Cream lard/butter, sugar and yolk together in mixer till light and fluffy.  Stir in aniseed.  Shift together, flower salt and baking powder and stir into creamed mixture. Stir in just enough water to make mixture hold together. Need until well mixed. On a flowered board, roll dough to 1/2 inch thickness. Cut into fancy shapes with cookie cutters. Mixed together the 1/2 cup of sugar and the cinnamon and sprinkle over each cookie. On an ungreased cookie sheet bake for approximately 10 minutes until nicely browned.  Enjoy!!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I tried both butter and vegetable shortening. the shortening version came out much crispier, and in my opinion, better; however, the chilling and rolling out the dough was much more difficult. I am not sure the chilling is necessary if you use shortening.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Telling bakers to put a 9” springform pan in the freezer assumes that readers have a large or sparsely filled freezer. I don’t. So, I looked up other gâteau recipes here. Dorrie Greenspan has you place it in the refrigerator…just for a longer time. Fridges generally have more space than freezers. Just putting that out there, for those who don’t own SubZero fridges or deep freezers.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Peaches

A cooler with ice will work

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Bethlur freezer has no ice cube maker, maker, so there you go. In fact, ice cubes mice cubes and such are hard to get by here in Europe. But I’m trying.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Can this gateau be made using a pan other than a springform?

Also, a photo that better shows what the filling looks like would be helpful.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I have eaten Gateau Nana starting when I was a small child in South Louisiana . It is delicious with a cup of strong coffee in the afternoon or served for dessert after a wonderful meal. This is the exact recipe that my grandmother used. You can’t go wrong with this .

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Mary Maybe a replacement for the traditional (Kari) Pecan Pie at Thanksgiving?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Mary I remember purchasing these cakes at the Kitchen Store in Grand Coteau, LA. I have looked for a similar recipe ever since moving away. So happy to have found this!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Reminds me of the Swiss Bündner Nusstorte. The most delicious treat in the snowy alps with a cup of tea in the afternoon.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Now that I’ve had slice after slice each time  I pass by the cake plate in which it rests ( day 2 ) each bite is a revelation. The recipe was not perfectly written but I know enough about baking to have traveled its pitfalls of prep successfully. Note that I forgot to use superfine sugar and used ordinary sugar. If you don’t have superfine that’s ok. As for “raw” pecans I’m not sure what those are except I searched for them at the supermarket and found a a box that said raw. I only use local farm eggs (@$7/dozen )  I get especially for my baking and and barely have any left. I’m not sure if I’ll make it again but relish every leftover morsel.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @john golden how would you describe the consistency of the crust? Is it a cake or a rough puff?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @john golden in my experience (growing up in a pecan growing region), the default for pecans (sold as shelled pecans) is raw. Roasted/toasted and/or salted would appear on the label.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @john golden raw just means not toasted. Nothing more.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Love this recipe. Can I freeze this cake after baking and before the egg wash?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I just finished preparing this. What a production!  Follow directions carefully. Dealing with the dough is tricky rolling out. It should be refrigerated for a least 30 minutes. But it takes about that time to prepare the filling while the dough chills. I will give more details once it’s ready to serve

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Thinking of making this with hazelnuts; any thoughts/caveats? Should I toast them lightly first? Thanks for any insight.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I'm thinking the same thing. Why not toast the nuts for a nuttier flavor?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Ellen Tabor I always toast nuts agree it brings out the flavor

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Made my filling with a combination of walnuts and pecans and it was superb!

For the flour portion of the cake, I substituted one cup of almond flour for one cup of flour to achieve a different texture and taste. 

Instead of adding an egg yolk to the nut mixture, I added some of the extra egg whites, and it turned out to be great. Also, for the egg wash, I used some of the egg whites instead of another egg yolk, combined it with the cream, and it worked out perfectly.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made for dessert and it didn’t hit the right dessert notes for my palate. Maybe because no one at our table drinks coffee with dessert? Ate the next morning with tea/coffee and enjoyed it. It was fairly dry- more of a scone than pie/cake. Made as written and it was plenty salty, I’m glad I used unsalted butter and did not roast/salt the pecans in advance. I would make again if I was hosting a breakfast or afternoon tea.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Love this! Not overly sweet. So tender and rich. I halved the recipe and used a 7" springform pan and just one whole egg in the filling,  which worked well. Baked 45 minutes. I should've put the pan on a cookie sheet as it leaked (butter I think)and smoked up the kitchen.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this as written, making the crust and filling the day before baking.  Work with the dough when it is cold but malleable.  I used a 9 " tart pan with removable bottom and no parchment paper.  I cut down on the salt a little bit in the filing, but no other changes.  After brushing the top with egg wash, do a decorative pattern and make a small whole in the center of the crust and a few slits in the top before baking to prevent cracking.  Came out beautiful and tasty after about 35 minutes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I have made pie crusts with Bob's Red Mill GF flour mix, and have found that it does work. It's difficult to work *with*, however, falls apart and crumbles while trying to move it to the pan. I've found it more suitable for single-crust pies. The bottom crust is not so difficult, as it can be pieced back together (because it *will* crumble while transferring) once in the pan. For the top crust, perhaps using the dough as a streusel would work best with a GF flour dough.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this tonight. I wonder if the saltiness is due to using table salt instead of kosher salt. Used table salt and dialed it back by nearly half the kosher salt rec. Not too salty. My top definitely cracked. I think, if I make this again (which will depend on whether or not my suegra likes it), I will build a bit of room in the crust on top because the filling expanded and cracked the crust. Still lovely and rustic. The house smelled like heaven. Also, I took a generous sheet of parchment paper and crumpled it up in a tight ball before smoothing into and over the sides of the pan. This way, no butter leakage. The pattern on the top really emerged in the final 10 minutes of baking. Cute.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this exactly as written and it was excellent. I rolled the dough for the top layer between two sheets of parchments and chilled it until it was firm and it was easy to work with — no tearing or falling apart. I also served it with a rhubarb compote, but I could see it working well as a backdrop to all sorts of berries and fruit. I found the recipe to be very straightforward and well worth the effort.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If we were to assume for a moment that some NYT readers live in NYC and don't have counter- or cupboard space for a stand mixer, what is the alternative here? Is by hand going still going to work at Step 3 and beyond?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Ava, Brooklyn You can always knead by hand or you can buy a dough hook attachment for a traditional hand mixer.  I did that before my wife bought me a stand mixer for Christmas and it worked fine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Ava, Brooklyn: You can make this by just like people make challah by hand.  The dough will be sticky at first but as you knead the dough the loaf will come together. You can need on a counter if you have the space or you can need in a bowl. You can do a search for kneading techniques. Everyone has their favorites. I have used both coil folds, slap and fold, and used a scraper to knead dough in a bowl to make challah. The differences are minimal. This recipe uses 5 eggs for two loafs. The recipe I use for challah uses 2 eggs, 3 egg yolks and oil for one large or two smaller loafs. In fact I use extra large eggs even though the recipe calls for large eggs. It’s is as sticky as this dough. Mixing and kneading in a bowl keeps the mess contained and keeps the area much cleaner. The method of kneading isn’t important.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Check Julia Child's "Mastering the Art of French Cooking", vol 2.   Very few people had stand mixers in 1961...

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am very surprised the recipe calls for salted butter. There is no universal standard for the amount of salt in salted butter and it varies between brands. Recipes using unsalted butter will have a specific amount of salt to add precisely to control its effects on yeast. Maybe this recipe is forgiving enough? But generally, when using unsalted butter, add 1/4 tsp salt for every 1/2 cup of unsalted butter that you need.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Fritz Kosher salt?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Natasha
Yes, kosher salt tends to be less salty for the same volume. Diamond kosher salt is the least salty, so it’s harder to over-salt with.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Note: kosher salt brands are not the same. Diamond and Morton's flakes are different so they're not interchangeable by volume, only by weight. If a recipe calls specifically for Diamond and you use Morton's (or table), you need to use half the amount by weight. 1 Tbsp Diamond Crystal kosher = 8 gm 1 Tbsp Morton's Kosher = 16 gm https://www.kingarthurbaking.com/learn/ingredient-weight-chart https://www.kingarthurbaking.com/blog/2020/07/29/why-is-salt-important-in-yeast-bread

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@FRITZ
Based on the chart you provided, 1 1/4 t of Diamond salt would be 3.3 gram.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: THis makes a terrific duo of loaves, but I have to say, as a person who bakes a good deal and makes several types of bread with some regularity, this is the most unusual bread dough I have ever made.  Twice I was sure I had done something terribly wrong, but it completely works.  Be patient, esp. at the last step of mixing when you have to beat in all of that butter - it takes awhile but it works!  Also - makes great French toast!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Slavin I am so glad to read your comment. I’m making this bread today for a second time because I threw yesterday’s dough out. I was absolutely certain I’d done something wrong. I wish the recipe had addressed the dough in the first stage. It does say “a soft, loose dough” but it doesn’t say this dough will still lay in your bowl and will not be in a ball! Cannot tell you how glad I was to read you too thought you had done something wrong. I’m at that stage of mixing as I write so I shall carry on! Thanks for those dose of courage this morning. I needed that.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Slavin Same panic/confusion here. Doing this with European ingredients (living in Switzerland) my dough turned out almost liquid in the first attempt. I increased the flour to > 500 g which changed the dough from „liquid“ to a ridiculously sticky lump. I still put it into the fridge over night and used a lot of flour to coax it into the loaf pans. The brioche turned out amazing! 
Just went through a second attempt today using 450 g of flour —> great, great, great! I think I will settle for 475 g and ignore the „tacky but not sticky“.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Tony happy to see your comment. I recently made another NYT’s loaf, similar/enriched etc. I always use the metric conversion option & like you I was absolutely stumped when after adding the 3-4 cups / 360-480g  I STILL had a just past liquid looking dough. I almost threw it out. I reviewed & reviewed the recipe thinking I must have spaced & added extra liquid somewhere. I had not. THEN I thought, the culprit must be the flour. I recently got a new organic APF, typically here in the US it’s Bobs Red Mill or King Arthur if you’re sourcing organic/GMO free. I busted my scale back out,  fluffed this new APF up a bit to get as accurate of a ‘scoop & sweep’  cup as I could AND after measuring 3 cups the weight came in between 160-170g. I went with 160g meaning I was a full cup short based on the recommend 360g/ 3 cup minimum flour needed for this bread. Every internet baking site has APF weighing in between 120-135g max. Once I added in the additional flour, after a good rest as I was figuring this out it came together & the bread was delicious. Lesson for me & heads up to all - if you switch your flours up do a base weight test to avoid massive frustration!!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Tacky is the back of a post-it note. When you lightly press into the dough, it should spring back. Sticky is clinging to your hands.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @teresa I think because the loaves are shaped and in the pans with slings and the act of removing them from the pan and then putting them back in to proof and bake would be too damaging or deflating. If you were going to need your pans, I think you could freeze them as directed and when frozen, remove them from the pans and wrap well. Then when you are ready to proof, re-insert them and carry on.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am old so I have old cookbooks. I also have my mother's ancient cookbook. My cookbook never recommends using a dough hook, although I think they were invented my then, on tender breads like Challah and Brioche. My mother's cookbook was long before stand mixers were a thing, and hand mixers were rare. They all give very elaborate directions for hand kneading. One points out how you can work our your frustrations by kneading dough. I try to cook with love so I don't do that often.

commentBody: For those of you asking: A good rule of thumb when it comes to calculating salt in bread formulas is 1.5-2% of the flour weight. Which in this case would be 6-8g.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Jackie: You can use honey instead of sugar but you probably need to reduce the amount of honey (honey is sweeter) and the amount of milk as honey is a liquid. You also might lower the temperature of the oven or tent the bread with aluminum foil (either side up) half way through cooking the bread so that the bread won’t burn). Of course you can do the simple swap of equal quantities and see how you like the result.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Can you use honey only?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Honey will brown quicker than sugar so using all honey may make your bread brown quicker and can even scorch it. And in a 400 degree oven, it's likely to get very brown.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love the article associated with this recipe! It is especially true of clafoutis— as this recipe and the alternatives you can find on-line and in French cookbooks will attest. It’s very flexible. I am giving only three stars because the amount of sugar is about twice what the French recipes call for and four times what I use (two tablespoons).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Way too much sugar for me. I bake my fresh cherry clafoutis (10 servings) with a total of 60 grams of sugar and whole milk instead of heavy cream.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This recipe shows a clafoutis with pretty skimpy fruit, unlike other Times recipes.  I (roughly) doubled the fruit and mixed peaches and nectarines, because that’s what I had. I baked slightly longer, because we like a cakey texture. Also, I used peach brandy, which gave it a nice spark.

As the notes say, you can use any fruit, and mixtures of fruit, like peach/blueberry and plum/apricot, even mango/strawberry. The same goes for the lovely Times strawberry spoon cake, and many others.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I have tried to bake a mango pie. Baked or cooked mangos turn white and lose their flavor. I now only use mangos in recipes that maintain the mangos as fresh or frozen.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In the spirit of this being a learning experiment, can anyone tell me why this recipe specifically calls for salted butter (vs. unsalted) to prep the pan? I can’t imagine it matters… but maybe it does.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Jim, I don’t have a scientific explanation but  I find a touch of salt can make some dessert great by adding a contrast with the sweetness of the dish. But then I’m from the west of France and there salted butter is pretty much the only butter…

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Why a nut flour? I'm allergic to almonds, and am not exactly going to out to my local coop (I live in rural VT) to ask for hazelnut flour. I thought this was supposed to be a homey dish. I'll be using all flour -- which is the way any French cook I know would make it. What silliness!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Thanks to those of you who suggested more fruit and less sugar. If fruit is ripe, there is no need for sugar that masks the flavor of the fruit!

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe is perfect as is. Some say there is too much sugar. I do not agree. We dug in before it was completely cooled. It is so creamy and rich and the peach fruitiness was perfect. It took longer to bake than expected. It was still not set at 60 minutes. I gave it a full 70 minutes, and it could have gone longer if I wanted it more cake like. It was perfect. It was my first clafoutis but not my last. I might add some cinnamon next time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Half the sugar. Macerate the stone fruit in about a 1/4 c sugar and a 1/4 cup brandy/kirschwasswer/Grand marnier. The Julia Child recipe in “Mastering” is the best.

author: Lisa Donovan

commentBody: All regular flour should be fine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Aldi sells a pound of almond flour for under $6.00.  Once opened, store bag in a container in the refrigerator.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Great recipe and a clafoutis is a comfort-food dessert. 

Needs about 3+ C of fruit; 2 C was pretty meager on the fruit distribution. Slightly under-ripe fruit works well; perfectly ripe can be too juicy and makes the custard loose. 

Went with a generous tablespoon of Grand Marnier, which was divine! 

Looking forward to breakfast tomorrow.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’m sure this is delicious but this is NOT how clafoutis should look. I grew up in France where my mother and l made clafoutis every summer with sour cherries from our garden. There should be way more fruit than the picture suggests, and be more ‘flan-y’ than ‘cake-y’. Any sour fruit will be great for a clafoutis. The word, fwiw, is said to come from the Occitan ‘claffir’ which means ‘to fill’.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: ½ the sugar, twice the fruit ... Excellent.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was my first clafoutis in a few years (it used to be my go-to puddings) and I regret following this recipe.  The excessive sugar quantities and unnecessary addition of almond flour are overwhelming and kill the delicious sharpness of the fruit, instead of offering a neutral background in contrast.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love that this uses almond flour because I also use a 1 for 1 gluten free flour in place of the regular flour since I have family and friends with celiac who are gluten intolerant. It works great!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am from New Orleans of French heritage. We always had a clafouti of some fruit throughout the yeay. We used much less sugar, both flours as available ( pecan flour is more predominant than almond) and more salt and butter. I made it as described and though slightly sweer, it is superb on Day 2.

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan

Then it was still somewhat liquid 3/4 of the way through the cooking time and was spilling over the sides, but the top was starting to get quite brown.  So I lowered the heat and let it cook longer.  Absolutely delicious!  Our dinner guest asked for the recipe and made it two days later.  He did have the same liquidity problem.  Perhaps both of us used very juicy fruit?  I do weigh my ingredients.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Looking forward to trying this recipe.  My comment here is about the accompanying article: right-on.  I meet people who are hesitant to bake because they worry about measuring everything precisely and following instructions exactly.  I reassure them that baking isn't nuclear science.  There's a lot of room for variation, intentional or not. It may not come out exactly the way the author's version does, but more likely than not, it'll be something you'll still want to eat.
