commentBody: Has anyone used sherry instead of rum? I have some oloroso that I will try.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I wish the author had a “Tip” about substituting regular flour for the almond/hazelnut fours. Nut allergies are common and such a tip should be a regular practice. My son, my daughter, a grandson and I are all allergic to nuts.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: this recipe is just so skimpy on fruit... it just begged for more.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was my first clafoutis, and wow, what a treat! Agree with others that this is very sweet so I will probably cut the sugar next time. Made with frozen peaches saved from summer and worked perfectly!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this in July w/fresh, dark, ripe plums from a wild tree at the edge of my yard in Portland, OR. We had it on a sunny Sunday at noontime. The “less sugar, whole milk” advice in other comments worked for me. It was delicious, delightful & satisfying in every way. I write to note that btw/getting the ingredients together, pitting the plums, measuring & mixing, baking for an hour & cooling for an hour, the whole process takes a leisurely 2.5 to 3 hours. “Cooking time: 1.5 hrs plus cooling” is pretty optimistic for the occasional home baker, in my experience. So be sure to set aside sufficient time. Totally worth it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I live in New York City and simply can't find plain simple almond flour--how nuts is that???  Any suggestions--I've tried usual supermarket chains, plus Trader Joe's, Fairway, Wegman's.  When I try to find it online there are some options but none that is stated to be simply "almond flour"--some specify for muffins or cake--can I used one of those for this recipe?  (You can probably tell I'm not a very experienced baker.......)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Bob's Red Mill brand is pretty widely available.  I use the blanched version, not the "natural" one.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Trader Joe’s has very good almond flour. Costco Alonso sells it. Bon Apetit!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hi Lois! If you can't find almond flour and have a sturdy food processor, a big bag of blanched almonds can be turned into almond flour. Just make sure you don't pulse it too much or you'll end up with almond butter. Go for a fine breadcrumb texture--a little finer than the Italian breadcrumbs you can buy at the store.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Costco sells almond flour and you can get it from ama$on as well

commentBody: Can you make this without all the heavy cream?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I make clafoutis with silken tofu (no eggs or cream) and it comes out absolutely delicious. Search for “vegan clafoutis” recipes and you’ll find some winners.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
The first time I had clafoutis was a pear clafoutis. The recipe came in an old issue of Cooking Light and I adored it. (It had a touch of cinnamon.)  Since then, I've moved on to cherry, nectarine, and even strawberry.

Clafoutis is one of my favorites!

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan

How do you get the clafoutis to look like the picture? I used firm Italian plums and followed the recipe exactly.  I had to bake it for twenty minutes more than the suggested baking time as it was  extremely liquid.  It’s a very bland brown, like an apple tartin, and nothing like the picture which looks more like a plum tart cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My experience making clafoutis with blanched almond flour  -- Trader Joes, Whole Foods, health food store -- was unsatisfactory.. The end result was slightly gritty not a smooth custard.  I use almond flour or combined for all kinds of things.. but this did not please.  I would only use wheat flour.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Excellent. It’s peaches in a custard. A keeper.
I would have like the custard to be a little bit firmer (I cooked it for 65 min) but still excellent.
Suggestions anybody on how to get the "custard" a bit firmer?

commentBody: Made this tonight and it was so delicious. BUT; I must say, contrary to the recipe notes, there ARE things easier to make than this dish.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Well, I have never made clafoutis without beating white eggs separately until stiff. I like it rather fluffy. I also used 75 grams flour + 25 grams almond flour, and a tiny amount of baking powder.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: No more clafoutis for me. Both times I’ve made one, first using Julia’s recipe and now this one, it came out with a rubbery bottom layer. I’m thinking a Bain Marie would solve the problem. The other issue with this recipe is I followed people’s suggestions to use more fruit. All the extra water left a curdled layer on top. I had to toss the whole thing.

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: 4th time I'm making this....AWESOME! I use 1 cup of cream and 3/4 milk, gluten free flour instead of regular flour. Have made it with just plums (twice), just strawberries (frozen) and today used plums and frozen pineapple. What a fun, delicious and easy recipe!

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Due to comments, I halved the sugar. I should have done what I usually do - make it as written first time around. It was a little runny, took over 75 minutes in the oven for it to set

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I read the comments and halved the sugar. Should've done what I usually do and make it as written first time around. It was too runny, took over 75 minutes in the oven to set. I will stick with MRShulman's cherry clafoutis recipe next time around.

author: Lisa Donovan

The reference to a sheet pan led me to believe that this must be baked in a dish or skillet set on a sheet pan.  Since it is quite bubbly that seems reasonable, but the baking takes forever to get the center done!
Some more specific baking instructions are needed.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody:
Very sweet story with this recipe.  

Only comparable one is my mother used to bake 5 loaves of white bread twice a week.  And if she made it on Saturdays, we could have the warm bread with butter and honey — nothing like it until reading this now.

See, we are all really alike after all.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: There's magic in that first tortilla off the comal.  And more.  In a house with multiple children, there's usually just one that stands by the cook and watches.  The author was one, as was I.  We watch, spellbound by the magic, and our reward is the first tortilla.  The other kids are all busy doing... something.  They come in later, get a tortilla, and run off again.  But we got the first tortilla - and a lifelong memory.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: @carlos my parents went to Mexico in the mid 1960s to study art and get their Masters degrees. When they returned to Canada - I was born in 68. My Mom learned to make many traditional Mexican foods from the housekeeper they had in their small town they lived in. I was an only child so / I was that lucky kid to learn also and get to eat the first one. Thank you for sharing your story. At my age - recalling something so lovely like that is precious. Gracias por la bondad y recuerdos

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: In the '70s one of the girls on my floor in the college dorm made these in an old school electric popcorn popper, the only appliance were allowed in the days before microwaves.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: These are great with out the vegetable oil added! So fluffy and light. Just let the doe sit for 20 minutes before rolling out.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: Use soft wheat flour.Lilly White or your favorite.     hard wheat flour will make your tortillas tougher it has too much gluten much love and some leaf fat “not refined “ not rendered,cooled and then grated into your flour will make your family richer;)

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: Marilyn, corn and wheat flour tortillas are not the same.  For this recipe, you need to use an all-purpose wheat flour.   I agree with Eddy that a "soft" wheat flour is excellent.  Bob's Red Mill pastry flour works well.  If you can only get King Arthur, you might use a tiny bit less flour and be careful not to work the dough too much.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: My grandma made her tortillas just like this, but with bacon fat instead of canola oil.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: @OliviaBloom this recipe came directly from my abuelita! Oil and all. :) We used lard for other things but she preferred her tortillas with oil. I tested it with lard and shortening as well. Oil was better! Not for health reasons, just total preference. You can replace the oil with lard, though, if you like. That’s the beauty of a recipe like this - meant to try as written and then adjust as you see fit. Happy cooking!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: What a wonderful story.  It painted a beautiful picture.  Being a rookie at tortillas, I was wondering if I needed any oil in the cast iron skillet or should it be dry?  I'm looking forward to giving this a try.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: No oil in the skillet.  It should be dry.  The tortilla dough should not be sticky, so it should be easy to lift with your fingers.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: Thank you for bringing back memories of my own grandmother. As a child, whenever we visited her house, I would always go straight to the bowl where she kept tortillas wrapped in a tea towel. She would warm one up directly on the gas stove, without using a comal, letting the flames lightly touch her fingers as she flipped it. Then, she would hand it to me, still piping hot, and I would slather it with butter before stuffing it in my greedy little mouth.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: Reading this unlocked a memory while we were living outside Atlanta in the mid 60s when my dad did a summer semester at Emory. My mother  put tortillas on the grill and basted them with butter. Simply delicious. I can’t wait to make these! Thank you for sharing.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: Oh my. My grandma's tortilla's were identical, which may not sound noteworthy, but it is. A little to fluffy for a burrito, more of a bean spoon or plate mop, and slightly burnt spots but the rest of the tortilla cooked perfectly. The burnt added just a hint of good bitterness. I've never had a tortilla as perfect.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: I use warm tap water with the salt fully dissolved in the water. You can do this with or without the baking powder. Can use most fats: lard, butter, olive oil.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody:
I can’t wait to try these after recently new habit making naan flatbreads. 

Anyone got a source or recipe for what sounds like a snacking addition to honey, butter, and salt - pickled chile de árbol? 

Search not showing a prepared option or recipe. I do see they’re more akin to habanero hot than jalapeño, so it may at be more than I can enjoy.  But I’d love to try it!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: @McinDC hi! Thanks for the lovely note - you’re going to want to work on a dry, not oiled comal. While a good seasoned iron is always helpful and delicious, it’s not imperative. To keep your cast iron or comal seasoned, it’s good practice to wipe it with neutral oil and let it sit in a warm oven (my practice is that I wipe my comal with a little oil and throw it in an oven that has slightly cooled off from baking something - it’s the perfect temp and you can let it rest as the oven cools.)

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: One of the very last things my husband's grandmother was excited to do- seated in her bed, supported by pillows- was to roll out some tortillas. She was in her late 90's, one of 12 children born in NM ca 1905. I remember her beautifully gnarled hands, still able to roll out the dough with her special small rolling pin, so pleased to be able to "help" my mother in law (whom she lived with till the end), with dinner.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody:
These were great! I have been looking for substitute tortillas since Trader Joe's Truly Handmade tortillas disappeared.  With a little more practice on part, I think these will be perfect! 
I've tried the other two tortilla recipes on the site  but these are more what I've been looking for in that they are a bit thicker.

author: Lisa Donovan

commentBody: For another option, you can cover 1/2 of a tortilla with sliced or shredded cheese, then fold the other half over the cheese. Then heat both sides to melt the cheese inside.

author: Lisa Donovan

commentBody: This is the recipe my grandmother from New Mexico made, although she always used regular table salt rather than kosher salt.  We always ate them, thick and warm, with red chile sauce. author: Lisa Donovan

commentBody: Lisa Donovan, what a lovely story you've shared with us.  Thank you.  And I'm going to make your family recipe soon.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: I love flour tortillas, although corn tortillas seem to be more traditionally Mexican. Flour tortillas are popular in the Baja. Whenever I travel there, I come home with about six kilos of tortillas from Tortilla de Harina Lulu in San Jose del Cabo. Theirs are super thin. Still have some in my freezer but I do have my tortilla press at the ready to give it a go should they run out before I return again. As other writers commented, lard would be traditional, or some hydrogenated oil.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: @Beth Rogers your story is neat but / flour larger tortillas are also very authentic to Mexico. It’s very different than corn. Used for different things.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Flour Tortillas With Honey and Butter Recipe
commentBody: What memories this brings back!  I grew up on a sheep ranch in Wyoming. We hired sheepherders from northern New Mexico. During summers the herders stayed in sheep wagons which had a small wood burning cook stove.  I think I was about 9 or 10, staying with Tony in the wagon herding with him. Every morning he fired up the stove and made these directly on the iron stove top, and we ate them hot with butter and jam.  I remember it being some of the most delicious food I ever ate!  Thanks!

Many years ago, when we lived in Mississippi, the wife of my husband's coworker enchanted my husband with a slice of buttermilk chess pie.  He declared it was the best thing he had ever tasted. I was standing right there. He was never the same. I had to relocate him to Texas to break that spell.  I will be trying to put a spell on him and others come Saturday. Thanks for the inspiration.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For the small audience out there who make their own, whole milk kefir is a great 1 to 1 substitute for whole buttermilk. Serious Eats did a comparison.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Never mind that the modern product called whole milk buttermilk is what is needed for this recipe. The commenter stating that whole milk buttermilk in effect is not really buttermilk is correct. Actual buttermilk is the byproduct of the milk that is churned to make butter, with fat removed and only a few tiny flecks of butter left. The butter and buttermilk were left at room temperature approximately overnight to become ‘cultured’ or soured. Buttermilk you pick up in the store now is not that.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: RE:  Buttermilk.  The key word is cultured.   To full fat milk is added a bacterial culture that thickens and sours it, like other soured dairy products, e.g, yogurt, sour cream, etc.  In New England, Kate’s whole cultured buttermilk is widely available.  By the way, if you have the bacterial culture, it is very easy to turn your whole milk into buttermilk, but doing so will lengthen the prep time of the recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Whole, as in ‘full-fat’, buttermilk seems like something that doesn’t exist as authentic buttermilk is the by-product of removing fat (butter) from whole milk. 

I am not from the Us though, interested in learning what is understood to be whole buttermilk.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You are correct about true buttermilk, but I have never seen that for sale (made it myself a couple of times when I made butter). In the U.S., what is generally sold as buttermilk is a cultured milk - somewhat similar to kefir, but without any yeasts. Most stores only carry low-fat "buttermilk" that contains added ingredients including thickeners, but some stores carry both low-fat and whole-milk buttermilk with live active cultures.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am grateful,for this recipe as it is reminiscent of my Texas grandmother’s chess pie, but she only used egg yolks, and added a little nutmeg.    Chess pie was always made when she made angel food cake, it is how she used the yolks.

I like to use cultured buttermilk whenever possible, but it's not something I always have in my fridge, so a quick option (and not the lemon juice + milk) would be nice to keep on hand.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I made this pie yesterday.  
This recipe filled my 2" deep ceramic pie plate to the rim.  It is delicious but in the future I will halve the recipe unless I am baking for a crowd.  For those who are questioning the 3 cups of sugar, keep in mind that this is essentially a double recipe - there is enough filling for 2 regular depth pies.  
I did not freeze my pie shell.  Instead I par-baked my homemade crust for about 7 minutes at 425 and followed the instructions otherwise.  It turned out fine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
whole buttermilk is sort of a misnomer.  Buttermilk is the byproduct of buttermaking.  Most of the fat is taken out by the butter and real buttermilk is lowfat.
Whole buttermilk is cultured whole milk and is closer to yogurt made from whole milk than to real buttermilk.  I have added lemon to whole milk but I think the consistency of what the recipe asks for is closer to the consistency of a thinned out yogurt.  I would try 1/2 whole milk mixed with 1/2 whole milk yogurt.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Bordens sell whole buttermilk. Can  whole yogurt be used ?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It sure can...and if it's greek yogurt, just thin it out with a bit of whole fat milk so it's the liquidity consistency of buttermilk

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Who needs pie crust? I bake buttermilk chess pie filling in small fluted glass cups. Sometimes I put a fruit element such as a cherry or brandied apricot on top, and/or sprinkle with nutmeg, etc. Our family enjoys this dessert, especially after a big holiday meal. Too many extra calories in pie crust, no matter how delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this, to the letter, as the house was cool this morning, and served it to my dinner guests on the patio as the sun set. Used the 3.5% buttermilk I had from Costco here in Alberta. Freezing the crust was A+, as was the pie. No modifications. Thanks for a lovely recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Not too sweet? Three cups of sugar sounds like an awful lot.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Great pie! But honestly, I could not make it with 3 cups of sugar. I actually halved the sugar and it was perfectly sweet. I realize I have violated the recipe, but I am okay with that since the pie is delightfully vanilla-y and lemony. Silky and cool. Now I have to do a better job of making a homemade crust.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Do try the pat-in-pan pie crust recipe easily found on a search engine. It’s a creation of Pennsylvania Dutch folks, and to mix and spread it IN the pan is so easy. You get a short crust, not a flaky one, but I think it would work nicely with this filling. That what I am going to use, no mess on the counter and tasty, too. I use Crisco oil, that seems to work the best.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I'm really intrigued by this recipe and I absolutely love the pride with which the writer/chef describes their love for this kind of dessert. I do have a similar question though to the comments regarding the frozen pie shell. Even if I do freeze it whole with the pie in its pan and put it on the sheet pan, all my pie pans are glass. Putting a frozen glass pan into a 450 degree oven is bound to risk a serious crack. Maybe a metal tin? Ideas?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: So easy to make your own whole milk cultured buttermilk – especially with Kate‘s or any other marked with “live cultures”.  Fill a 4 cup Pyrex measuring cup to almost full of whole milk; cover &  nuke until it reaches “the almost to the boil bubbles around the edge stage”.  About 12-13 min. Remove and cool to about 104°;  stir in approx 1/3 cup of Kate’s, cover with plastic wrap; then into unheated oven. Will thicken overnight and further after refrigeration.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Same here, Joan.  We live in the Southwest and I haven't seen whole cultured buttermilk at the market in over 10 years.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The quality of what's labeled buttermilk varies widely. At its best, its just cultured milk, with no salt or other adjuncts. Inferior brands add gums, acidifiers, colors, and flavors to mimic the characteristics that develop naturally from long fermentation. Choose the buttermilk with the fewest ingredients or, if you use it regularly, consider culturing your own. Substitute milk soured with lemon juice or vinegar if you must, but it won't be as good.

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This has been my go to for 30 years
1 stick unsalted Butter room temp
1 ¾ cup Sugar
3 Eggs
1 teas. Salt
1 teas. Vanilla extract
3 tabls. Flour
¾ cup Buttermilk
1   9 inch Unbaked Pie Shell
Preheat oven to 350 degrees F
Cream butter and sugar until there are small lumps.  Beat in eggs stop as soon as batter is smooth. Stir in: salt, vanilla, and flour, then stir in buttermilk until mixed completely. Pour into 9" unbaked pie shell and bake in a preheated 350 degree oven for 1 hour 10 minutes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
"Whole buttermilk" is a confusing term, as all buttermilk is technically low-fat according to US labeling guidelines.  
Buttermilk is the result of churning cream into butter, with buttermilk remaining as the low-fat liquid byproduct.  It is then cultured.

Traditionally, or in the case of higher quality options nowadays, the cream would have been cultured first, with both the resulting butter and buttermilk being cultured products.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Continued...
Nowadays many commerical buttermilk products are actually skim or whole milk that has been cultured.  Slightly different product but essentially the same function and taste.  So it may be possible to find "whole milk buttermilk" but it is not a true buttermilk.
Either way, as long as it is not "fat-free", you should be fine.

1/2 tbs lemon zest
1/2 tsp vanilla extract (the 1 bean is way too much)
1/2 c. sugar
1/4 c. flour
1/4 tsp salt
3 eggs
1 1/2 c. cultured buttermilk

You'll be able to get through a slice without feeling sick. By the way, that pie crust should be pre-baked (375, blind bake 20 minutes, then remove weights and bake 15), then removed from tin and frozen.

commentBody: You can also make your own whole buttermilk (and get some homemade butter out of it too!) by taking heavy cream (or heavy whipping cream) putting it in a kitchen aid mixer, adding the whisk attachment and letting it go until it separates into butter solids and liquid buttermilk. commentBody:
I’d never had chess pie, but had heard of it for years, so I gave it a go this Thanksgiving.  I will admit; I cheated a bit; I used 2 tsp vanilla instead of a bean.

It was good, but… with so many other pies to choose from, I probably won’t be in a hurry to make this again.  It has a LOT of sugar, and not that flavorful.  It’s surprisingly chewy.  When I jiggled it to see if it was done, I thought it hadn’t set— but after 15 minutes more, I realized it had a giant puddle of butter in the middle.  So I sort of basted the top.

Like I said, it was fine, others liked it.  But if I’m going to make something this decadent, I’ll probably go with chocolate, banana cream, maple… something more interesting.  This is jes’ pie.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My paternal grandmother's family were all pie bakers...never cakes. author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
1teaspoon coarse kosher salt, such as Morton (or use 1½ teaspoons kosher salt)

The author is probably referring to Diamond Crystal, which is saltier by volume, but since she doesn't name it, perhaps the recipe follower will use a brand that is more like Morton, and end up with 50% more saltiness than desired.

What worked for many, many generations of chefs and cookbook authors was uniform, granulated table salt. In almost all recipes, it simply makes zero difference which form of NaCl is used.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: These folks need to pick a lane, like Serious Eats ... everything is Kosher, it's easier to pick up in a 'pinch' and measure in my opinion.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @L'wood, the issue is that "Kosher" isn't a brand name. Different brands have different physical qualities (but as Steve mentioned, salt is chemically the same across brands). I believe Serious Eats uses Diamond Crystal kosher salt.

author: Lisa Donovan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: To add to the confusion, in my prior post, I stated that Diamond Crystal is saltier (than Morton) by volume.  The opposite is true. Proves the point that specifying granulated table salt (what recipe authors used to refer to as simply "salt," obviates any confusion over differently textured kosher or other salts.  Unless you're eating the actual piece of salt (as with a finishing salt) there is no discernible taste difference when using one salt or another.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I know it sort of goes against every recipe for tres leches out there, but has anyone made a successful version WITHOUT condensed milk? What substitute did you use? I love that buttermilk replaces evaporated in this recipe- I’d love to be able to replace the condensed as well with something - is condensed milk used simply for the sweetness and thickness, therefore enhancing the texture of the sauce and cake? Could coconut cream work instead with some added sugar syrup?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There is sweetened condensed coconut milk available and you could give that a try instead of the dairy version. Also, you could try Coco Lopez (cream of coconut as opposed to coconut cream). Coco Lopez cream of coconut already has sugar added (it's what is used in Piña Coladas).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
What's up with the SALT description in the Recipe ... isn't Kosher Salt 'COARSE'?

1teaspoon coarse kosher salt, such as Morton (or use 1½ teaspoons kosher salt)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It's not particularly clear, but Morton's kosher salt consists of super dense flakes. Diamond Crystal kosher salt is lighter, "fluffier", and crumbles when pinched between your fingers. Earlier this year, there was a shortage of Diamond Crystal due to a labor strike, so likely why the author mentions a different brand. Many restaurants use Diamond Crystal if that means anything.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Would grits or polenta be equivalent to coarse ground cornmeal?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The cornmeal made it texturally like cornbread which I didn’t care for.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this cake on Sunday. It was edible, tasted fantastic. The cake sunk in the center, could it be because I used a glass 9x13? It was a trick to get the perimeter to soak in the liquid because it all just pooled in the sunken center.  I also had about two cups of liquid left over. It seemed very thick, like it wouldn't soak in, it took a full two days for the cake to become completely saturated around the thicker perimeter. Not sure if I'll make this again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Tasted very good but seems more like cornbread style, it took a long time to absorb. The milk mixture was delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made exactly as directed and used all of the soak- worth the time waiting for each pour so that all the soak was used. Perfectly delicious! My family loved it!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I was disappointed with this recipe. It was OK, but certainly nothing revelatory. Definitely not as good as the Tres leches recipes from Samantha Seneviratne and Margaux Laskey also in this app. I might try swapping in the buttermilk from this recipe for the evaporated milk in theirs and see what happens, but otherwise I’ll stick with those recipes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I actually had no problem adding the liquid to the cake! It was easily absorbed.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Jes cornmeal is sometimes called polenta, although polenta is usually a dish made with cornmeal.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: No Mexican kitchen would put buttermilk in tres leches.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Do you think this recipe will work in a Bundt pan?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made exactly as directed except I only used 1 can sweetened condensed milk, and I’m glad. I think it would have been too sweet for me with the full amount.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this cake following the recipe very carefully. First of all it baked unevenly (a little higher on one side) so it was hard to pour the soak evenly at first. It was easier once the cake cooled a bit. The first 2 cups of soak went into the cake but after that they just sat on top. I stopped after 3 cups. I let the cake sit overnight as instructed. I was so excited to try it but it was dense, dry and grainy. Any thoughts as to where I went wrong or has anyone else had a similar outcome?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you love  Whole Foods tres leches this will disappoint you. The cornmeal makes this a dry dense cake even after soaking overnight, the cornmeal gives it a grainy taste. It is definitely a more traditional tres leches and don’t recommend to anyone who prefers a silky white cake tres leches. My husband loves tres leches but was sadly disappointed by the cornmeal taste.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was good except for the cornmeal. If you like gritty cake then this is a winner. Not for me.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this with Bob's Red Mill coarse corrnmeal - and it stayed gritty, even after after a full day of soaking. What am I doing wrong?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Ok, after making this twice, I think, first, that the remarks that the cake is gritty due to the cornmeal is a function of mixing the batter. You really have to mix the batter exactly as stated in the recipe - in two 6-7 minute cycles. You really must be vigorous with it. Secondly, the comments that there is too much sauce are spot on. There is way too much sauce. I recommend making a pro rata reduction by about 1/3 of each of the ingredients.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The best part is it is a forgiving recipe. All these fussy posts make me giggle. Tres of any lèche type will work. :)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: One thing I do for strawberry frosting is to pulse freeze dried strawberries (the kind you buy at Trader Joe’s) in my mini food processor (you could also do this by hand easily enough), then sift out the seeds directly into the icing bowl. This allows me to reduce or eliminate the jam in the frosting and keep a more dense consistency but delivers intense strawberry flavor. And the color turns a natural light pink.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I used jam instead of preserves. Based on other reviews, I reduced sugar to 1 3/4 cup in cake and to 3 cups in frosting. I used natural powder coloring. Instead of preserves for the layers I used fresh diced strawberries. Result = PERFECTION. A delicious, fresh summer cake devoured - and enjoyed - by all!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For those who prefer less sweet: Martha Stewart’s strawberry cupcake recipe has very similar amounts of ingredients, but uses fresh strawberries instead of jam and a lower sugar Swiss meringue buttercream. Turned out great (700 g of sugar, 19.4 g/cupcake). Another option is to use roasted strawberries instead of the jam as suggested in the introduction.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: LOVED this recipe. Followed comments and stuck with 1 3/4c sugar in the batter and 3c in the icing. The cake was plenty sweet but not overpowering - still a light, slightly tart strawberry taste! Decided to make my own preserves for the first time and learned some things - strawberries are high in water and low in pectin, so I ended up using gelatin to set the preserves - which in effect made this a (delish) Jello cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @cb try making freezer jam. Wonderfully flavorful.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It is recommended to "roast  your own high season strawberries"  but no explanation of how and when /how to incorporate them in the recipe. Substitute equal amounts roasted strawberries cup for cup for the preserves?  Would love to know as we have abundance and would like to prepare to freeze (hopefully?) for using later in winter?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I thought the consistency, etc were great but -- as warned -- it is extremely sweet. If I make it again, I might try adding a bunch of lemon zest and cutting down some of the sugar (or using unsweetened jam?). One hack I'm using as I eat it, and loving, is to serve a piece with a wedge of lemon. Squeezing it on really cuts the sweetness.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Way too complicated- a lot of steps that can be simplified!
Complete lack of concern for kitchen with much less equipment!
Way too much fat, too much sugar!
Nevertheless very tasty!
Enjoyed the cooking anyway!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@jobst the lawyer These are pretty standard cake ingredients/steps/equipment so not sure what you’re going on about? 
Helpful tip for you; if you’re watching your sugar and fat intake you don’t have to make cake, try an apple.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I will use vanilla extract for this bc I strongly dislike vanilla paste. I detest the texture of vanilla bean paste. It’s more expensive anyway. 

There is so much going on with the flavors, I’m surprised that the paste is called for here. The Nielsen Massey brand site says they are pretty much interchangeable. 

My whole point in writing this is to encourage people to use what they have in their pantries regarding this ingredient.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I thoroughly enjoyed baking this cake! I made it exactly as stated, it's a tall, gorgeous pink cake! You can't be in a hurry to bake this...and taking time to set the frosting layers in the fridge helped a lot! I ate the cake that was cut from the top and licked the frosting whisk... it's in the fridge now to share tomorrow!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is a superb cake. I made it for my son’s birthday and had two 12-year-old boys and my husband tell me it was the best cake they’d ever had. I followed the comments and kept the sugar to 1 3/4 cup in the batter and I used pulverized freeze-dried strawberries in the frosting instead of jam (less sugar too). When I make it again, I may add more pulverized freeze-dried strawberries to the batter to up the strawberry flavor. If you want to impress people, this is the cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hi did you use cake flour? Did your cake look like the featured image? I am so confused on why my cake turned out to be so dense and heavy. Thanks

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe was a complete fail. Wish I had thought to read the comments before hand. The cake is super sweet and sort of dense. Not very pleasant at all. Also the frosting came out like soup. Really wanted it to be better.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Just tried this last night—we enjoyed it! We’re amateurs at baking (e.g. used old hand mixer), so we appreciated the comments noting ingredient and prep difficulty. But we pulled it off. We followed the suggestion to swap preserve layer with fresh sliced strawberries instead—tastes great and gives a lovely visual (also topped cake with sliced strawberries). We cut back on the powdered sugar for frosting (maybe 1/2-1 cup less than recipe). Fun weekend project for the season.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
The cake was heavy and wet; the icing was sloppy and only just about stayed on. The cake in particular was too sweet for my taste (even leaving out the jam between layers), though I'd been warned of this in the reviews. The consistency was the real disappointment. 

I followed the recipe to a T.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This cake is terrific. Dense and moist, but not "stodgy." Serve with lots of fresh strawberries! 

The sweetness varies depending on what brand of strawberry preserves you use. I found that Bonne Maman is much sweeter than Crofter's Organic w/ Cane Sugar. 

The frosting is delicious, just like strawberry ice cream. Wonderful as is, but you can't taste the cream cheese. Next time I think I will increase the cream cheese and reduce the butter, to hopefully make it more tangy and cheesecakey.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Skewers to anchor layers should be a note in recipe comments. If you used as much icing between layers in that photo, you need to stabilize potential movement!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I’m looking for a way to use up a lot of leftover jam.  This looks interesting but way way WAY too sweet. Surely you don’t need so that jam AND all that sugar…

Still it’s an interesting idea to substitute jam for part of the sugar in a recipe and REDUCE the amount of sugar.  4 cups icing sugar AND jam in the icing— holy sugar rush, gives me a headache just thinking about it. 

Fresh strawberries are an obvious improvement, but bc then I’m still stuck with all that jam!!  What to do??

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you don’t add the red coloring gel, the cake will be a very nice gray.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve made another version of this cake using fresh strawberries - note that they are full of water so cooking them down by either roasting in the oven or on the stovetop with some sugar will get rid of the extra liquid and intensify the strawberry flavour. Freeze dried strawberry powder will also help the flavour be more discernibly strawberry-ish and the cake more naturally pink - but more muted than if you use gel food colouring. The powder can also go into the frosting.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I typically don't use a spray on my cake pan but since that was an option I figured I would try. I do not recommend. Take the time to butter and flour your pans. Also if you do use the spray to prep your pans: when you are trimming the cake tops also trim your edges. My cakes were significantly harder on the edges than any other time I make cakes and grease and flour my pans. With how soft this frosting is - it's a nightmare to cut with a harder edge to the cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made it. Cake is cloyingly sweet with all the preserves AND that 2 1/4 cups of sugar. Whew.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Halved recipe, made 2 dzn cupcakes. Used frozen strawberries that I cooked on the stove until syrupy, added a dollop of orange marmalade, then mashed them before adding. No food coloring in batter. I didn't care about the color, moreso the taste and texture which were fine. Strawberry powder in the frosting added flavor and color. Good recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Debby, for the butter the recipe is correct. 1c   2tbsp is equal to 255grams.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I didn’t have gel food coloring so just used a little of the liquid stuff. It was a two day project as I made the cakes one day and then put it together the next. I had to keep it in the fridge in between frosting it and then after because the frosting kept melting. I think I’d skip the jam in between the layers if I make it again although I did use raspberries and they were great. The cake was moist but the frosting was a little sweet. It’s a show stopper!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I'm going to make this for my sister-in-law's bday cake - thx for the idea - I will puree strawberries and use those instead of the frosting in between layers. Excited.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This cake is delicious. Make sure the preserves you use aren’t too sweet - there’s a lot of sugar here. I wanted the frosting to be a little more tart so will add some lemon zest next time!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was the most difficult cake I have ever made!   And, the most expensive!   I spent $21 on the vanilla bean paste and $24 on the preserves alone!   It was good but not worth all the trouble and expense!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I cut down on the sugar and with all the preserve it was very sweet.  I think more strawberry flavor would make this better.  I didn’t use food color so my cake was a pretty pink.  This is a big cake that could serve a large family or a dinner party.  I thought it was a lot of work but well worth the time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was a showstopper at a Memorial Day party, with the icing a beautiful pink even without any food coloring. It was intensely rich though, and was the first time I've ever had a cake layer fall on me (luckily only partly, and only one of them). I'm wondering what I could do next time to get a lighter feeling and fluffier cake. I will definitely only make 2 layers worth of cake so that the size is more manageable.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
A friend made this cake for me and I thought it was the best cake I had ever had, and I don't like cake. She sis not put preserves between the layers. 
I then also made it and it was a disaster!  The batter and the frosting needed a whole tube of food coloring to turn pink, and my frosting turned out like soup. The butter and cream cheese were very solid, and after I added the sugar the consistency was about right, but once I added thonce I added the jam it turned into a liquid nightmare. I neeu

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You might want to try using paste coloring instead of liquid. Adding that much liquid to frosting could change the consistency.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly. If I did it again, I would reduce sugar, as other commenters have noted, and not mix so much. My mixer (a Kitchenaid pro 6 quart) is maybe more powerful than the recipe expected. I mixed as directed, and the cake sank dramatically. Still tasted great, but was kind of an ugly baby.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
The grams conversions for butter in the recipe are off. 1 and 1/2 cups of butter is 340 grams, not 255 grams 

Also too much preserves and butter in the icing. Made it soft and had to be refrigerated. I had to adjust the amount and used more cream cheese and less preserves.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Overall okay - way too sweet with the preserves, and 35 min was too long in my oven (came out really dry). I used fresh strawberries instead of preserves as layers. If I made it again, I’d probably leave the preserves out of the frosting and decrease sugar in the cake itself

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Beware of the original recipe using strawberry preserves…I usually make a new recipe without adjustments the first time around. This cake, unlike most from the NYT, was just awful. Judging from the comments, others have baked it with success after making some alterations. I strongly suggest doing so!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Followed the recipe, adjusted sugar as advised, and the cake was super dense and heavy in weight. I used 4 8-inch pans instead of the 3 9-inch pans so started checking at around 35min for doneness and the knife was super dry already so i removed from oven. Not eatable - super disappointed - and this was for a large party. I’m going to retry it in a 9-inch pan and leave for 40-45min hoping it’ll be successful. Lesson learned: don’t try new cakes for large parties, remain with what you know !

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made the cake again because I was so confused on why I wasn’t achieving the result in the image and thought I did something wrong the first time.. something is off with this recipe I think. It’s again turned out super dense and no one liked it. This is not a cake. Or else you absolutely need cake flour?? I used all purpose. Anyway good for those who made this into a nice cake - lI am usually a good baker but I was embarrassed with this cake at two major occasions now (today for Thanksgiving).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @ViNYC - I also tried (and failed) twice. The first time, it had a lovely strawberry flavor, but was flat and dense. I thought I’d used a preserve with too much liquid, so tried the recipe’s recommendation the next time. It produced a slightly lighter cake, though still extremely dense, but barely any strawberry flavor compared to the first. I agree that something is wrong with the recipe. I’ve never had such poor results. Was really looking forward to celebrating my daughter’s bday with this cake and had been saving the recipe for the occasion. Like you, I will stick with what I know next time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @ViNYC I had the same result. I think the skewer comes out with no crumbs long before it’s done. That’s the only thing I can think of

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: What color is the cake if you don't add the food coloring?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A previous comment noted the cake color would be grey.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: How do we use fresh strawberries to make this? The season is about to start here in Washington state and I am excited to try it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: use the fresh strawberries to make strawberry preserves.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I make a fresh strawberry  cake (recipe differs) and use pureed strawberries - 1 cup puree in the cake, 1/2 cup in the frosting. I'm thinking it should work - and I think the amount of sugar would be fine to sweeten the berries. Actually I think the cake featured here may be a tad oversweet with all those preserves and added sugar.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: i disagree with the idea of adding wet and dry in three additions each. start with the dry because mixing the flour in first coats the flour and minimizes gluten stimulation. then add half the wet, another third of the dry, then the remaining wet and finally the last of the dry to take up any remaining wetness in the batter.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This site lists Edna Lewis’ wonderful recipes for strawberry preserves. Take a look, you’ll love it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Looks like you forgot the link.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It does have the strawberry preserves in the actual cake( I thought it might be a white cake with the preserves between the layers) so it will take on some color. Personally I hate pink so would not add the food coloring and just use the preserves.. but that’s just me!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Why not use regular vanilla instead of vanilla paste?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The paste has flecks of vanilla bean in it and has a bit more intense flavor since it is made by using the vanilla beans that are crushed and mixed with vanilla extract.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You can use vanilla extract if that’s what you have!! It’ll hardly make a lick of difference in a cake like this. Using something like vanilla paste shows us more when vanilla is the focus flavor (whipped cream/ice cream/vanilla frosting)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A delicious cake! My recipe calls for defrosted frozen strawberries (instead of preserves) which I think is better.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
First had this cake in Midtown Memphis 20 years ago. Then learned to make it at home. My recipe replaces the strawberry preserves with a package of strawberry jello. Yeah, jello, the sugar powder stuff that that comes in a boxed bag. 

Don't try this a home. Round here my version of this dessert is known as "the cake that killed Elvis". Mmm...Mmm.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Sounds yummy

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Can you make this in a Bundt pan?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I might try, and then thin the frosting and drizzle it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: how do you roast your own strawberries? Hint please?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've never roasted strawberries. The opening notes suggest them as a substitute for strawberry jam. I'm intrigued. If roasting instructions were included I would be inspired.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I completely agree!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The butter measurement is confusing. I’m just going to go by weight, because one cup plus two tablespoons is not 1 1/4 sticks. Help?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For butter in sticks here in the US, 1 stick = 1/2 cup = 8 tablespoons. So the cake recipe uses 2 sticks of butter + 2 tablespoons (18 tablespoons). The frosting recipe uses 3 sticks of butter.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: How did your cake turn out?  Did you use 255 grams?  As you suggested that is about half of the amount the recipe shows if you measure in cups.  I want to make this but that is a HUGE difference, and I don't want to waste the ingredients and my time.  Thanks for your input.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Never made this particular recipe but have added pulverized freeze dried strawberries to add flavor and (ahem, natural) color to other strawberry cakes

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Freeze dried raspberries are also outstanding.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Too much dough, Augh! Each pan had 800 grams of dough, and I had to deal with overflowing and dripping. Should I have prepped a fourth pan?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Yes, it’s a big recipe.  I think I might cut it in half and make cupcakes. I don’t have many occasions that call for such a big cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: One way to roast strawberries is this method. I use it all the time with great results! Wash berries and cut only the large ones in half. Gently toss in a bowl with some sugar, juice of half a lemon, and a dash of almond extract. Be careful not to overdo any of the add ins. Place strawberries on a jelly roll pan and roast at 400 for between 30-40 minutes. If strawberries are placed in 1 layer, they won’t produce as much juice. Alternatively, you can use a small Dutch oven and roast for 40 min

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I usually grind a small amount of pink peppercorns over the strawberries before roasting to add to the flavor. Just a small amount to add interest but not to overwhelm

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Too sweet. Way too sweet. All I taste is sugar.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: What is vanilla paste and where to get it? Would dehydrated strawberries work? How about oven dried strawberries?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I've never used vanilla paste, but I've ordered vanilla beans and extract online from these two places.

Beanilla
King Arthur Flour

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This cake brings back summer and birthday memories for some of us. You will see it in many baskets on the greatlawn when displaced southerners gather for their summer picnics. IYKYK

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: One can find non-toxic (plant-based) food coloring on line.  It’s what I use for all my cakes and frostings, especially for children.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious!! Cut cake recipe in half for 3 6”layers. Cut icing to 2/3 with icing to spare (best icing I have ever had and the leftovers seem to be disappearing ). Absolutely will make again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I used strawberries we picked at a Tennessee farm and made fresh strawberry preserves to use.It was a sensational cake. I reduced the sugar in the frosting by one cup to preserve some of the tang of the cream cheese and not have an overwhelmingly sweet frosting.  I did not use any food coloring. The cake was an off white/gray color (who cares?) and the frosting was light pink. We froze the third layer of the cake and made it just two layers and it was big enough to feed 9 of us, with leftovers!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Instead of using food coloring, try strawberry powder in both the batter and icing. You can get it from Amazon and other bulk retailers. Way better flavor and color, and likely better than using a good dye.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made a puree out of plain roasted strawberries in place of preserves and that helped control the sweetness and turned my cake a really pretty pink. I didn't use vanilla paste tho, just extract so maybe that helped the colour.  I shouldn't have put the puree in the frosting as now it has a slightly split appearance, but still tastes nice!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For the amount of work andnumber of ingredients  this cake requires it was just ok.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Does anyone know how tall each layer of cake should be? I made the recipe but I think each cake did not rise correctly.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Check your baking powder and baking soda. If they have been opened for longer than 6 months they are most likely the culprit for the cakes not rising properly.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This cake is a lovely jewel.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My daughter requested raspberry cupcakes for her birthday, so I gave this recipe a try, swapping out the strawberry jam for raspberry. The cake and frosting turned out unbelievably sweet. I don’t recommend this change, I’m disappointed. Maybe it will be to the kids taste.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: To reduce the sugar, I would use strawberry purée and buttermilk powder in the cake and an Italian meringue buttercream with ground freeze dried strawberries. Will let you know how it goes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For those who prefer less sweet: the introduction to this recipe mentions using roasted strawberries instead of jam. Martha Stewart’s strawberry cupcake recipe has very similar amounts and proportions of ingredients but uses fresh strawberries and a Swiss meringue buttercream. They turned out well and still have 700 g sugar, or 19.4 g per cup cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Instead of red food coloring I added pulverized freeze-dried strawberries and decreased sugar. Delicious recipe!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Do not use food coloring, there are so many natural powders like dragon fruit, strawberry or beet that add extra flavoring and beautiful colors without the chemicals.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is so much cake. It does not translate well to cupcakes, FYI.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Freeze-Dried Strawberries:
The process of food-processing to sifting out seeds, do it fast on a rainy day. The powder will start to clump in the humidity..

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I followed other comments and reduced to 1 3/4c sugar, and made the frosting from the Strawberry Lemonade cake instead (https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1025504-strawberry-lemonade-cake). Everyone loved it!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I love this cake.  I made it for my husband’s birthday last September and I’ve been looking for a good reason to make it again.  This time I followed the recipe completely—had to go back to the store for more preserves twice (NEXT TIME READ AHEAD).  I wouldn’t change a thing!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe didn’t work for me. I’m a fairly seasoned baker and followed the instructions precisely. The test skewers came out completely clean before the time stated so I pulled them out. I think you need to almost over bake this cake. It was super dense and heavy. However the strawberry flavor was unmatched. I don’t think I’ll try again.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I just learned today that it’s actually NECESSARY for the crust to be warm when you add the lemon mixture — otherwise, you can get upside down lemon bars (the filling seeps below the crust). This info comes from Bon Appetit and the recipe developer who just about lost her mind testing dozens of lemon bar recipes.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Agree. My grandmother's recipe has a note: "add filling to crust hot from oven!"

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Such a small amount of pecan in the crust. Can you make it with an increased ratio of pecan to flour?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I make mine with 60% crunched almonds + almond flour and 40% flour.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: The recipe author, obviously, found the stated amount of pecans to be optimal. Experiment with different ratios if you like.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Well, my crust was definitely very warm, if not hot, and the lemon mixture seeped below the parchment on the bottom of the pan, as well as seeped into the crust. I ended up with a soggy, mushy, unappealing mess. I've never had such a horrible outcome from a recipe. I thought I had followed the recipe quite faithfully. Needless to say, I'm not going to try again.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
Love this! Lemon bars are usually too sweet for me, so I use only 1 C sugar and no powdered sugar.

To prevent seepage, make sure your shortbread is pressed tightly against the pan sides. When it comes out of the oven, if it’s pulled away from the sides, use the back of a spoon to press and re-seal. When adding the curd, pour it over a spatula to prevent making divots (seepage!) in the shortbread.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: This note regarding seepage should have been part of the recipe!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I seal the crust, as it comes out of the oven, by brushing slightly whipped egg whites all over the inside of the shell.  It fills in any cracks and creates a seal, preventing the filling from seeping.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
I loved reading the article, but the recipe not so much. 

I got upside-down bars.  The topping went between the layers of parchment paper.

There were several other problems with the recipe and the instructions. I won’t make it again.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is wonderful albeit a little labor intensive (zesting/juicing lemons, toasting and grinding almonds). I did substitute 30 grams almonds for 30 grams flour for more almond flavor. The lemon custard is very, well, lemony. That said, find a copy of the 4/7/24 NYT's magazine and read Lisa Donovan's description of her time as a private chef. It's pure poetry and a lovely piece on finding yourself and what's important in life. Delightful!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: This is an excellent recipe. I suggest pressing the crust into the sides (not all the way up), but just enough to give it a lip to contain the filling. My only gripe is it took 20 mins longer to bake, but I blame my own oven. These were a crowd pleaser — some went back for thirds!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Most AMAZING recipe for lemon bars yet. I had to bake my bars for 5 min over, but after that they were really  good. Thanks!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I ended up with upside down bars. The corners of the parchment leaked and the egg mixture ran under the bars. I understand the crust must be hot to prevent that too. They were delicious but messy.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
Besides ways too many steps/procedures to do before the pan finally goes in the oven for the last step, I also had seepage of the filling.
The bars have a great very lemony kick, but in relation to the effort employed I won't be making them again.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made lemon bars many times but not with a pecan crust. It sounds delicious. I also have found that adding lemon zest to the crust is always a great way to intensify the lemon flavor, which many people really love. I would highly recommend doing so.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I made these lemon bars following the recipe to the letter. It was my entry for a dessert contest at work. And guess who won! It came out perfect. Thank you!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
Needs to be thinner crust. Find a 9 by 13 or use 2 pans. 

Easily customizable! I used lime/coconut

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
I made this 4 days ago and I gave it a C. The crust was uneven and I had to bake it an additional 15 minutes over the 25 minutes suggested. I did not put the sheet pan underneath as suggested and I think that’s why it took longer. However, I remade it today and was very strict with weighing and measuring being certain to be exact (not a little over or under). 
I have to say WOW!! What a difference it made! 😁

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I made this the other night because of my overflowing lemon tree and not because I like to bake…I don’t like to bake. The crust was hot, just out of the oven, when I poured the lemon liquid and it didn’t seep. I love the kick the kosher salt adds. I think the crust is a bit too thick (I used a 9 x 13 baking dish), turn up the lemon flavor of the filling and make it more gelatinous and not so dense but they’re pretty darn good anyway.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Do you think 1/2 c of Flour can be Gluten Free flour?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone else ever noticed that this is kind of like baked lemonade?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I liked these in terms of flavor but found the crust to be a bit mushy - I like the more crumbly firm traditional shortbread crust. But otherwise very good.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: My only quibble with this recipe is how difficult it is to put it into the pan in step 2. But it is now the most asked-for dessert I have ever made. Incredible recipe!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: In a word “odd” - and not tangy enough

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: No idea what went wrong here. My crust came out very soggy. I did not have spillage or leakage of the filling, but the crust was almost greasy and not crispy at all. I used cake flour which may be why this came out wrong. The filling was fabulous!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: just about everything Melissa Clarks presents is worth making. I love these bars.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is presented by Lisa Donovan.  I do have to add, Melissa Clark is wonderful!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this recipe using gluten-free flour?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: The thought of adding zest to the crust as well sounds terrific?  How much do you add?  1/2 a tablespoon?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
Questioning the measurement for pecans….is it for the whole or pecan pieces or for crushed pecans?
Less pecans in former…more in latter.
Help.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I use walnuts in sweet crust as they are more affordable and what I had. Works great.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I made these and they’re very nice. Prefer lemon bars with shortbread crust. Why change a classic?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Used hazelnut meal from Bob's Red Mill because I had it on hand. Delish.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: @Gian: Did you use the hazelnut meal as a substitute for the flour, for the finely ground pecans, or for both? Just curious...

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried using vegan egg replacer for the eggs ? I’d love to make a vegan version of these !

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: My final result gets only a B  on account of gooeyness (the filling). Might separating a few of the eggs - omitting the whites - improve the consistency? Also agree with other commenters wanting more pecans in the crust; mine is bland.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: My pecans promptly turned into pecan butter when I tried to grind them. Are they too warm? What can I do to avoid this?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: For Kate:  Pastry chef trick:  always grind nuts with some sugar from the recipe (keep total sugar the same) and nuts should be room temp or cooler.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
Perfect ratio of crust to filling. I swapped finely ground salted pistachios for the pecans. I also corse chopped additional 1/4 cup of salted pistachios and sprinkled on the top when 10 minutes remained on the timer. 
They were perfect and didn’t need powdered sugar. My guests raved. I stored them in the refrigerator overnight, then cut with warm knife as directed. Leftovers still were fine from the fridge 3 days later.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: These were very disappointing. The crust is not as good as traditional lemon bar crust and the curd was very tough (I might have overcooked slightly but after 25 minutes it was sloshy so I put it back for 10 more) and not all that flavorful given the amount of lemon juice and zest. Far better and easier is the lemon bar recipe on the Once Upon a Chef website. I've made this recipe dozens of times and it's always delicious. https://www.onceuponachef.com/recipes/luscious-lemon-bars.html

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Added note to my previous posting, I reduce the sugar in the curd of the Once Upon a Chef recipe for Luscious Lemon Bars to 2.5 cups.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Once cooled, and before adding the powdered sugar, can these be frozen?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Can these bars be frozen?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I’ve had a terrible time with this recipe. First, it took 5 lemons to get 2 T of zest. Then after baking for 25 minutes, it’s still very soupy.  I have it in for another 15 minutes, I may have to go out and purchase a dessert for my book club tonight!  Ugh!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: These are absolutely delicious. I was short on regular flour so I substituted about 80g with almond flour and it sent them over the edge - I would make them that way again!

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Thank you Beth G for posting an alternative recipe that was SO much better. I was super disappointed in this recipe. The crust was meh. There should have been more pecans - just 1/3 c was not enough. I baked a few extra minutes since the filling was not set and then refrigerated overnight. The filling was still sticky the next morning.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Add me to the number of those who got some upside down (filling on bottom, crust on top) bars, as well as some with filling on both sides of the crust. The crust was not hot when I poured the filling into the pan. I am glad I did only half a recipe. Not the ideal lemon bar presentation.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody:
Will use slightly more pecans next time. Had no problem with filling seeping below crust - maybe pecans were not finely ground?

A simple recipe though lengthy to execute, but a keeper for sure.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: THESE DID NOT WORK. I have never had lemon bars not work. The crust was baked a little over 25 minutes, but it was not very "crispy." When I poured the egg mixture over the crust, it made a hole in the crust and dribbled out from there! I quickly stopped pouring and attempted to spoon the eggs/lemon juice mixture over the crust more gently, but it still ran down the sides (against the paper liner) and when cooked, I also ended up with lemony "layer" bars. They aren't pretty, but they are tasty.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Like Susan Sawyer, I had a soggy, inedible mess.  I think there is too much butter in the crust, not enough dry ingredients (especially not enough pecans). It pain me to have wasted all those eggs and butter.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: made these w walnuts because I couldn’t be bothered to buy another nut, I think it still added a good richness when toasted/ground. My lemon needed more like ~35 minutes to cook well, and kind of sunk in the middle when cooking (but still tasted great!) agree with others that you should pour the lemon/egg mixture pretty much directly after taking crust out of oven (I think I waited abt 5 mins). Would make again and experiment w some other nuts/maybe adding a bit more lemon juice

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Can this recipe for lemon bars be frozen?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Not really my favorite lemon bar recipe. The crust seeped to the top and it ended up tasting like a kind of weird custard.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Everything came together  with effort but it cracked as I took it out of the pan . Not sure why, maybe still too warm? I couldn't cut into bars, but cut into irregular pieces and it was delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Crust comes out chalky and dry, I much prefer the traditional shortbread crust

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I make a nut crust for cheesecake that works well here. No flour, gluten free, just finely chopped pecans, butter, sugar, and salt as you would make a graham cracker crust (spiced with cinnamon or cocoa for cheesecake but not for lemon bars). By the way, there is no need to toast the pecans first as you will bake the crust before filling.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: I strained the lemon / egg mixture before pouring onto the crust, because I don't like the texture of lemon zest. The flavors were excellent, both of the filling and the crust. Very bright lemony flavor. I was a bit disappointed in the filling's color, though: it was pale yellow -- like a dirty dandelion -- instead of a bright lemon yellow. I suspect I overbeat the eggs.

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Can you replace the granulated sugar with confectioner's sugar with no other changes?

author: Lisa Donovan
recipeTitle: Lemon Bars With Pecan Crust Recipe
commentBody: Seepage hint:create a little lip around the shortbread/crust border & make sure the liquid stays w/in those borders!  I definitely had too much filling, so I just threw away the surplus.  No seepage, cut the sugar in the filling in half & they came out great.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was a Christmas Get-Together special around our Michigan family. However it was chopped Snickers Bars on the top rather than the Pecans. Because if you're going all out, don't stop yourself.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you over whip the cream don’t despair! Add a little (2 tbsps at most) unwhipped cream (or milk) and stir gently, this will take the cream back to the perfectly whipped stage.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is even better with pistachios and pistachio pudding

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Could you share the original recipe?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
the version i learned how to make was called "Better Than Robert Redford." 
Melt 1 stick margarine, pour into an 7x11 pan (probably originally 9x13, but the smaller pan produces thicker layers), add 1 and a half cups graham cracker crumbs and freeze for 5 minutes. Blend 8 oz room-temp cream cheese with 1 cup confectioners' sugar, add 4 oz of thawed Cool Whip. Spread this over crust.
Prepare 2 packages of instant chocolate pudding using 3 cups milk, spread this over cream cheese. Top with nuts.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Big smiles reading the article and recipe! My mother called the dessert "Chocolate Torte". Years ago, I developed basically the same 'from scratch' dessert using organic ingredients. We call it 'Supreme Organic Junk Food' and it is as beloved in my family as the original junk food recipe. Side by side, the scratch version is 100% tastier. If chocolate isn't your thing, a butterscotch version is as easy and delicious. I hope everyone who fondly remembers the dessert will try this version.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I remember my mom doing a graham cracker crust variation--pulverized graham crackers mixed with nuts and melted butter and pressed into the bottom of the pan. She would also sometimes add a layer of cherry pie filling between the cream cheese and chocolate pudding. 
Thanks for the nostalgia!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I kid you not—I stopped by my 90 year-old mom’s house this evening and as we were making a pot of tea she brought out two tea cups, each filled with pistachio pudding, generously topped with cool whip and toasted walnuts, and graham cracker segments tucked into the side of the pudding cups. A deconstructed Delight, so well-timed given I’m reading this article just a few hours later.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Did anyone else call this dessert Robert Redford?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Yes! My aunt used to make it and that’s what she called it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I completely understand the desire to make a "real" version of this and ditching the store-bought convenience versions of the layers. But...for those of us who won't be able to finish this in a day, or maybe like having leftovers after putting a certain amount of effort into cooking something, not using stabilized whipped cream will most likely result in a watery mess after a day in the fridge. I hate to think I am making a pitch to use something like Cool Whip, but maybe a stabilizer?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Maybe some cream of tarter?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Since the whipped cream goes on last, why not whip the cream as needed and add it at serving time?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've used instant vanilla pudding, about 1 tablespoon or so, to stabilize whipped cream, with great results. You don't taste the vanilla pudding (which sometimes can taste a little "chemically" to me, at all.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Excluding the nuts, this recipe sounds like a dessert my aunt made for decades that she called "Chocolate Delight."  She's been gone for years. I miss her and her dessert.  I may have to try this recipe in her honor ----excluding the nuts, of course.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is my all-time favorite dessert, I mean all-time.  I had this for every birthday party in elementary school instead of birthday cake.  This is the fancy version, which is fantastic, but you can certainly include or omit convenience ingredients.  The original recipe uses Jell-o instant pudding and Cool Whip; I don't have a problem with the Jell-o-it's just sugar and cornstarch which go into pudding anyway-but Cool Whip is icky.  Use real whipped cream.  Thank you!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My sister provided the recipe [ from the inside of the pistaccio pudding box ] to my wife and she made it for over 40 years for special occasions . We like it both ways . Also like it better with a nutty crust rather than graham cracker !

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: makes it vegan though, if you use nutmilk or oatmilk for the pudding

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My coworker makes his mom’s version, Pistachio Lush. I feel no shame in begging him to bring it whenever there is an office potluck.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is to gc: Now you're talking! Thanks for the "real deal," which is with the store bought puddings and what not. (I will always love the cakes my 1950s/60s mom and aunts made using mixes and embellishing them. I don't want to do that everyday, but now and again I do.)

author: Lisa Donovan
