commentBody: My Mississippi MIL makes this for holidays and special visits from my husband (her son). Thirty or so years ago, she would make this whenever he came home from Ole Miss - leading his two sisters to say the “pet” must be coming home, leading to the name, “Pet’s Dessert” a name that continues today.  She makes it in those big turkey roasting pans, allowing her to give her “pet” (my husband) a slice as big as his head. It is delicious and is made with pudding mix and cool whip.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For the cook who worried about the refrigerator life of whipped cream, you make a stabilized version of whipped cream using mascarpone cheese. I think it could only add to the deliciousness of the dessert. The recipe is online under stabilized whipped cream

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: 921 calories per serving! Yikes!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Our version of this , with completely different ingredients, comes out twice a year and is called Spoonable Pumpkin Pie. Oddly, the recipe came from a dr’s office magazine called Diabetes Today (not why we make it) and it is heaven in a plate/in a bowl/by thé spoonful/whatever!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For years I've made a McCall's recipe similar to the chocolate pudding in this recipe.  One ingredient missing here is almond extract.  In my opinion it's a tasty addition.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mother had a coconut version, prepared with a coconut/brown sugar crush, French vanilla pudding, and toasted coconut sprinkled on the top. We just called it "Mrs. Trier's dessert", because she got the recipe from the mother of one of my sister's elementary school classmates. Amazing how many varieties there were built around the same basic dessert structure.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Go ahead and use instant pudding if you want, but skip the Cool Whip. Real whipped cream is so, so much better! If you're making it ahead of time, or have leftovers (unheard of at our house!), use stabilized whipped cream, otherwise you'll have a sad, runny, unappetizing pool of separated whipped cream.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Diana Part 2. OK, puzzle solved. Wikihow suggests whipped cream will last 5 to 7 days when it’s continuously refrigerated, and if the whipped cream was cold when whipped. It could also be that it was stored towards the refrigerator door instead of to the back of the fridge, where the temp is colder. Also suggests to store it underneath other chilled items to get the coolest temperature, preserving the peaks and texture. https://www.wikihow.com/Store-Whipped-Cream

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I have never had my freshly made whipped cream separate - I am really curious why everybody thinks it will due to a second day in the fridge after the dessert is enjoyed.  Perhaps if using a chemically altered "non-fat" or "light" whipped cream it will separate, but I always use the real, fresh thing.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've found the "cook and serve" versions of the jello pudding mixes are outstanding, and a whole different experience in depth of taste and moth feel, from the instant versions. Not too much extra work at all, just use the same milk, box of cook and serve pudding, heat while stirring, use. So good. The chocolate sugar free cook and serve pudding is a great option for those who prefer it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Feeds 12 adults or 3-4 stoners.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There's a coconut version, too, (Coconut Delight) with coconut in the pudding and in the crust.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: my mom added thawed, drained, tart cherries from the summer harvest between the chocolate and the top layer. I am still drooling!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The true ‘70s version used Jello chocolate pudding, cream cheese plus Cool Whip, with a graham cracker crust! When the recipe ran in our newspaper it was called “The Next Best Thing to Robert Redford.” Eventually we just called it Redford. It was easy and delish, in that ‘70s frankenfoods way.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is a delicious make ahead dessert that travels well. People commented about using real whipped cream and leftovers being runny. I have had this leftover in the fridge for a little more than 24 hours and there is nothing runny. I used real whipped cream. Don’t whimp out and use graham crackers instead of pecans in the crust. The pecans add an almost salty caramel flavor and some needed texture in an otherwise completely creamy dessert. The results are worth the time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mom made a family version of this recipe called “Dessert Secret.” The crust was graham crackers and saltines crushed with butter, topped with Jell-O butterscotch pudding and butter pecan ice cream mixed together, a layer of cool whip, and finally a generous helping of chopped pecans. I grew up in the desert and remember standing in front of the refrigerator in the height of summer, heaping spoonfuls of it into my mouth while the cool air blasted me in the face.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Aww, yes, my mother loved all the new “fake” foods and recipes. This one she called Chocolate Eclair dessert. Such fond memories. The other one was a 4 Day coconut cake. That recipe should be written up.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Remember Whip n Chill? Yummy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Oh, Whip 'n Chill! About as fake as you can get, but boy did we love it in my family. Texture kind of like a spongy cloud . . .

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Where I grew up, this is a Mississippi Mud Pie. Just swap the homemade pudding and whipped cream for instant pudding and Cool Whip. You can also use graham crackers with the pecans instead of flour for the crust so you don't have to bake it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was my Aunt Sally's signature recipe for years. I once watched a squirrel house an entire pan of the stuff during a family picnic. From then on, it was known as “squirrel surprise.”

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For those wanting stabilized whipped cream, just try mixing all of the whipped cream and cream cheese together and splitting it up rather than only adding some whipped cream to the cream cheese mix and reserving the rest for the top. The cream cheese will stabilize the whipped cream, and then you can enjoy this for a few days in the fridge!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Tom B:  Before I started making what we call “Natural Chocolate Delight,” I also thought whipped cream was not stable enough to last in the fridge,  but my daughter really wanted me to develop a clone for the instant pudding/Cool Whip version.  This daughter, a professional baker, assured me the actual whipped cream would do fine, and it hasn’t broken down ever in the 20 years I’ve been using it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We called this “next best thing to robert Redford” this recipe used store bought pecan sandie cookies with melted butter, for the crust, chocolate pudding as made above, cream cheese layer and same whipped cream. Sprinkled with toasted pecans. No separation of whipped cream with cream cheese incorporated. It won’t last a day in the fridge, it’s that incredible, hence the Robert Redford comparison!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was excellent. The crust, in particular, with the pecans was outstanding. And the layers of filling were perfect. My only complaint about the recipe is the size of the dish specified -- I have no idea in what world this would be enough for a 9 x 13 pan. I used a 9 x 9 and it was perfect (went all the way to the top.)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Sounds terrific, but unless you are a professional chef competing on Chopped, there is no way that the prep time for this is 20 minutes. It would really be helpful if the prep times were less aspirational and more realistic.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Oh, my!  I haven't thought about this in decades. My Mom would make it with butterscotch pudding.  It was so delicious.  Thanks for sharing and updating a classic!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We called the version that uses pudding mix Chocolate Lasagna. It’s delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It won't taste the same home-made. People feel all superior when they don't use mixes, but that was the memory your mother made. However, been looking for years for a recipe called Lemon Fluff, none I have found even slightly resemble that recipe my mother found in a magazine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
1 can evap milk
1 3 oz pkg lemon gelatin
1 cup sugar
1-1/3 cups boiling water
1/4 cup lemon juice
1-3/4 cups graham cracker crumbs
5 T butter, melted

Chill evap milk. Dissolve gelatin & sugar in boiling water, stir in lemon juice. Chill til syrupy. Mix crumbs ​& butter; save 2 T as garnish. Press remaining crumbs into 13 x 9 dish. Beat chilled milk until soft peaks form. Beat gelatin until tiny bubbles form. Fold milk into gelatin. Pour over prepared crust. Sprinkle w crumbs. Chill. Garnish.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mom made this with Cool Whip and boxed  instant pudding. It was a frequent dessert for dinner guests in the 1970s. I am excited to try the from-scratch version.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The first time I had it, it was at a Church social and ironically called Holy Smoke.  My husband still dreams some 40 years later that I will make him that dessert.  Now I will since it doesn't have instant pudding and Cool Whip.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Grew up in a house where we had German food for holidays and Italian for every day and shopped at The Co-op. Growing up, I don't think I ever had instant anything aside from skim milk (gross) and fruit flavored jello when I was sick. This desert just fixed whatever I missed. I'm so glad the first time was from scratch! Wow! Cut it in 1/2, substituted walnuts for pecans (don't like them!), and made it in an 8x8 glass Pyrex dish. Sublime!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: From scratch and now even "Better than Robert Redford" - but not as good as Paul Newman.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I didn't grow up with this dessert, but I love seeing all the names people have for it in the comments. More than one reference to Robert Redford!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: How nostalgic! This was made with instant pistachio pudding and Cool Whip in my house growing up. When my Mom wanted to be extra fancy, the top would be decorated with halved maraschino cherries.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Pistachio almond surprise comes back to my house every St Patrick's Day!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this as written (with weights) for a birthday dessert for my gluten free friend, using Measure for Measure flour.  Every layer of the dessert was wonderful and just delicious.  I did one thing differently timing-wise:  I made and assembled the dessert through the pudding layer & refrigerated overnight. I added the final whipped cream layer a couple hours before serving, just to insure a nicely whipped layer. The dessert was a big hit!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I agree with Cris - made with weights, made the pudding a day ahead, then made crust with GF flour and assembled up to the top layer, chilled overnight, finished up the top layer of cream and nuts for serving. It was inhaled (well, maybe not literally). Big hit for a crowd.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I remember this dessert being called Death by Chocolate.   At 921 calories and 64 grams of fat per serving, my conscience won't allow me to make this, no matter how delicious it might be.  A simple chocolate pudding with a dollop of whipped cream and a sprinkle of nuts will satisfy me just fine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you’re concerned about the whipped cream destabillizing try adding a teaspoon or two of piping gel in the latter stage of whipping. This will help to keep its whipped consistency for a couple of days.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I converted the original recipe to a made-from-scratch version essentially like this one years ago, and it has remained a favorite of family and friends ever since. The crust is an essential element here.  Don't skip it!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: "Extra Creamy Cool Whip" (emphasis on the Extra Creamy version) is as close to homemade whipped cream as you can get. And I will stand by that. So does America's Test Kitchen.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For me the surprise (and, to be honest, disappointment) is that what clearly appeared to me as a hot dog buried in the lower depths of this desert in the illustration photo, upon further examination and closer reading turns out to be merely chocolate pudding.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Back in the 70's and 80's in Austin Texas, there was Mike and Charlie's restaurant. "Babe's Delight" was the featured dessert. I remember the shortbread cookie layer, the cream cheese layer (tinted a soft pink), the dark chocolate layer, and the pillowy whipped cream topped with a sprinkle of toasted Texas pecans. You always knew when someone was having it for dessert  when they took the first bite: head tilts back eyes closed, huge closed mouth smile, look of utter "delight!"
Great memory!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
BEST METHOD FOR ROASTED PECANS:
Preheat oven to 400 degrees. Spread pecan halves on a cookie sheet and put in oven. Turn oven off. Nuts will be perfectly done when oven is cold.  This method I learned from Kathryn, a lady from South Georgia.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I like desserts less sweet, so I made this with half the sugar in the pudding and whipped cream, as well as twice the bittersweet chocolate. I use gluten free graham crackers (whole) instead of a baked crust. It was tasty the first day (5 hour rest), and it was amazing the following day.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Just made this for the first time for our Easter gathering and it was a hit! For the calorie conscious, we cut it into ~20 squares rather than the suggested 10 servings which was still more than enough per serving. It’s very rich so you don’t need much to get your fix.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It’s great so many people have memories and various versions of this. I personally grew up eating the boxed chocolate Jello pudding and Cool whip version and always loved it, so I’m partial to this combo of flavors and this recipe does not disappoint at all! While nostalgia has its place, I am much more interested in making something from scratch these days, which might be why I haven’t made this dessert from my childhood. Huge thanks to Lisa for a beautiful recipe! I love the pecans (as opposed to almonds I had experienced in the past), and I will absolutely never use cool whip when this whipped cream was so easy and delicious. I did not have any issue with it holding up in the fridge. Spending a little extra time making it made it all that much better!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Our grandma called this “Four-Layer Delight” and made it with butterscotch pudding. Such a treat!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was such a hit at my Oscar party :). I used the oat milk chocolate pudding recipe from The Times and almond flour in the crust. SO GOOD

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My wonderful mother-in-law, Mary,  introduced me to this dessert over 40 years ago when I married her precious only son.  She made it with butterscotch pudding and called it “Lucky If You Get A Bite”.  It was a staple at Thanksgiving dinners and family get togethers.  She has been gone for over three years now and I miss her and her magical cooking skills oh so very much.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: One of my favorite things to make when I was a kid. The title on my handwritten recipe card is The Next Best Thing to Robert Redford. My crust was made with flour, butter and pecans. I used cream cheese, non-dairy whipped topping and Jello pudding, one chocolate and one vanilla. I would layer the puddings over the cream cheese layer and swirl them. My family liked it better than the all chocolate version. To make it fancy, I would grate or shave chocolate on top. I need to make this soon.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve made this twice and both times it was delicious, and sleep-inducing! It must be the pork.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The pudding was a bit soft. Next time I’ll use less milk and maybe add some instant pudding for a more firm texture. Tasted great!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this dessert yesterday for Thanksgiving. The pudding layer didn't set that well. I followed the recipe. It was chilled overnight for at least 8 hours. Then, it was transported in the car for about 45 minutes and refrigerated again. It didn't maintain its integrity when I cut into it several hours later. What went wrong? What could I have done to make sure it set well?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Shout-out to Grandma Betty Pauls and her version of this - Better Than Sex Cake. Even better than the taste was bringing it to a work event and telling folks what it was called. Always a big hit!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This seems to be a common dessert trick: pick 3, pile up and add nuts  - whipped cream (do it yourself and you gain extra points!), caramel, some form of chocolate, peanut butter, cake batter, jam... - and bam! you have dessert.  This may be tasty to some. Scroll down the news to hear about obesity, diabetes, heart disease, etc.  And for the next trick!... put another layer of peanut butter and drizzle some chocolate syrup, maybe some of those multicolor sprinkles. Trick or treat! Yum or yuck!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Question:  recommendations on best material for the 9x13?  I have found glass burns crusts such as this and would appreciate advice from others. Metal ?  Ceramic?  What is best here.  Thanks.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Glass conducts heat differently. Lower the oven temperature by 25ºF and you should be okay. Or yes, just use a different pan.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We’re a smallish household… I wonder if a half recipe might do well in a 9-inch pie pan? The math indicates close-enough in area, but as to eating experience? Is it too runny to “sit up” as a slice of pie? Grateful to hear any thoughts!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If I were doing a half-recipe, I'd try using an 8-inch pan.  Good luck!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: How do I make this nut free and gluten free at the same time?  Two folks with allergies in the family.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Use a gluten-free graham cracker crust, for which there are many recipes online.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We called it Chocolate Yummy because my mom took it to Wednesday night potlucks at our church!! Definitely a family favorite!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For the person asking about a nut-free and gluten-free variation, you can use gluten-free graham-crackers for a graham-cracker crust and just leave out the pecans.  There are other crust options that use oat flour or oatmeal— just do a search and see what’s most appealing.  If some people want nuts, have a bowl of roasted chopped pecans on the table for sprinkling individually.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I make a similar version, but with instant pistachio pudding.  My now adult kids love it, I usually make in in a vintage Pyrex cornflower pan.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I need to substitute Splenda for the sugar for dietary reasons. I also would substitute almond flour half and half with all purpose flour for the same reason. Does anyone know how to make confectioner's sugar using Splenda?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My family enjoyed this—the origina, all store-bought, version—back in the 80s. My sister’s house mother at Vanderbilt made it for her sorority, and she called it Girdlebuster!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This dessert is good with lemon instant pudding, too.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We called this chocolate delight growing up. Instead of making the crusty from scratch, we'd make it from shortbread cookies or Pecan Sandies, then sprinkle some of the crumbs on top

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you’re looking for a stabilized cream that’s make ahead and always reliable, grab some kappa carrageenan powder (mod pantry) and use Margaret Manzke’s recipe for vanilla kappa. Beautiful texture and tastes wonderful.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I called it chocolate trifle or strawberry or banana trifle depending on content. I must admit I used instant pudding (chocolate or vanilla), a mixture of cool whip and crème cheese, graham cracker crust, and cool whip on top. On occasion, I would use ladyfingers for the layers instead of Graham cracker crust.  All this I put in a  bowl or compote where you could see all the layers.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: One correction. This is one of those recipes that should say: Serves 1-10

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My ex's mother in law was famous for chocolate sin. It was THE dessert to take to any potluck

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Could you use a gluten free baking mix for the crust?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: definitely - or your favorite gf cookie crust recipe. I learned that for gf cookie crust I have to bake it just a bit longer so it stays crisp and not soggy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Add 1 T of instant vanilla pudding per cup of whipping cream to stabilize it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We had this every family gathering. Everyone called it Sheila's dessert since my mom was the one who always made it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mom called this Guadalupe River Bottom Pudding Cake. I loved it!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In the unlikely event that every crumb doesn’t disappear quickly, this amazing  dessert can be frozen. I don’t make it often nowadays, or have leftovers when I do make it, but as I recall it’s best to serve the leftovers frozen (ideally with freshly made coffee) and eat as it thaws.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Cherry Delight was my family’s favorite version. Instead of pudding, a can of cherry pie filling on top of the cream cheese layer. Delicious. Everyone’s favorite!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: So glad Lisa modified the recipe to  eliminate the boxed pudding and store bought whipped cream. The amount of sugar can be tailored in both the home made pudding and whipped cream (which I always make from scratch.) A 'healthier' version

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Wanted to love it but just too sweet. Too much work, too much confectioner’s sugar everywhere. I tried to cut down but even with that, not too many takers here in my house.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The workflow in this recipe ensures that you will have lumps.  The “paste” you form from the cornstarch cocoa powder and eggs sits like a rock when you’re trying to blend ingredients.  Dish is a good idea but poor execution

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It's my great grandmother's cast iron in this household. No pancake tastes nearly as good if not cooked on that. I have been making something similar to this for many years, hme made pudding is lightyears better. Variant is Vanilla pudding and a layer of strawberries. For the crunchy top, use pretzels. Of course, like millions are likely asking, where is the chicken casserole recipe?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Chances are that the chocolate pudding mix will have a lower lead content than the cocoa and chocolate version so if you’re serving to children in particular, that’s a consideration.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've made pudding from scratch for years, but this one didn't set even though I followed the recipe, reached the temp range, etc. Ate a bit anyway to check the flavors, and it seems good, especially the cream cheese layer on the crust. Agree with the comment that you should top it with fresh whipped cream as the remainder used in the cream cheese layer is likely to break while the pan chills overnight.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Mine sat overnight and the cream cheese layer did not break. Some people are making up problems without trying the recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I had the same problem with the pudding. Also, the volume/weight is off on most of the ingredients. 1 1/2 cups of flour weighs 180 grams.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Lee-Lee, I think what people are pointing out is that the top layer of whipping cream, which is leftover from making the cream cheese layer is breaking when left overnight.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Use a little gelatin to stabilize whipped cream (from The New All Purpose Joy of Cooking). Sprinkle 1/2 tsp. gelatin over 1 Tbsp. cold water in a heatproof custard cup. Let stand 5 minutes to soften. Set custard cup with gelatin in a small pan with a little hot water; heat until gelatin dissolves. Beat 1 cup minus 2 Tbsp. heavy cream, with 1 Tbsp. sugar & 1/2 tsp. vanilla using electric mixer. Stir reserved 2 Tbsp. heavy cream into gelatin mixture & continue beating until stiff enough. Chill.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Love this! My mom always called it Robert Redford, lol.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this for Easter and my grand nephews' 6th birthday.  The two best cooks in the family declared it a "masterpiece" and "absolutely delicious". It was so very creamy and just the right sweetness.  Wish a piece had survived for me to take home.  Thank you, NYT.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was “Chocolate Torte” in my family, and it was a favorite. We lived outside the US when I was small and cool whip was such a treat that my parents once sat in our hotel room on a stateside visit eating it straight out of the tub. The subsequent gastrointestinal distress ensured they didn’t do that again, but I still think a dessert with cool whip is special :)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: easy whipped cream that will stay firm in the fridge for days, just add a tablespoon of instant vanilla pudding mix per cup of cream while whipping

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Growing up, I made it with butterscotch pudding.  Favorite dessert ever.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This reminds me somewhat of a simple trifle my mom made throughout our childhood. It involved boxed chocolate pudding, boxed chocolate cake, cool whip, and broken heath bar pieces. We would layer everything in a huge bowl in no particular order. I understand that the recipe here is quite different, but it's giving me the same vibe. And that vibe is: You cannot mess this up!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mom called this dessert “Holy Smoke.”

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is nice and relatively quick for a weeknight if you use instant pudding. But, it’s not “four layers.” Neither a crust nor a topping are layers. Good enough without the exaggeration.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I used to make this in a restaurant in Miami many years ago. It was called "Better Than Sex Pie." It truly was.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
For those nostalgic about Cool Whip you might be interested to know that the ingredients are:
Water, Corn Syrup, High Fructose Corn Syrup, Hydrogenated Vegetable Oil (Coconut and Palm Kernel Oils), Skim Milk, Contains Less than 2% of Líght Cream, Sodium Caseinate (from Milk), Natural and Artificial Flavor, Xanthan and Guar Gums, Modified Food Starch, Polysorbate 60, Sorbitan Monostearate, Sodium Polyphosphate, and Beta Carotene for color.
Enjoy!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In our family, we called this, “Mississippi Mud”. But I’ve tried looking for this recipe for years online and couldn’t find what we had growing up. This recipe is it! Although, nothing was homemade in my mom’s house, she’d use whipped topping and instant pudding. Can’t wait to try the scratch version.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've never figured out why people want to keep a skin from forming on pudding.  The skin is the best part!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delicious!!  I cut the sugar down in each component and it was perfect.  Definitely a keeper.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We call this Mississippi Mud, and it's appropriate. The river is so cloudy and the clear water blurs with the silt river bottom--much like the layers of this dessert will if left in the fridge for too many days. But the flavor is impeccable and very unlike the river water!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I have  a version of this called Lemon Lush which just substitutes lemon pudding for chocolate.   The one with chocolate was varied somewhat where I live (Indy) with a chocolate crust and called Dirt Pudding.  These bring back memories of pitch ins, parties, and family gatherings!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: People in Arkansas call this delicious dessert Possum Pie.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mom called this The Next Best Thing to Robert Redford then we shortened it to Robert Redford. 😆

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For Marian, I don't know if this is what you remember, but the 4-Day Coconut Cake recipe can be found here: https://www.justapinch.com/recipes/dessert/cake/4-day-refrigerated-coconut-cake-2.html

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Yes! Slight variation, but my family called this dessert, Next Best Thing to Robert Redford!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mom made something like this too-but it was called Mennonite Pudding. No clue where the name originated from other than we had many Mennonite families where I grew up and lots of moms sharing recipe cards. This is one dessert we all ask for regularly though!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Marcia Bolles—love the idea of pistachio pudding, and how about pistachio layered with chocolate or white chocolate pudding!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
We called this "Better Than Tom Selleck" growing up.

Guess that tells you how old I am! I am looking forward to making it again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My Mama called this Idiot's Delight. We loved it with chocolate pudding. She used Cool Whip for the top layer, and also made it sometimes with butterscotch pudding which was my little brother's favorite. My sister-in-law still makes it for him.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Unlike others responding, I have never made or even tasted this….but I’d like to!!! Is it cut and served like a pie, or is it spooned into a plate/bowl? Not sure how much it sets in the fridge. Thanks.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Mine sat overnight and I cut it in to squares when I served it. They came out nicely and I didn't serve it with a spoon so it was much like a pie.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I grew up with this too, but my mom's version was with oreos instead of pecans. It's such a big hit at potlucks with kids and adults. Leftovers make a great breakfast ;)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Any ideas on how to make this diary free?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe looks a lot like a dessert my mother used to make 50 years ago, and which my cousins still rave about. It had a walnut crust, a layer of chocolate pudding, and was made with Cool Whip.  She also had another dessert, equally famous, with yellow cake, vanilla pudding, canned crushed pineapple, Cool Whip, and walnuts.  I've never found the recipes, but I suspect they both came from the inside of a Cool Whip lid.  It would be heresy to say it out loud, but this recipe looks better!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Like reader Mary Robins, I have been looking for a *Lemon Fluff* recipe. I think it’s a dessert with layers similar to this. My dad says it’s his favorite dessert from his childhood, but we never got the recipe before my grandmother passed.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mother in law makes this with lemon pudding and calls it Lemon Lush...its one of my Husband's favorites!!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It reminds me of something we called "striped delight"

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My grandmother used to make this but with butterscotch pudding.  She called it butterscotch torte, and it reminds me of my childhood.  I like chocolate pudding better, and make it in the summer for my kids.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: As many people have noted, this is the more labor-intensive version of a popular dessert made with Cool Whip and jello pudding called Chocolate Delight (and other names).  The point being, you can sub in any pudding mix (lemon, butterscotch, vanilla, pistachio). You can also swap out the pecans for pulverized oreos, shortbread cookies, pretzels, graham crackers, peanuts etc. If you aren't eating it all in one sitting, the Cool Whip layer lasts longer than homemade whipped cream.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My Grandma called this “Scrumptious “ and it too had boxed ingredients. I have updated it almost exactly like you have, sometimes vanilla custard, sometimes chocolate, this is my 25 year old son’s most requested birthday treat. Thanks for sharing!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Is there a lower sugar version? All that sugar makes my heart pound just thinking about it!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Experiment and figure it out. I would lessen the confectioner's sugar by at least 1/4 cup. Lessening the sugar in the pudding is something you could do to taste, if you want a more bitter chocolate effect.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This sounds a lot like my mother’s 1970’s era “chocolate cream pie.” Baked store bought pie shell filled w instant chocolate pudding and topped w fresh whipped cream. Can still waste it now. And then there was her “green cake” made w pistachio pudding mix….

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: OH, THANK YOU for the delightful trip down memory lane. Somewhere I have Aunt Susan's recipe for Pistachio Cake, using a box of pistachio pudding mix and I think a yellow cake mix. It was delicious and me and my Cradick cousins found it to be the height of class.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This came with crushed heath bars at my house. Mmmmm.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: One of my absolute favourite childhood desserts, always made in the metal 9x13 pan (for family get togethers and there were usually leftovers to take home, thank goodness). Mom used to make an alternate version as well, using lemon pudding instead of chocolate - heavenly! I’ll give this recipe a try next time my folks visit.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Just stabilize the whipped cream with unflavored gelatin powder.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This started out in pistachio in our Michigan home in the 1970s called Pistachio Delight. Then someone brought it to a party in chocolate and yes some called it Robert Redford but we just called it Chocolate Delight. I’m sure mom used instant pudding and non dairy topping but it was delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For the crust, use crushed Ritz crackers tossed with melted butter. Press into bottom of pan and bake lightly. Let cool. Continue with recipe. Enjoy!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Too much work and time for such an 'ok' dessert. Not really a company worthy outcome, and too much fuss for the family!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Does anyone else remember a pistachio version with mini marshmallows and crushed pineapple mixed in?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The best version is with pistachio pudding and a crushed walnut crust. Steer clear of graham crackers.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mom made the “store bought” version and called it “Holy Smokr.”

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Moving to the South as a young bride in the 70's, my mother-in-law made this often, it was called Million Dollar Dessert, made with baked crust, instant Jell-o Pudding, Cool Whip and lots of pecans. Wonderful memories!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Ok to switch out the all purpose flour for a gluten free type?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I did and it was fine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A nightmare🤯. Butter in crust separated when it baked and ended up with half on top, melted, and a thin layer of toffee.  Triple checked and measurements were right. Ugh!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you are weighing the ingredients, the amounts are off.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Why is there so much butter in the crust? I thought it was going to be like a press-in crust, but the dough is very wet. It bakes to a fine texture, but still tastes very buttery.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A bit complicated (more than 2 mixing bowls needed) for me and my 10-year-old assistant, but with one cheat (boxed pudding) and a St.Patty’s Day variation (green whipped cream and rainbow sprinkles), it was a total hit. Note: our gf crust made it IMPOSSIBLE to spread the cream cheese filling, so we artfully glooped it with no disappointment in flavor.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I had a problem with the salt instructions.  In each part of the recipe that calls for salt, it says to use half as much if using coarser salt.  That doesn't make sense to me, because if you use the same volume of coarse and fine salt, the coarser salt will be less in mass.  So why use half the volume when it's already less?
In the crust, I would use finer salt anyway, because the crystals will stay the same size as when put in, since there is not enough liquid to dissolve them.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Didn't have quite enough whipped cream once I took 2 cups for the cream cheese filling to make the 4th layer....sort of a 3.5 layer surprise! But still very yummy!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Use stovetop pudding mix with 1/2 the milk called for on the package. Better texture than instant.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We called it ‘Better Than Sex’ in our household!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Make the pudding and the crust a day before assembly as they take time to make them and cool. 

After the crust cools, make sure it’s wrapped/covered until assembly or it will get too hard. Next time I make this I will cut the cooking time for the crust to keep it softer and not crunchy. Great overall dessert!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this many times, but with banana in the pudding.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Please don’t respond with hate but I have a question: I want to make this as a nostalgic treat for my mother-in-law who just moved into a facility. She always tries to convince herself she eats a “low sugar” diet (imprinted with Shasta soda and Dr. Atkins from the 1970s I think!) but doesn’t really have a health issue. Does the sugar in this recipe serve a purpose for anything other than sweetness - like structure or volume? Or can I use a measure-for-measure sugar substitute like Splenda or Swerve?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mom used to make this when I was growing up in Central Illinois. It was called Chocolate Lush. I’m pretty sure her version has graham cracker crumbs in the crust and then plenty of pecans, chocolate pudding mix, and whipped cream or cool whip.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Would it work to use almond flour instead of all purpose flour?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I don't see why not. My original recipe used flour, pecans and butter. Almond flour may make the crust a bit denser, but it should still be good.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The version I used to make had a layer of cut up brownies and was topped with mashed Heath Bars. It is obscenely good.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was a standard dessert when I was a kid, but Mom just made a basic crust and used box pudding and Cool Whip.  The cream cheese layer was pretty much the same.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In the South we call this Possum Pie. Delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Back home in Arkansas we called it Four-Layer Delight. Also great with home-made coconut custard instead of chocolate. If you are one of "those" people, try it with pumpkin-pie spiced pudding at Thanksgiving.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was my mom’s go-to recipe for holidays and potlucks and such when I was a kid. She called it chocolate swirl.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My guests loved this. Made a lot and I sent it home for a family gathering they have — they were so happy. No really my kind of dessert but fantastic for a big gathering.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
My fiancé’s family in Houston calls it Floor Pie, after someone dropped the tin at Christmas. It landed safely with collective relief: “Still good, though.”

A truly worthy household recipe, best eaten standing in front of an open fridge. When I finally make Floor Pie myself, I’ll probably add pretzels and an alarming amount of salt. BIG SHOUTOUT TO FLOOR PIE.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was fabulous as written.  Delightful nutty crust, although it stuck to my glass dish.  I'll lightly butter the dish next time and not press the dough too much.  And I plan to try Alton Brown's "Instant Chocolate Pudding Mix".  It has a higher ratio of cocoa to cornstarch and calls for Dutch Processed Cocoa - a darker color and more chocolate sounds good to me!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We called it "Husband's Delight"

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was delicious but the crust stuck to the bottom of the pan and was hard to get out.  Anyone else have that issue or a suggestion of how to avoid it?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: As I mentioned earlier, this is my favorite dessert ever and I'm making it today for Thanksgiving, but with Melissa Clark's pumpkin butterscotch custard.  I'm asking NYT to improve the title of this recipe, as it's hard to find on the website.  I learned it as a 5-layer dessert, not 4-and some call it "Chocolate Delight" or even "Robert Redford"-but "4 Layer Surprise" is a name without detail and eminently forgettable.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
We called this Chocolate Yummy in the 80s, and loved it with all the boxed stuff and Cool
Whip. Thanks for this version! Now I can make it living outside the US.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Ooh! My grandma used to make this with pistachio pudding in place of the chocolate (and cool whip, of course). I'm looking forward to trying this version!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I call it ‘better than Robert Redford but not quite as good as Paul Newman’ when I serve it at my annual Oscar party. I use The NY Times oat milk chocolate pudding recipe (bc it’s easier) and I use almond flour for the crust to make it GF. It’s the only dessert I’ve ever made that disappears completely by the end of the evening!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Mom always used Pecan Sandies in her pistachio version.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My husband cannot have any grapefruit because it interferes with his blood thinner medication. Would orange peel and juice work?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: can he have minneola tangerines? that plus lime might be good. Sweet oranges would be very different from the tartness of grapefruit

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You really can use any citrus here, keeping in mind that the sweetness of the result will relate to the sweetness/acidity of your choice. Blood oranges would be a great choice for achieving a result similar to grapefruit, since they're often a bit tarter than navel oranges, and bring some color along to boot.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you can find meyer lemons, they would work beautifully here. They're about the size and color of regular lemons but more round and with smoother skin. Their juice tastes very much like tangerine juice. Or try tangerines.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
In my catering days (many decades ago), I would prepare the base ahead of time and only whip the egg whites and fold in at the last moment. This made it much less of a last minute affair.
Also, I think blood oranges would be great!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There is only a single tsp of grapefruit juice in each souffle! It will not interfere with blood thinner meds in this amount.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve made cheese fondue for decades using a recipe from Jacques Pépin in which one starts with a simple béchamel, then adds yolks to the thickened béchamel. This recipe seems to go about it backwards - adding hot milk and cream to flour and yolks. I suppose it may not matter as the end result is the same but is one method better than the other?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Wondering if you could use blood oranges that are in season now and would a tartness and colour.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Sure! Why not, they're certainly less tart than lemon or grapefruit (if you were concerned about the grapefruit being too tart). However, if you really want to see blood oranges shine, this is my favorite dessert to star them: https://tasty.co/recipe/blood-orange-chocolate-olive-oil-cake

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Two grapefruits are required for the 2 tablespoons of zest called for, not the juice.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Fell in love with this recipe for its aromatics and versatility. But, encountered 2 problems: the deflation of my egg whites after adding the citrus sugar and the uneven lining of the molds. Both issues arose due to the moisture in the citrus sugar. When I make these again, I will use dehydrated citrus peel in the sugar to line the molds. And, I will use plain sugar in the egg whites. I will use all of the citrus sugar in the custard-base mixture, which can accommodate the moisture.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Looks delicious. Wondering if this could be made in one large souffle dish. I don't have 5 small ramekins. I se another similar question.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Any citrus juice would work! If you want any pink hue, try a blood orange, but I doubt it will matter very much for such a small amount. Frankly, why the recipe calls for two grapefruits for five teaspoons of juice is a mystery.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Frankly, you'll need 2 grapefruits for the required amount of zest and juice. No mystery at all.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
You might separate your eggs individually over a cup so if something goes wrong, it's a one egg problem not a bowl of eggs problem.

Any suggestions about making a single large souffle using this recipe??

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is a myth re being unable to have grapefruit/juice while on statin. A serving of GF now and then (minimal here) is nothing to think about at all. If you love GF daily, as my spouse does, it will likely reduce dose of statin you need as GF results in an more drug in your system. For daily GF lovers, have your liver and lipids checked in 3-6 wks following regular grapefruit ingestion, until you demonstrate stable results on any dose changes indicated. Less drug…YAY!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was my first attempt at a soufflé and it turned out GREAT!    I made it with blood orange and given that it was Valentine’s Day I topped it with fresh raspberries once out of the oven

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Strained custard is referenced at the end but I see no instruction to strain it. I presume to strain out any cooked egg?  I’ll use naval oranges and a vanilla bean.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I don't usually use flour in my soufles, is it necessary in this recipe?
Also, it appears that you don't use a bain marie when you bake them in the oven.  Is there a reason?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Since I have lived at 5480 feet above sea level I cannot get a soufflé to rise. How can I adapt a soufflé recipe when cooking in Denver?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am lactose-intolerant and can’t find lactose-free heavy cream. Would using all whole milk work?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There are products that break down the lactose in liquid dairy products and make them digestible for people with lactose intolerance. Just search on the internet for a brand name. You can buy one on-line or at your neighborhood pharmacy. If you use one of these products to make this recipe, it would have to be added to the heavy cream well before you assemble the souffles (probably at least 24 hrs. in advance) since it takes some time for lactose to break down.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You may also consider https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1015594-lemon-souffle which has a similar flavor profile but no cream/milk.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Does anyone have a suggestion for the size ramekin to use if you are going to make one large soufflé? I have a few options, is there a rule of thumb?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Do you think a different powder could substitute for the flour?  Arrowroot?  Rice flour?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
The pepper was way too strong for all six folks who tasted this.  
It only filled 3 8 Oz. ramekins.  Disappointing.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: To avoid getting yolk accidentally in the whites, separate the eggs in a ramekin first, then add to the rest of the whites.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Nice desert, but it lacks character. I would suggest a sauce to bring out the citrus flavor and give it a little more pizzazz, or perhaps a bit more grapefruit juice.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The quality of the black pepper you use will make a big difference with "pizzaz". Also, serve it with a Grand Marnier-vanilla ice cream for a very grown-up "creamsicle" flavor!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This turned out perfectly; I wouldn’t change a thing!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: So I made this today to celebrate the Olympics opening ceremony. My first ever souffle!! The flavors are AMAZING. I used one large dish instead of ramekins. Baked for 15 min and got a beautiful golden brown top. But had to put it back in for another 10-15 min. I was worried but it finished beautifully. And the dish was licked clean! We are at about 3600ft elevation and our oven is consistent. Wondering if anyone has suggestions on the timing?? So excited to try this again. Thanks!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this - my first souffle ever - to celebrate the Olympics opening ceremony. Incredible flavor. Wow! So good. Baked in one dish instead of ramekins. Started with 15min per the recipe but had to out it back in for almost another 15. I would love some insight about why that happened. Regardless, the dish was licked clean.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Not to scorn your pie crust recipe, but to save time couldn’t this recipe use puff pastry to hold the pimento?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I always add a little Worcestershire sauce to my pimento cheese.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Where on the menu does this belong? Never mind; obviously it replaces everything.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Would following this recipe but making miniature hand pies/turnovers also work?  (Seems they'd be a great hors d'oeuvre.)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I just made a Galette des Rois using store-bought puff pastry. Never again! Bland flakes of dry pastry crust are SO unappetizing! I prefer my own imperfect attempts -  rich, buttery and more like a pie crust to wrap a great filling.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @aunt reena. Maybe use Dufour frozen puff pastry made with butter. An excellent product not to be scorned.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Gale agree! Defour puff pastry is the magic! Worth every penny.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For the lazy amongst us, would I be better off subbing a puff pastry crust or a filo crust?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Go for the crust- it’s so worth it. I’m an amateur, and even I can make this crust. Hint- there’s a video of Lisa Donovan’s technique on YouTube. Search Lisa Donovan Perfect Pie Crust.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Michael: Thank you for the suggestion. Here is the YouTube link: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kpRbR62KOpw

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Thank goodness! My first thought on reading this recipe was "why on earth didn't they include a video of the pie crust technique?"

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Made this using good quality puff pastry. Followed recipe but should have only baked at 400 for 20 minutes - it was dark brown, bordering on burnt, at given temps and timing.  Otherwise good.

This is not something to cook when you are hungry, with the amount of time needed for chilling, freezering and cooling. Also I would use a bit more pimento and less salt.

Otherwise excellent and will make again!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: No one, and I mean NO ONE, makes better pie dough than Lisa Donovan. Can you used purchased puff? Sure. Should you? Absolutely not - this dough is heaven with anything in it, really.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Recipe calls for sharp cheddar and aged cheddar, with the only specification is that one should be white. But aren't these basically the same cheese?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Same cheese as long as they are aged identically. One may be sharper and more intensely flavored if aged longer. Cook’s choice. “There is no difference in taste when comparing white and yellow cheddar of the same age* – the only difference is the annatto food coloring.” https://youngsdairy.com/whats-difference-white-cheddar-vs-yellow-cheddar/#:~:text=There%20is%20no%20difference%20in,our%20eyes”%20is%20very%20true.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I agree with Jan. No difference between aged and sharp.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this for a party and it disappeared. Personally found it too salty and would omit the salt from the cheese pimento mix next time. The crust method is worth following but not so different from my usual way of using a large bowl, shmooshing the butter cubes into the flour with fingers, and a quick knead in the same bowl, thus avoiding a big counter cleanup. The baking pan was too large for the freezer so I put it in the refrigerator for an hour or two before baking which seemed to work fine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Eegulleye I haven’t finished making this yet but I should have tasted the pimento cheese before adding so much salt!! Way too salty!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: “Two great tastes that taste great together!”

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Excellent, the pastry is flaky like a croissant. I'll use the pastry recipe for all my flaky pastry needs.
I added bacon to the stuffing. Came out very tasty. A keeper.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I'm with everyone, whether we could subsitute ready made puff pastry?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Yes, I guess you could substitute ready made puff pastry but you'll miss half the taste. This pastry recipe is out of this world, very flaky, croissant like.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was fabulous, everyone including the not quite 1 year old loved it. Easiest pastry I have ever rolled. It was in the freezer overnight so I did leave it to thaw for about 30 minutes before baking. This makes a lot. Next time I will make two smaller 'pies' saving one to serve as a side with tomato soup and salad.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was divine, and will be using the dough method for all future pie crusts. I made this as an appetizer for a party and ate a leftover piece for lunch cold with a salad- yum.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Could one use frozen puff pastry instead of pie crust?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Am I missing the pimento (pimiento) ??  Is the red pepper a substitute ??  If so, that's an airball for true pimiento cheese

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Pimiento is a pepper. https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pimiento

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The red pepper is the pimento! Fabulous duo, I'm going to do this with puff pastry as another reviewer did!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Sorry, say I. Way too complicated. Summarize: a pie crust of your choosing filled with pimento cheese. And I don't dislike mayonnaise, but I think I do dislike it after the opening 4 paragraph. Pimento cheese could be OK but maybe not this way

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Could you replace the ice water in the crust with ice cold vodka? I always use vodka in my fruit pie crusts

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mind is blown. This Pacific Northwesterner makes PC every summer in hopes of sharing the magic. I have endured eye rolling but now, I have an all year version.  Thanks for making the PC as it should be- without cream cheese.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Not sure there’s a difference between “sharp” and “aged” cheddar - can someone explain? Thank you

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: White Cheddar rarely comes labeled "sharp" or "extra sharp."  Yellow cheese does.  I believe the  writer is looking for a tang for this recipe, so look for those labels.  BTW:  "extra sharp" cheese is dry so it doesn't melt as well as "sharp" cheese does.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My spouse and I substituted the Lee bros. pimento cheese recipe (as southerners we feel like we know good pimento cheese) and made this as a galette. Delicious but v. rich, and I'm not sure pimento cheese needs to be dressed up this way. It's plenty good on crackers or sandwich bread. The NYT ran the Lees' recipe: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1017354-pimento-cheese

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hi Tim! The double layer crust really helps cut the intensity of the cheese and encourages party bites instead of meal size slices as a galette might - it's all about balance. A galette, to my mind, would be too rich, I agree. Try it as suggested. And I 100% agree about the Lee Brothers! Wonderful recipe for pimento. Wonderful guys, too. Happy baking! :)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This woman is a wizard. Perfect recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Excellent. I needed 8 tbsp water before crust would begin to come together.  Had to refrigerate in middle of processing. Needed an extra 5 mins in my oven to get browned. But all good in the end. Can’t wait to try this crust recipe with other ingredients inside!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I think this would be great for Pie Day.  3/14.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: First time making this dough and it came out beautifully!  Did not do an initial bake at 450. Froze for 1 hour and then baked at 425 convection for 25 minutes.  Fluted pastry wheel gave a professional edge.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: not to diss your recipe for pimento, you could sub in for a pimento spread from Trader Joe's which is delicious

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I was wondering if you might suggest other fillings for this magical dough.
It was a fascinating process, so elegant and sensual.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For those of us who have horrid, small side-by-side freezer sections, does anyone have tips about how to freeze a larger pie/tart like this if you don't have that width in your freezer? And as I'm typing this, I think probably make two smaller tarts, but I'd welcome suggestions!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
put it outside in current freezing temps! protect it from being carried off by the wee little beasties in a cooler or lidded plastic container.
Or, ask a neighbor or friend who might have space in their freezer and make a smaller version in exchange

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: mystiknitter, you're my hero! My closest neighbors in my building have the same terrible freezer, but I'll try stashing the pie outside in a cooler!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I think this is great but for an easy hit, I'll use pre-prepped puff.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you were to do the unthinkable and use a prepared puff pastry, what amount would you need to make this?  Thank you!  I am going to wait until summer when tomatoes are perfect in North Carolina!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This is a game changer - really really tasty.
Puff is the way to go for rank amateurs like myself.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This would go good with broccoli. Like those flavored croissants they have at the coffee shop.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am gluten intolerant so used 2 rolled out Schar puff pastries. The scratch pimento cheese spread is divine cold and delicious baked. I am adding this to treats I will bring to parties.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was fantastic.  The crust is unbelievable.  Thanks for the tip about watching Lisa's YouTube video.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I love pimento cheese I make my own using Miracle Whip (MW) (ok, I know, please no groans) I wonder how this would taste with MW instead if mayonnaise? 
I do love this crust easy and fast and good..

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: if freezing per directions, should I increase the baking time?   More than 5 minutes at the higher temp?additional time after turning the oven down?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: What kind of hot sauce do folks prefer for the pimento?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Disappointing. I love pimiento cheese. Used puff pastry.  this was just too greasy and oily.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am a Louisiana native. I've made and eaten pralines (pecan candy) all my life. The first thing about this recipe that I find odd is that it takes 8 hours and more. If your candy takes that long to set, you've not cooked it long enough, 30 minutes or less. The tip about using salt to cut some of the acute sweetness is a good one. But what are pralines if not the holy grail of sweet. Last, we use a can of sweetened condensed milk instead of cream. That cream is why it takes 8 hours--too wet.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I agree that 8 hours is 'way too long.  I make them with evaporated milk (and some Karo syrup).  They are firmed up in 2 hours or less.

As for making them less sweet, that borders on heresy for a Southerner.  Pralines should be (as a wise person once put it) "so sweet they make your teeth ache."

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @C.K.W. and @James: Both of you made suggestions for substituting something sweet for the cream. Are your substitutes for both the cream and the sugar? Or, do you also add the recipe's sugar?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @C.K.W. I totally agree - 8 hours is wild! When we've made them (my dad is from new Orleans), if the first one hasn't firmed up by the time the last one is out of the pan, they weren't cooked long enough. We scrape them up, put everything back on the stove and cook it some more. I've only ever used evaporated milk, never heavy cream.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I lived in New Orleans for 47 years and the only pralines I make are in the microwave. They are delicious and disappear immediately after cooking. My daughter in law made 400 for favors at her wedding. Just cook 1 box of light brown sugar and 8 ounces of whipping cream for 11 minutes on high in the microwave. Add one stick of butter, 1 teaspoon of vanilla flavoring, and chopped pecans, whatever amount you want. Pour out on wax paper on your granite or marble counter. Let cool for hours.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Dianne do you stir before pouting them out?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hm. The article mentions that good pralines contain pecans that are “thoughtfully toasted.” My southern family emphatically agrees: please toast the pecans (thoughtlessly or thoughtfully, just don’t burn ‘em) first! It makes a difference.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I take 4 pecan halves, place them at right angles and pop a caramel on top.  Bake briefly to soften the caramel and spread it out over the nuts. Then cover with melted chocolate, either milk or dark, and let set.  Easy pecan dixies/turtles/whatever you call them.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: That sounds delicious, but do people ever call that a praline?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This sounds like a turtle? but NOT a praline.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
They aren't kidding about using a big-enough pot.  As the mixture boils it will double or more in volume.  I had some boil over on me once.  Cleaning caramelized sugar off your stove top is something you do NOT want to do.

Also keep in mind that you will adding a lot of pecans to the pan, so plan accordingly.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The accompanying article gave me back a memory of my mom who I miss. Her favorite pralines were in a basket by the register at Country Pride, a little cafe, appropriately, in the country near Amarillo, TX where she raised me, and where her dad’s family had a dairy. I can see her in my mind unwrapping and nibbling on it while holding the wobbly wheel as the car bounced down the dirt road home. Here's to scrappy single moms who give their kids the world and find joy in small simple joys like these

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made them as written. They hardened nicely within two hours (not eight). They were, in a word, amazing. I'm chucking all plans to make a complicated cookie as a gift since I know that people will fall over with joy once they taste these pralines. What I'll do differently: set out all ingredients in measure and order so that there will be no fumbling in the making because once the mixture begins to boil there is no time to dither before it goes way too hot. Thank you so much for this recipe!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There is no way anyone who LOVES pralines can have them last -- tin box or no.  Skip Step 5 say I and deposit them in your mouth asap.  You'll be transported to heaven.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Sugar free Keto pralines would lead to excommunication and revocation of your Cajun card in Louisiana. 
We only make them for holidays and special occasions and if you can’t have one or two during Christmas season because you don’t want to break your keto diet please don’t waste good pecans.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I will be making this KETO  -- sugar free using an allulose sweetener  and a very low heat.  Have had great success with carmelizing the allulose and decent flavor..  Go slow.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Cook too long and you'll get a stiff mess in the pot. As you get close to temperature; Listen to your pot! The instant you can hear the scraping of the sugar on the sides; you're ready to scoop out the mixture onto the parchment paper. Wait too long and it will become too stiff.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I use the Joy of Cooking recipe which calls for buttermilk and baking soda instead of cream. But I am from Texas and this version reminds me of those at a neighborhood Mexican restaurant of my childhood. A little more air and the buttermilk corrals the sweetness. Big hit every Christmas.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Really easy and delicious. Made exactly as per recipe. Use a smaller scoop than you’d think as they do spread. Toast pecans at 350 for 4 min then stir and 4 more minutes. Stir mixture to cool off for a couple minutes before scooping. Cooled to solid in less than 2 hrs. Placed them in individual little cello wrappers and will be placing into my holiday gift baskets. Will definitely repeat!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I tried substituting eagle brand sweetened condensed milk for cream.  Total disaster, they came out drier than a popcorn fart.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this recipe as written and these were wonderful pralines--better than purchased in the French Quarter!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've read that pralines should be cooked to 234-240 degrees. I cooked to 236 and they set up within 24 hours. I sprinkled them lightly with Maldon sea salt. Best praline recipe ever...no hard and sugary texture!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
My family (from Louisiana) makes a very similar recipe, but after you take the caramel off the heat and add vanilla, you beat the caramel for 30+ minutes just until stiff peaks form. Then stir in pecans, scoop onto wax paper, and refrigerate for as long as you can manage to keep your hands off of them.  
Mixing the caramel makes a much softer, melt-in-your-mouth texture. Decadent.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Shawnee Barton, the minute I saw that recipe I thought of my mother, who adored pralines! She raised three kids on her own, and if she were here today I would make her a batch. Brown sugar, butter, pecans, and cream—what’s not to like? This sounds like a good recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Can you use coconut cream as a dairy alternative and have it “set up” properly?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I had never made pralines before.  I made them today following the recipe exactly as written.  I originally planned to make them as gifts. I think I've changed my mind.  They are heavenly.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: These are awesome!  With all the Christmas baking I’d r7n out of vanilla, but I had vanilla beans. I opened two and scraped them into a T of bourbon. Delish!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made a nut free version of these using broken up pretzels, the waffle shaped butter snaps from Snyders.  This recipe was very easy, just make sure you set everything out before you start cooking, measure out ingredients and use a pan that's tall enough.  I was using a large soup pot, thought it was too wide to register the thermometer reading, switched to my saucepan, then had to switch back to the soup pot because it bubbles up high.  When in doubt use the bigger pan.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Beware of cooking time! I wanted to make sure they would set, so I boiled them for the full 12 minutes. At 10 minutes I had a smooth glossy mixture. At 11 minutes the butter began to separate and I ended up with a gritty fudge mixture that hardened in the pot. So disappointed - would have appreciated a warning about over cooking.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I thought 8 hours had to be a typo. Mine are ready to eat after 15 minutes of cooling. My mother's recipe uses brown sugar and evaporated milk.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve ever owned a candy thermometer. Any tips for managing the recipe without one?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Get one. They're inexpensive and a must if you're going to make candy. An instant read is useable but inconvenient.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I make mine with butter vanilla brownsugar and cream, melt, bring to boil, boil 1 minute and then add sifted powdered sugar and pecans.  Done in 5 minutes.  Maybe not as shiny as those in New Orleans but amazing taste and quick quick quick.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made these for the first time, and because I was in a hurry, I cooled them in the fridge.  They were absolutely great that night, but the next day they were grainy!!  Was it b/c of the refrigeration, or something else? I live at 5500ft altitude.  Maybe they sensed my midwestern roots and had a revolt!  Any ideas?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've made this recipe multiple times - tweaking it each time. Be careful of the salt. Reduce it if you don't want them to taste quite salty. The baking soda isn't necessary. I like them better without. Cooking until 246 can make them gritty when they cool, instead of smooth and you risk burning the sugar. 240 or a little more is sufficient. Watch for the color you want and take off the heat when you get it, once you've reached 240.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @abby they were gritty because they cooked too long. You just need to cook to 240, not 246.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I use buttermilk, not cream. Found the recipe in a N.O. cookbook.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If I. Don’t have a cast iron pan what should I use?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I am sure a regular pan would also work - there are so many that one can use!  As the chef said in the NYT Article, she is experimenting with an old recipe.  I have been cooking/baking for years, and have never used a cast iron pan to bake a cake.  But  I will try, just for the experience.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Everyone should have at least one cast iron pan and this size is perfect.  I use mine so much I just leave it on top of the stove.  Buy one.  You won't regret it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Whenever a recipe calls for citrus zest and sugar, I peel the zest with a very sharp vegetable peeler, add the zest along with the sugar in the recipe into a food processor; a few pulses later you have full flavored orange or lemon sugar. For this recipe, you could then add the vanilla, fennel seeds and salt and just pulse a couple more times. Transfer sugar mixture to the bowl of a mixer, and proceed with step 4. Then make the orange puree in the orange scented food processor.  Easy!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Phyllis- this was an excellent suggestion- thank you!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I didn't have any oranges so I used fish. I didn't have any flour so I used Panko. I omitted the Chantilly cream. I skipped the food processor and fried the breaded fillets on the stovetop. Good use for a 10" cast iron pan

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I made this this morning. I gave it a full 50-minute bake. The toothpick did not come out  "clean"  I pulled it as I was leary about overbaking it in the heated cast iron pan. 
I followed the directions exactly and did not peel the second orange - this does give the cake a real chewiness from the rind (not too bitter per other comments) - I suggest "roughly" peeling both oranges - leaving 25% of the pith/peel. Skip the nuts as the long bake time makes them soft/chewy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Reminds me a little of Giuliano Bugialli's orange sformato. For a vegan version, I like the "orange poppy lazy loaf" which uses whole oranges  at the blog gretchensveganbakery dot com. She was a professional and owned a bakery in NJ and then walked away from butter, eggs and dairy, closed up shop and has created wonderful vegan recipes online. I've made the lazy orange loaf a few times, and am excited to play with adding some elements from this recipe (the semolina, walnuts and fennel).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Step 2 says to zest one of the oranges to add to the sugar, so that one will not have any peel on it.  The other will.  So I could wash both oranges well before using.
Then you slice off the stem end of both oranges, cut them up into pieces (I would remove any seeds) then whiz it all up in a food processor into a kind of slurry.
Tip:  rub your zest/sugar hands together before rinsing for a softening & fragrant exfoliation!  Your hands will thank you.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: When I make the whole lemon tart on this site, if the lemon has a thick pith, I carefully zest then sloppily peel most but not all of the pith off (discard excess pith), remove the seeds. If a lemon has thin pith, I just seed it and go with the recipe. When I’ve ignored this and chucked in the whole lemons with thick pith and seeds, it is inedible. Keep in mind that the sugar in the recipe is expecting to counter some of the bitterness from the pith. I’d imagine similar rules here.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Whole oranges with skin, definitely. Check out Samantha Severiatne (sp?) Recipe for whole orange snack cake on this site. Delicious with a great depth of orange flavor. As for cast iron, you also can't beat the skillet apple cake on this site, the cake is delish and the caramel frosting to die for.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: About making the orange purée for this cake: cut the ends and discard. Using a potato peeler, peel the orange skin and set aside. Now you will have a naked orange with the white pith on top. Using a sharp knife, remove the pith and reveal the orange. Discard the pith. Cut the orange in quarters and remove all the seeds to discard them. Now you have the orange peel along with tbd orange flesh. Purée these two things for your batter. The whole operation takes a minute or two but well worth it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this cake twice already. First time I followed the recipe to the letter and it was fantastic. On second bake, I went fully left and opted to not only INCLUDE the ends of the orange but also charred some of the branches from the tree which bore my citrus and included the soot with the dry ingredients. The resulting cake was inedible. Wouldn’t recommend.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Because of the cleaning methods suggested for cast iron I have resorted to keeping two pans — one for savory and another for my favorite sweet items like Dutch Baby pancakes. When I retrieved it to use for this cake it already smelled of vanilla and lemon instead of Moroccan spices.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Lots of comments about oranges and seeds. The recipe specifically calls for NAVEL oranges - which don't have seeds.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I read the instruction to 'paddle all the livelong day' and it made me wonder. Usually, one does not beat cake batters all the livelong day because that develops gluten and makes for a tough cake.

I also wondered about the variability of oranges: some have very little pith and some have a thick layer.  Which oranges were used?  

This recipe could use a bit more calibration in order to be friendly for all bakers.  (Disclaimer:  I didn't make this because I had questions.)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hot tip for leftovers if you have any.  This cake is delicious but in a small household like mine, I end up with a stale bit leftover a few days in.  I found a use for it that’s very satisfying- croutons!   I cut the stale cake into small cubes and dried them in a cookie sheet in the oven.  An arugula salad with blood orange, celery, blot peppers, cinnamon pecans, and a fig balsamic topped with croutons made from this cake was DELICIOUS.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is absolutely delicious. Highly recommend the toasted fennel its a very nice addition to the cake. The combination of toasted walnuts with buttered orange in the cake is so addicting. My cake had a nice crunchy top but was a little clumpy under the top layer and didnt come out of the skillet very clean. I probably didnt do something right but would totally make this again, loved it!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Thank you!  I have been searching in vain for someone commenting on whether they liked the recipe once they made it. Appreciate it!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This cake is incredible. Complex, tull of orange flavor, deeply moist. And yes, puree the whole oranges, skin, pith and all. Zesting one orange leaves a lot of the peel. Use it. There's plenty of sugar to counter the bitterness of the oranges.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you don't have a cast iron pan you could use a regular 8 or 9 inch round cake pan.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Cast Iron Skillets are fairly inexpensive, and can be used to good effect in many recipes, both sweet and savory. I also have a few that came from my mother, and grandmother. You might also be able to find one at a yard sale. They will last forever!. You will love it!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Do you peel the oranges? Or purée them skin, pith and all?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I asked this question earlier and, based on majority of the replies, it seems that we DO use the oranges with pith, skin, etc., except for the zest we took off the first orange.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In my experience, orange pith and rinds aren't bitter as every recipe using them seems to note.
