Limes for sure, Lemons too, but we use those little gems whole in Shaker Lemon pie with delicious results. So may I suggest we free ourselves from this unneeded cautionary step and use the whole orange with wild abandon.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Maybe this is implied, but should the whole oranges be pureed, skin, pith, and all?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Erika, the directions clearly say to "finely zest" the SKIN of ONE orange into the bowl of sugar.  Use a Microplane to do this.  For the  second orange, do not use the orange skin, pith or seeds, just the orange sections.  The pith is usually stringy and bitter, which is is many people generally remove and do not eat it. The chef also says to "then trim a bit of the stem end off both oranges and discard" before pureeing. I have not made this cayet, my comments are based on the chef's directions.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I respectfully disagree with Diana - I believe the whole oranges (minus the two ends) should be pureed - other cake recipes similarly ask for the whole citrus fruit to be pureed (sometimes boiled first) to impart a lot of citrus depth to the final product. See Claudia Roden's orange cake on this site, ands others:)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe is based on one that does, in fact, use a puree of the whole orange, so I read the directions as intending to use the entire orange.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is just another take on an ancient recipe - much older than 2003. I use a recipe that doesn’t take near as much butter, the entire egg, just one orange and a simple cake pan. It’s marvelous. I’ll try this one too.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This may be my new favorite cake.  The texture/crumb is so great.  It is slightly bitter due to using the whole orange, but it gives the cake such a unique and refined taste.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I just made this and am eating my first piece now. Delicious! I only used 1 cup sugar, it's plenty sweet. I also used gluten free flour, and 4 mandarin oranges because I had them already. It's very moist and very good. Recommended!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Impressive and easy. Guests quickly vanish this cake. A favorite variation: reduce sugar to 1 cup, replace 1/4 flour with cornmeal, use olive oil instead of butter, & bake in a spring form.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was terribly hard the very next day.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I could see making this with almond flour and chopped toasted almonds.  Why would you need salt if you're using salted butter?  Why would you use salted butter if you're adding salt?  I would skip the fennel seeds; toasting and grinding a 1/4 teaspoon has got to be more trouble than it's worth.

As for Mary's question: any heavy pan - but not nonstick - will do.  The issue is that the pan have a 10" diameter, be hot when the batter is added, and be heavy enough that it won't warp in the oven.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If I don't have a stand mixer? Just lots of elbow grease or something more?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You absolutely don't need a stand mixer for this recipe; a wooden spoon and some careful stirring will work perfectly well. Only a very few recipes actually require a stand mixer, and I wish that NYT Cooking would do a better job of acknowledging where a stand mixer is merely a recipe writer's preference.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Beating buyer and sugar until fluffy is a standard cake making step which gets air into the mix. Five minutes with a stand mixer equals a lot of elbow grease. Do you have a hand held mixer? That's what people used in the olden days before having a stand mixer was so common.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I know, blasphemy, but I grew up with my mother using a Duncan Hines orange cake mix that was, for me and us, quite delicious. I have not seen that particular mix for many decades now, assuming it's gone, gone, gone, along with so many other nice things. Anyway, this looks scrumptious for all the same reasons and will need to try it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Duncan Hines orange cake mix is available from many retailers, and from duncanhines.com: https://www.duncanhines.com/cake-mix/signature/signature-orange-supreme-cake-mix

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Definitely the first question that popped into my mind: use the whole orange with skin, pith, and potential seeds and all? Not commonly expected and easily clarified with a couple extra words. As it reads, the answer is yes.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I think, since you are cutting the oranges in wedges, you can and should remove the seeds. But the recipe clearly means use the whole orange, skin and pith.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Navel oranges don’t have seeds, I think that’s why she specifies them.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The recipe DOES call for using the peel and all (other than the ends) from that second orange. That’s sort of the point of this recipe. And do get navel oranges because they generally don’t have seeds so you don’t need to worry about those (seeds would create bitterness). I have seen and made lots of cakes using a cast iron skillet. I hope you do try it; you will like it!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Pre-heating a cast iron pan to 400°F is the standard way to make skillet cornbread, then melting "a generous amount" of butter in the pan before pouring in the batter and returning it to the oven.  This looks like a tasty variation.  (I could do without the micro-managed recipe steps, this isn't brain surgery.)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: For people asking about a cast iron pan. A regular pan will work. This recipe reminds of the "puddings" that my grandmother and her friends made down south. We always made them in a cast iron skillet. Maybe that's what they had growing up.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: So good. I used almond flour instead of semolina. It had a lovely texture. I buzzed up four small oranges which made 2 TBSP extra so added that to yogurt for tomorrow's breakfast. I didn't feel like being confronted with 2 egg whites so used 1 whole egg instead. Oh! As the cast-iron pan is heating up in the oven anyway, throwing the walnuts in to toast them saves a pan. I loved the flavour and texture of this cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
"trim a bit of the stem end off both oranges and discard. Cut oranges into 8 pieces and puree in a food processor or blender"

Does this mean that the unzested orange should be pureed with the peel on?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I'm not crazy about fennel seeds, so I subbed in some cinnamon.  The cake is delicious!  Definitely a keeper.  Also, the recipe halves easily.  Use an 8 inch cast iron pan to do the honors.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I don’t have a lot of experience baking, but I was really intrigued by the idea of making a cake in a cast-iron skillet. I don’t have a stand up mixer, and I mixed everything by hand. I couldn’t resist, adding a little bit of fresh grated ginger, to me the cake was just calling for it. Next time, I’d cut the sugar down a bit, it is very sweet. But it is delicious with a vibrant delicious orange flavor. I’ll be enjoying it this week!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: there is an excellent recipe that you can google online for a SICILIAN WHOLE ORANGE CAKE from christina's cucina, which uses the entire orange or if you prefer a lemon.  i have even used grapefruit.  all versions are excellent and seem to be easier than this recipe.  besides, you can bake it in a pyrex baking dish.  this recipe seems more complicated.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly and I LOVE this cake!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Delish. Love the chewy crust. I didn’t make the icing and didn’t miss it. Cut the sugar by 50 g and it was plenty sweet.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I followed the recipe for the cake but halved the sugar (1 cup) and zested both oranges.  

Perfect. The cake gave off a nice orange scent that stayed in the air.  I then let it cool on a rack, followed by cutting it into two layers. The bottom layer was coated with 1/8" of orange marmalade.  

The "frosting" was followed per recipe.  I liked my modifications. They worked well with the roadmap of the recipe, awarding myself and the author 5 stars.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Needs more fennel!  The 1/4 teaspoon toasted and then ground was lost, and I think would have been a great flavor.  I've read all the discussion about the oranges and won't add my two cents, but I thought the cake was a great and easy treat for adults, especially with the chantilly cream.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Agreed love this recipe and I actually made it again with more fennel just because I loved the combo so much.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Followed recipe verbatim. Meaning zested one orange and  I didn't peal either orange. Just cut up and pureed in a blender. Opted not to put in fennel.

We loved it. Unique dessert. Perfection.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this recipe exactly as written. I found it to be exceptional. My husband and I already consumed half of it and I just baked it 25 hours ago.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I simply loved this recipe and it came out perfectly. I made it exactly as written but I didn't make the chantilly cream topping. I thought the fennel seed was a wonderful addition even though there was only 1/4 teaspoon in the whole recipe, it was the perfect amount, imparting a special, but subtle flavor. I think the cake may be good with the nuts sprinkled in the pan before the batter, instead of incorporated into the batter. I may try that next time. This is a very special recipe.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is bitter.  Have to like bitter to love it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Yesterday's visitors let me know that they were coming about an hour before they arrived. I pulled a can of OJ concentrate out of my freezer and made this cake with the concentrate substituting for the orange pulp and zest. I decreased the sugar to 1 cup, knowing that the concentrated orange juice would be much sweeter than fresh oranges. The cake was an easy and delicious hit, with just pantry staples!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This cake was beautiful as it comes out of my cast iron pan, but it was a little hard to remove.  It is like an Italian snack cake.  I didn't make the frosting, just served it plain with a touch of vanilla ice cream. Superb.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this without peeling the second orange and it was perfect. Easy, didn’t stick, make the cream for sure.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I followed Phyllis' advice and let my food processor do most of the work. I even added my butter to the sugar mixture, before pureeing the oranges in the same bowl. This cake turned out beautifully, moist with a lovely flavor and texture. But next time I'll reduce the sugar to 1 cup so I taste more orange than sugar.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This recipe has way too much sugar, I went down to 1.5 cups and next time might go to 1 cup.  Also, I used almonds instead of walnuts, and agree with the other writer, you could probably skip the nuts because they do get kind of soft.  Strange texture, haven't decided yet whether I like the cake or not.  Flavor is not complex enough for me.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Loved it. Made it for brunch. No chantilly cream. My only objection was slightly too sweet for my taste. Next time we’ll try with one and a quarter cup sugar.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I used some organic navel oranges and followed the recipe exactly. it was delicious, especially a day or so later and even without the chantilly cream.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Fantastic. I made this vegan with egg substitute, olive oil. Baked 45 mins. It was a bit moist but dried out overnight. I used half the sugar. Still was too sweet for me.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Love this cake and my family requests it often.  Recommend reheating the pan once oiled to get it really sizzling hot. Also baking time is at least 50 mins before a toothpick comes out clean. Don't fret about the pith, its fabulous as written!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Went down to 300g sugar without any problems with texture, rise or sweetness. Slight bitter note from the orange, but in a good way. Like a marmalade. 

MAKE THIS CAKE. It is EXCELLENT.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I just made this exactly as written, with one exception.  I don’t have a cast iron pan, so I used a nonstick 12 inch skillet.  It may have been slightly flatter as a result, but the cooking time (45 minutes) was fine.  It was perfect — the best cake I’ve made in ages.  This recipe is a gem. Unique flavor — the fennel is a total game changer.  I will make this often.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I followed the advice of another commenter and put the orange peel and toasted fennel seeds in with the sugar in the food processor which worked well. After that I gently pulsed toasted almonds (I didn’t have walnuts) into the sugar mixture. Also added 1 teaspoon of orange extract for extra orange-ness. Cake was moist, dense and delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The addition of fennel seeds is brilliant. I did not use a cast iron pan - all good. A buttered and floured pan with parchment. I also did it by hand. And substituted walnut for pistachio. Super cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is delicious! Well-written recipe always appreciated. Replaced about 50g of AP flour with hazelnut flour & used toasted hazelnuts. Very Italian! Thanks Paul Bertolli!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Brilliant cake. Simple, but the texture is amazing, the flavor clean. The perfect snacking cake. Used fennel pollen instead of fennel seeds for a more floral note, and almond flour for the semolina. Will make again!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I really love this cake. And it got me thinking, it's everything I want cornbread to be. I like the idea of cornbread, taste. Hate the texture. Even when its moist its dry. And mealy. So I made this recipe but with corn. Turned out exactly what I want the texture/moisture of cornbread to be. Did take nearly an hour to bake. If I made it again, I'd cut the sugar to 1.5 cups. A bit too sweet for cornbread. Used orange zest. Probably cut that in half. Four ears, box grated, pureed, then strained.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I'd love to create a GF version. I'm thinking chestnut flour (suggested) plus maybe superfine almond/coconut/KingArthurGF to sub for the all purpose flour. Thoughts?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: You might want to search cooking.nytimes for Claudia Roden’s orange almond cake which is gluten free. She calls for 1/2 lb ground almonds. I used Bob’s Red Mill super fine almond flour (it’s 100% almonds). It worked perfectly and was delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: @Amasa came here for this, wasn't disappointed!! Thank you

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Do you keep the peel on the orange for the puree?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The recipe does not say to peel oranges, so keep peel on orange for purée. Claudia Roden’s orange cake uses entire orange, peel, pith and all. She boils the oranges before using though.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I’m concerned it won’t release from the cast iron skillet.
Is this unreasonable?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I think that's why the pan is preheated, to prevent sticking.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If your cast iron is properly seasoned, it should release easily.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I want to make sure so,  is the cake served in the cast iron or is it turned out onto a cake plate ?  I will be using one of my cast iron frying pans, I’d rather serve it for company on a cake plate . Just asking

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If you can take individual slices of cake from the kitchen to the table on individual dessert plates, you can certainly place the entire cake on a cake platter and slice individual pieces at the table. What difference would it make serving the cake from a cake plate or from the cast iron pan?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: What do you all think about making this with lemons instead as long as I make sure I have the 1.5 c. of puree? (I am allergic to oranges but not lemons).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I would love that flavor, but it might be REALLY tart in comparison to oranges.  A lemon-lime combo might work, but then you'd have "7-Up" cake.  (Ha.)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Is there any way to adapt the recipe if you don’t have a cast iron skillet?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I don’t have salted butter. How should I adjust to compensate?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: lizadams, Re how to compensate for unsalted butter since you don't have salted butter handy, in general there is a scant 1/4 tsp of butter per 8 oz of butter.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The total cooking time is stated as "2 hours", yet, it really only adds up to "1 hour". Typo? Or am I missing some extra step that takes an hour?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Maybe total time until ready to eat? If it didn't mention early morning baking I'd be tempted to say an hour for the chef to pour herself a glass of wine?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Would it be possible to make this with olive oil instead of butter or will that change the texture in a way that won’t bake up correctly? I’ve had cakes like this in Istanbul that use olive oil,

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Since I am lactose intolerant, I always substitute butter in muffins, cupcakes, and cake recipes with olive oil on a one-to-one basis. It works just fine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Do you think I could sub in almond flour for the walnuts? Nut allergy in the family but almonds are ok. Thank you

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Just a word on cast iron skillets: Le Creuset cast iron skillets have  porcelain coatings, so they have all the amazing qualities of regular cast iron without the worry of acidic foods, proper pan seasoning, rust, holding flavors or cleaning thoroughly.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The porcelain/ceramic coating also wears away with time. I have my great grandmother’s cast iron skillet. They are super easy to care for. Don’t leave standing water in one, but even if you do, you can remove rust, acidic foods are only an issue if you leave the food in it for hours at a time and I have /never/ had a issue with my pans holding flavors. As for cleaning throughly? Rinse it out while it’s hot If you have to use soap, rinse throughly and coat w/ a very thin coating of oil.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I have a question about the fennel seeds.  Is there a more common substitute?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Could I substitute almond flour for semolina?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I've never used almond flour, but I do have it on hand. Ok to use as nut flour? I'm eager to try this. Love reading the comments.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I'd love to try this recipe but am not sure how many oranges to use.  I know the recipe calls for 2, but oranges come in all sizes.  How big are they in this recipe?  I wish all recipes would measure ingredients in grams/ounces.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Does almond flour work (well) as a substitute for semolina flour?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Just please follow the recipe and tell us what it tastes like. If you don’t have the ingredients or proper pan, buy those first. Assume that if you have difficulties with the recipe on your first try, that it is probably user error. 
Then, share your experience of eating this cake. What is the texture? How prominent is the citrus taste? Does it look good? 
That should do it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: How did the cake turn out? Is it something worth trying?? Is there someone out there  who made it and  weigh in on the recipe and its results?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: As Su notes, stand mixers are seldom necessary for any cake batter, including the creaming of butter and sugar. Spoons, sturdy ones, work very well, and another excellent tool for mixing batters is the Danish dough whisk (q.v. on Amazon to see what they are).  It's faster than a stand mixer and provides more control, and clean up is as easy as washing a spoon. Smaller ones are good for liquid batters such as pancakes, larger ones for stiffer doughs and mixing ground meat with other ingredients.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made it according to the recipe, made sure there were no pits in the orange. Using the whole orange puree with peel on, as called for, resulted in a bitter cake that family don't want to eat. I wish I had removed all the pith and used just the peel and orange segments instead. As is, a waste of time and ingredients.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Maybe I'm missing something,  but one orange is zested, is the other peeled before pureeing??

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I used this basic recipe to make a lemon blueberry skillet cake.  Delicious!  Used 4 lemons to sub for the two oranges.  About 1 1/2 cups blueberries in place of the walnuts.  Because the lemons had very thick skin, I zested them, then cut off the pith before making the puree.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I don’t have a lot of experience baking, but was really intrigued with the idea of making a cake in a cast-iron skillet. I couldn’t help adding a little fresh ginger, to me, the cake was calling for it. I don’t have a stand mixer and mixed everything by hand. Next time, I will cut down the sugar, it is quite sweet. However, it has a delicious orange flavor, and a nice caramelized crust, really delicious. I’ll enjoy this week!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I thought this recipe sounded divine, so tried it for the first time.  Followed the recipe & placed the cast iron in oven at least 1/2hr before the baking of the cake. I substituted 1cup reg flour with the roasted chestnut flour. I must have had smaller than expected oranges as I needed to pulse a third to end up with the 1 1/2c of puree as indicated.  The middle wouldn't bake completely - I ended up taking out of the oven after 1hr of baking. The edges were brown/delish - middle undercooked.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this yesterday. In the article she says any nut flour will do so I used almond flour in place of the semolina. I have a well seasoned 8” iron skillet that I used. Everything else I did exactly as the recipe is written. The resulting cake was wonderfully fragrant, nutty and rustic. But the middle was underdone, probably because the skillet was too small. I also didn’t like the slight bitterness from the orange pith. Next time I’ll cut the recipe in half and trim more of the pith.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this because I was craving an orange flavored cake. However, I won’t make this again the use of the whole orange, including the peel, in part of a bitterness that I did not care for.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made it as written.  Including putting both oranges pith and all into the slurry.  Cake tastes like pith.  Inedible.  Maybe I should have known better.  Waste of time and ingredients.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: What is a 10” pan? A #8 is 10” at the top a #10 is 10” at the bottom and 12 at the top…

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: No fennel.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Do you need unwaxed oranges?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Navels have few to no seeds and a minimum of pith. Mine were large enough to yield 2 cups of puree. Followed the advice of another commenter and pulsed the zest, fennel, vanilla, pinch of salt and sugar (<350 grams) in a food processor first. Loved the toasted fennel and walnuts. Came out perfectly after 45 minutes. Moist and tender and flavorful. My husband loves the chantilly cream but the cake is delicious on its own without the embellishment.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I was intrigued by this recipe and baked it as written. It was good but I couldn’t help wondering if it needed so much butter (1cup) and sugar (2cups). I researched some other Sicilian orange cake recipes referenced in the notes and found some with less butter and sugar. I won’t make this again, I’ll try others that are less decadent.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: True, step 2 is confusing. The zest into the sugar is a nod to Pierre Herme, it will give the cake a boost of orange that is neither sour or bitter. The trimmed orangeS give the cake it's full 'orange-ness' (sweet-sour-bitter). She has very helpfully included a volume measurement of     1 1/2  cups of puree. This is a sophisticated recipe with real technique.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I don't understand the flour directions. 1/4 cup semolina and 1/4 cup all purpose? or just 1/4 cup of either? or 1/2 cup of either? Very confusing.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If using the semolina, you use 1/4 cup. If you are not using the semolina, you should put in an extra 1/4 cup of all purpose flour. But you need to add a quarter of a cup of one or the other.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This cake is bitter. It was ok with the sweetened whipped cream. I couldn’t taste much orange really, let alone the fennel. The bitterness from the pith just overwhelmed everything else. I used navel oranges as called for. I was curious about this recipe and curiosity satisfied I won’t make it again (or any other cake that calls for a whole citrus with the pith).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Make the lemon  (egg and butter free). wacky cake, but sub the rind and juice of an orange for the llemon.  The orange juice is used as part of the one cup of waater in the recipe.   I always have to make two of these for a buffet.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Used zest of both oranges, peeled most of the the pith off before blending.
Used 1 tsp fennel seeds.
Subbed salted pecans and cashews instead of walnuts because that’s what I had…
Nice.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is so delicious! A standup mixer would be much easier than a hand held mixer. But it can be done.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Sorry for this question:
I am not sure if we puree the oranges with their peal or not ?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this this morning.  After 40 minutes at 350 the toothpick came out clean, top was browned, no motion, let it set in the pan 30 minutes. Yet it was quite underbaked.  Someone else did 50 minutes and that might be what you want to do.  (and for the record, I zested one orange,  sliced both into 8 sections including peel, and pureed it all).

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
This was a complete waste of time and quality ingredients!!!! 
It was bitter, the texture was awful and it was recipe for heart burn. 

I’m an experienced cook, the toothpick came out clean, but it was gooey.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made exactly as written, except (always an except) I put a bit more than 1 1/2 cups of orange puree (to use up what was made), It definitely took the full 50 minutes, and even then the cake came out very moist..I think it was cooked, but it was so moist it could seem as if it wasn't. (If that makes any sense).The flavour was definitely VERY orange forward! I thought it could use a bit more fennel seeds. My guests loved it and asked for the recipe! I cant imagine making it without a standup mixer

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this today, according to directions, the walnuts were omitted because I forgot to buy them. It was delicious. Not bitter. Used 1 1/4 cups of sugar. Took about 40 minutes to cook.  My suggestion is to use organic oranges. Its a moist cake with the puree, the stand mixer whips and aerates the butter - it needs the 5 minutes to get fluffy. Its funny that no one has commented on the Chantilly Cream - adding a pinch of salt really changes the flavor of the cream.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I Made 1/2 recipe, as it easily cuts in half. That meant I toasted 1/2 of 1/4 teaspoon of fennel seeds. There were 5 of them. 1 whole small orange made exactly 3/4 cup, obviously I didn’t zest it first, since it was going into the food processor. It was so much less cumbersome to use a hand held mixer. It’s baking now. We’ll see if there is too much fennel.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Absolutely wonderful! I eliminated the walnuts because my household isn't fond of them, (would have subbed almonds if I had had them) and served it with whipped coconut cream and raspberries. Made the house smell amazing while it was baking!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Since our backyard oranges are small and less sweet, I ended up rough cutting off about half the skins of 4 and all the skins of 2 to reach the 1 ½ cups of puree. 
I wanted to make sure the cake was moist and so I instead used ½ cup of oil with ½ cup of butter.  Also, I used an instant thermometer and pulled it at 205 degrees to avoid over baking
The topping was too subtle for me, not sweet enough and seemed too light bodied so I added more sugar and a little cream cheese for body.
So Good!!!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made the cake exactly as written and it's very moist and dense with all butter like a pound cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Can you suggest a way to bake this without a cast iron skillet? I don't own one, or have room for one, and, otherwise, have a very well equipped kitchen.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Nigella Lawson's clementine cake is delicious and foolproof.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Can this be made gluten free?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Dear Readers: there are some orange cakes that use almond flour and pureed boiled oranges and there are many other recipes that use all purpose flour and pureed uncooked oranges. This recipe is of the latter genre. Follow the recipe and you will be fine. If you want gluten free then the boiled orange and almond meal is the way to go. Both are very different and very good.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The cake is delicious, though painstaking and messy to make. But after resting for 30 minutes in the pan, I took it out and it fell apart. I coated the pan with lots of olive as instructed.  Didn’t help. I won’t make this again

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: That's surprising. I waited til mine was just a little warm and it turned out perfectly.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Whole orange including peel?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Yes. but be sure to remove both of the ends from each of the oranges.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Orange flower water might add an excellent bit of depth to this recipe

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The flavor from two whole oranges is plenty deep, It would overcome orange flower water.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Followed cake recipe exactly and the result was delicious. Followed the recipe for the topping, but the buttermilk made it taste too sour for us. (But the buttermilk gives it a beautiful texture). We added more sugar to taste and some left over orange pulp, and we’re happy with that adjustment. The star is the cake, so the topping — however adjusted — should not be overpowering. This dessert goes into dessert rotation!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Judith brought up some good questions.  I delayed making this because of the variability of navel oranges (ratio of flesh to pith, etc.).  What is helpful was the volume of puree needed for the cake (1-1/2 cups).  My navel oranges available to me were horrible -  mealy and dry flesh.  All I could get that was decent was 6 clementines.  Zested two of them, and pureed all six.  I had extra puree which was great with yogurt :-).  The cake was fragrant and had a nice bottom crust.  Lovely flavor.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: After reading the recipe directions twice I was still not clear about to use or not to use the rind. Reading comments didn’t make it any clearer. I am going to discard the bitter pith and use all that remains to purée.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Use the rind. That's what makes this cake special. And there's enough sugar to overcome the bitter.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Wondering if the fennel seed is critical for the recipe?  A friend is deathly allergic.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I would say "no", it's not critical. But it is really a nice but subtle flavoring which I totally loved.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this cake yesterday. I  had some doubts about using the entire orange, pith, skin, and all, but I followed the recipe as written, zesting one orange into the sugar and then cutting it and the second orange in quarters to puree. It is fantastic! I did use a 12" cast iron skillet because that's what I have, and it worked fine. Using the entire orange results in an intensely orange, slightly bitter puree, but the bitterness is lost when the cake comes together. Delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Navel oranges worked perfectly.  I might scant the sugar a bit though I notice the sweetness more when I had a piece without the chantilly cream. I'm not a big cake person, but this was quite good - very flavourable. I was a little wimpy on the fennel. I'll do the full 1/4 tsp next time. (There will be a next time!)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: can you use a spring form pan?  with parchment on the bottom?  Or is it better to use a pyrex pan.  My cast iron pan it too big...

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this cake and anticipated it's coming out of the oven with the fragrance of orange.  First, it was not baked in 45 minutes, but took at least one hour to finish.  When cool and sliced, it crumbled terribly.  This cake is dense as well.  I followed the directions.  I weighed the ingredients.  I put the cast iron skillet in the oven to heat as the oven was heating.  The skillet did not sizzle as described.  I was very disappointed with the results.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made the cake as directed with oranges.  It was fantastic. It didn't need to topping, which I think undercut the moist richness of the cake.  I disagree that it is "easy" as you need to toast the fennel seeds and the walnuts and use both a stand or hand mixed and a food processor or blender.  However, I consider this cake a great success.  It may end up as a Christmas dessert.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I would make sure you really grease the pan well at the bottom, the orange juice & pulp make for a sticky bottom.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made it exactly according to recipe--whole oranges, cast iron pan. After 45 minutes middle was still semi liquid. I'm using a predictable, accurate oven and "normal" oranges, not particularly wet. Put it back in for 10 minutes and the top center never really seemed to set. Dunno. Tastes good.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Making it now; realized my cast iron pan is 12 in., so batter will spread more and cake will be flatter.  One person used an 8 in pan and said middle wasn’t done so I’m guessing that larger pan will mean the center will cook faster (and maybe in the baking time the recipe calls for, since many said the middle wasn’t done). I have a 10 inch stainless steel pan; wondering if I should use that. maybe compensate for bigger pan by adding proportionally larger amounts of ingredients?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: No fennel or semolina, but everything else as written. Texture is dense and moist, in a positive way. The citrus: as is this cake was bitter, although not so much that it was inedible, but it is a prominent note. Only removed the stem end as directed and the oranges I had did contain a fair amount of pith. I could see zesting both, and letting the osmotic pressure of the sugar draw the oil out like an oleo saccharum, followed by removing a majority of the pith. Will make again.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The flavor can not be beat.  This, however, was so crumbly that it it was not really cake.  I think all that creaming of the butter, etc. was not a good thing.  I followed the recipe exactly, and was entranced with the toasted fennel seeds and pureed orange, but I will try to make this with some oil in it next time, to get a more cake-like crumb.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: “1/4 teaspoon fennel seeds, lightly toasted and coarsely ground (optional).”

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I recommend pureeing enough orange for 2 cakes. So delicious, you'll want a second one soon.
Would this be too rich as a layer cake with ganache filling????

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is good! I made it as written. (I originally picked the recipe because it would be easy to make dairy-free; ended up not needing to make the butter substitution.) The fennel and walnuts really elevate it. My one question: When should one take it out of the pan? I let it cool in the pan for about an hour, and while I had no trouble getting it out in one piece, it left a bunch of the lovely caramelized topping behind. I just ate that with a spoon but I'd love to have had it on the cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this last night. Cooked it for about 50 minutes - the outer edges and top were dark but the center was undercooked and fell apart after cooling. I also found the orange peel (which I left intact when making the puree) a bit overpowering. If I make it again I think I will reduce the temperature, bake longer, and will probably remove half of the peel / pith before making the puree.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I didn't have semolina flour, but chopping the walnuts superfine gave the cake the texture it needs without the semolina. And I have this in the morning with a little plain icelandic skyr instead of the accompanying chantilly cream - delicious!.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: this is tasty, got rave reviews. smells delicious while baking. i used 1/2t fennel, would use more next time. subbed cornmeal for semolina as was out,pecans for walnuts as thats what i had. thought the nuts kinda got lost. next time will try subbing one egg for two yolks, and i think this might be as good or better as an olive oil rather than butter cake. i will confess to not mixing as long as directed ;)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I made this with 100g instead of the 400g sugar and still found it almost too sweet in the end (that being said, I usually start my first attempt of any NYT cake recipe with about 1/3rd of the sugar).
Second, the batter turned out relatively dry, so one really is spreading it in the pan, not pouring it. Had me worried for a moment, but the cake turned out absolutely fine and spongy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was super easy and amazingly delicious! Will definiately make again. I did not try the accompanying cream, maybe next time.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Nice, moist cake, but I wanted still more orange and fennel flavor. I added more zest and fresh (not dried) fennel seed, but otherwise followed the recipe. I'm going try an olive oil cake recipe using same concept to see what happens next.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’ve added hemp hearts to this batter for even more texture.  Works great, adds protein and other goodness, tastes delicious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was delicious- a very adult flavor- the fennel is a wonderful note. I omitted the nuts per another comment. Baking took about an hour- could be my oven. I would make it again for sure.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Step 2 is confusing: ...finely zest one orange into it. Set the bowl aside and then trim a bit of the stem end off both oranges and discard. Cut oranges into 8 pieces and puree in a food processor or blender. Do I have to cut the white skin off the zested first orange? What about the second one, peel it?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I cut the sugar to one cup but otherwise followed exactly and it was fabulous! (No cast iron either, just my cake tin)

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We’ve made this several times, and have loved it. But: there is a reason, we think, for the advice to serve/enjoy warm right out of the oven. As the cake cools, the middle (the dome) will start to sink. So if made ahead for guests, it will look like a bit of a collapsed cake, even though it is thoroughly cooked throughout. We just tried baking this in a springform pan. The batter filled up the pan much higher, so we baked it much longer (60 min). It still sank, even more, in the middle over the course of 4 hrs. We’ve concluded (though we should’ve recognized this from the start) that this is a very heavy batter. Right out of the oven, there is still enough warmth to keep that middle from sinking. Our plan: go back to cast iron pan, and don’t bake hours in advance for guests. Easy enough to do the prep ahead and bake later.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Note to the food police: You can't arbitrarily reduce the amount of sugar in a cake recipe without adversely affecting flavor and texture. This is a "high ratio" cake in which the amount of sugar exceeds the amount of flour. The sugar's needed to offset the bitterness of the cocoa, which gives the cake its rich chocolate flavor. If the cake offends your sensibilities, have a piece of fruit for dessert instead.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A kind note to those with health concerns. I participate in a wild game cooking FB group with a few simple rules. The most important rule is you never criticize what someone else eats.  Everyone is different.  Some want a cake with all the sugar and all the butter. It's cake! There is nothing inherently wrong with that.  If it appeals to you, make it and tell us how it was.  If it doesn't, just move on to something else.  Why shame others for liking what they like?  Me?  I'm making the cake

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I agree. After the last few years, I say: Life can be hard. Enjoy your cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Well said.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: My mother never baked cake for me; there wasn't time with our family business. The only times I would get cake were when she helped serve at a funeral at church and she would bring me a piece of cake home. I always called these funeral cakes. I'm going to make one in her memory; today would have been her birthday! I will use 700g of sugar too!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: In response to the health concerns—one slice, once in a while, won’t hurt the average person. Obviously, this is a special occasion cake. I like any cake with buttermilk in it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: There are no health concerns.  Nobody eats this kind of cake, or probably any cake, every day.  Bringing up health concerns every time there's a recipe for something sweet is pretentious.  Every doctor I've ever been to has advised "all things in moderation" and that goes for cake as well.  If one doesn't like cake then eat pie.  In moderation.  Unlike your remote, life doesn't come with a rerun button.  Enjoy it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I can p.r.o.m.I.s.e you this cake has been tested. Tested through years of church socials and birthdays and new babies and celebrations and “just because you are my friend” occasions. I have personally been given a slice from a neighbor who made it from the primary source and it is delicious, but, more than that, it is love. The simple act of baking and sharing is love. Lisa Donovan’s recipes are so full of love they make me weep with pleasure in the knowledge I am so well cared for.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Back in the days when I had reasons to bake cakes, I found that baking the layers in advance led to the best results. Bake them, cool completely (out of the pan) and refrigerate wrapped in plastic. You get one messy process out of the way (and cleaned up!) plus ensuring the layers are fully cooled and ready to frost. As for making this recipe my own, I'd use strong hot coffee instead of the water and raspberry preserves between the layers.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This is Annie’s sister.  Superb cake.  Tender.  Pan of cupcakes plus a 2 layer cake if your cake tins don’t match.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Lovely cake.  I’ve lived in the south my entire life and never heard anyone describe any cake as a church cake, but where I grew up in Eastern NC, ladies did compete to see how many layers tall they could bake cakes for funeral wakes and church homecomings.  Very thin yellow layers and a fudgy cooked icing.  I never had the knack for making it, but I’d crash a stranger’s wake or a homecoming at a chute didn’t know for a slice of that cake!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: It sounded delicious, I had to look it up! I think this might be it: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1014042-chocolate-little-layer-cake

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: On the DelMarVa peninsula, this kind of many-layered cake is known as Smith Island cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This frosting recipe is EXACTLY like the one in the Frog Commissary Cookbook from Philadelphia except for the last sentence of the directions. It is a wonderful recipe, I’ve used it for frosting or fudge sauce since the middle ‘80’s, nothing else. But please NYT, don’t rip it off without giving credit to the Frog Commissary. Also I highly recommend the cookbook if you can get your hands on one as I think it might be out of print. My cookbook opens automatically to the Killer Cake recipe page!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I have that cookbook!  Back from the 80's.  Killer cake and carrot cake are my favorites.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Such a wonderful cookbook and such a wonderful restaurant -- still miss it.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made this cake and now it is our official chocolate cake recipe for all time. Wonderfully fudgy cake with intense luscious icing. Due to kitchen turmoil, I made the three layers the day before assembly and cooled them overnight on wire racks. Just pressing a piece of wax paper on top to await assembly the following day. The icing is just barely enough so plan to use something else in between the layers or else be sparing in the use of it for the between layers. I did refrigerate after each layer

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I halved the recipe and made 2 8” layers. The batter was thin and didn’t look like enough but it rose beautifully and came out delicious and moist! Next time I’ll use hot coffee instead of boiling water.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Would it be sacriligious to bloom the cocoa in the boiling water before adding to the wet mix?  Also, would I tempt eternal damnation if I whipped the frosting? I like a fluffy whipped frosting!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I would suggest against the blooming, as you do want the hot liquid at the end to meld all the ingredients smoothly.  As for the frosting, do what makes you happy.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I do that in a very similar cake and it works fine.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was the best cake I’ve ever eaten.  Worthy of a Paul Hollywood and Prue Leith embrace.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I believe this is called a church cake because it's good enough to take to the gathering after Sunday church services or to a church social.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Reduce sugar to 1.5 cups in cake, and 1 cup in frosting.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
They are called “ church cakes “ because they are good enough to take to church functions and share.
   No holiness required.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: When I saw this cake this morning in my in-box it reminded me of my aunts favourite chocolate cake. The only difference is that she used whipped cream in between the layers. Must make another one soon in her honour. My aunt Hazel was a beautiful, classy, smart, fun lady from New Brunswick.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: That sounds wonderful! Just straight whipped cream? Any stabilizers/flavors?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Superb cake. I reduced entire recipe by half to
Make two cakes. I cut sugar to 200 gms and cocoa to 47 gms because that’s all I had in the pantry. Instead of plain water I added instant espresso. So yummy. I served with tea this evening.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: meaning 2 8" layers perhaps?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: So this icing - it's nearly the same as the one on the most successful cake I ever bake, one that brings grown men to tears of happiness. 30 years ago, a recipe in a women's magazine called for a devil's food cake mix, two layers. Slice each layer horizontally for 4 layers. When making the icing, add amaretto when cool, and  sprinkle some on each layer for good measure. I think you could serve the icing on carboard and people would still claim it was the best cake they had ever eaten.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Lisa Donovan is a wonderful baker! I hope we see more of her recipes regularly in the NYT. Thanks for this one!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
2/3 recipe, 9 x 13 pan

coffee instead of water

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Okay, maybe it’s just me, but the frosting and filling looks awfully thin compared to the thickness of the layers.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Just how I like it. This is a rich ganache and should be sufficient.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I followed this recipe to the letter, something I rarely do. This may become my go-to Birthday Cake recipe. It was moist, flavourful, and impressive, and was just as good the next day. It isn't too sweet, even to my Aussie palate. Next time I will simmer the cream and sugar a little longer than 6 minutes. There was some fine sugar crystalization in the ganache.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Hey cake chefs.  there are plenty of cake recipes that used less sugar and more flour.  These are just different cakes. So, if that is your need use a different recipe to create your layers.  one thing this type of recipe does for the chef is less mounding of the cake as it bakes.  Cakes with sour creme, yogurt or creme as an ingredient general have this attribute which lends itself well to multi-layers + higher sugar content with the creme makes the layers easier to handle in my experience.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This was good but not really remarkable.  Like other Southerners making comments here, I've never heard of "church cake" and doubt that the explanation given is very accurate.  I disagree with the notes advising radical sugar reductions; reducing sugar more than 25% will seriously harm the texture in cake and doesn't work well at all in frosting.  I substitute some of the sugar with King Arthur's excellent sugar substitute, which tastes and works better than most.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’m a true Southern (Alabama). I made this cake to take to Christmas dinner at a friend’s house out of town, so I made it a few days in advance. I followed this recipe exactly as written, weighing every ingredient as I went. I refrigerated after frosting each layer. Once finished, I put the cake in a sealed cake carrier and stored it in our 50 degree garage (we live in New England now(. It was absolutely perfect! I would not change a thing! Huge hit a dinner!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: As another reader noted, I have lived in the South, and have never heard the term "church cake." I have lived seven decades and attended many churches and innumerable Wednesday night suppers and other church get-togethers. I find it very interesting that so many language differences occur in the south. Can someone pls tell me where this term is used? I never heard it in Kentucky, Tennessee, Georgia, Florida, Texas or Virginia. Just curious.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The lovely Cheryl Day made a church cake on Chris Kimball's Milk Street cooking show (PBS) some months back. Ms Day is a cookbook author and was born in Savannah.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Native Southerner here; broad Southern roots (Virginia, Louisiana, Kentucky, Georgia) on both parents sides, including plenty of church going & cake baking. 
Never heard the term “church cake” in my 71 years of life.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Made a half recipe as an 8” cake (plus some cupcakes). Added a touch of espresso powder to batter and frosting. It was perfect.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
I could not agree more with the readers who are tired of being schooled and lectured by the nutrition police about ingredients like sugar, butter and anything that’s not vegan or gluten free.

Nobody is twisting your arm to bake and eat this cake. If it’s not for you, skip the nasty notes and type in Sugar free, gluten free or low fat, you’ll find many recipes here that will better suit your diet. 

I’ve baked this cake it’s fabulous! Thank you NYT Cooking!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Maybe I am not reading carefully-where does the boiling water go-into the "well" of the dry ingredients? I am not an avid baker/cook and would like to challenge myself to make this delicious sounding cake for my daughter's birthday

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Another Alabamian here( nods to rupert) and I too made this cake for a special birthday celebration on Christmas Day. Followed the recipe exactly and the results were spectacular.  This cake was not only the prettiest cake I ever made but one of the tastiest. I enjoyed baking it as the technique is slightly different from most recipes I use. To those who thought the frosting was skimpy I say it was sufficient and more would have been gilding the lily! Saving this one.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Did I miss which size cake pan to use? 8” or 9”, does it matter? Also, if you’ve made the frosting how stable is it at room temperature? I have the worst luck with buttercream sliding all over.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Everything you asked is clearly written in the STEPS.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I’m genuinely sad for people who made this and somehow the recipe didn’t work. This is the easiest, hands-down best chocolate cake I have ever made. I have made it half a dozen times since Lisa shared this recipe and it’s consistently spectacular—and I am an average baker at best, though I do use a scale and I don’t make substitutions. A friend’s husband—a very picky old-school southern gentleman—called this cake “righteous” and asked his wife to get the recipe. Righteous it is.  Make this cake.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Someone gave the link to the Chocolate Little Layer Cake adapted from Martha Meadows recipe. I made the cake a couple of years ago and it was fabulous. Just like the ones my friend's mother made. Ms Meadows was from SE Alabama where we lived and a lot of the moms would make these multi layered cakes. However, my mother being from Philadelphia never got the hang of it. A wonderful cake to bring back wonderful memories. Thank you NYT.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
@Anne: I remember the Frog/Commissary downstairs cafeteria in Philadelphia in the early 80's - wonderful Carrot Cake.
Can't wait to make this Church Cake!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Holy mackerel, this may be the best cake I've ever made or eaten. Very easy to make, unbelievably moist and chocolatey - this is my new go to recipe for birthdays, anniversaries, and chocolate cravings.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Please don’t omit the parchment. One of the stickiest cakes I’ve ever made. Basically turned into a tear-filled bowl of chocolate deliciousness. An epic slow motion meltdown on GBBO. Use caution.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Ingredient list should have included "fire extinguisher" and also "shop vac"
And also, depth of cake pan :P

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This easy, forgiving recipe made a fantastic cake - perfect crumb texture with a deep chocolate flavor. The fudgey-y frosting made my cake look impressive. I halved the recipe using 2 9” pans and it still made a sizable cake that could feed 8. I subbed buttermilk with the yogurt:water that NYT suggested. Also added some powdered espresso to the frosting, but it was unnecessary.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this yesterday and it was the biggest flop of a homemade cake I have ever made in 50 years of baking. Absolutely not sure what happened. I folowed the directions carefully, measuring exactly. It was like a bomb went off inside the poor thing and it tasted foul. It ended up in our compost. Clearly I messed up the chemistry somehow. Whatever the cause I am not going to be making this again. Expensive mistake!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Attention gluten free bakers:  I used cup4cup flour. Followed directions as written. Baking time increase by about 15 minutes.  Keep checking every 3 minutes or do. Middle of each layer fell, so not exactly as pictured!  Absolutely delicious, no ch as the in flavor because it was gluten free.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Amazing cake. I did  not have 3 cake pans of the same size, so I used 2 and split the batter in half. I baked for an additional ten minutes.

I refrigerated the cake after adding each layer of frosting to make sure it set.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I used a springform pan as my 3rd 9" pan. just an idea if you had one.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Gluten free guidance was very helpful. I just subbed one-to-one GF baking flour (Bob’s Red Mill brand) and cooked 15 minutes longer. I took it out when the pick inserted to test was a little moist, because other GF cakes I’ve made have been tough around the edges by the time the center was as dry as a regular version would be considered done. This resulted in a perfectly-moist done cake. Overall, delicious and a very nice treat for a special GF person.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Why would my layers all sink in the middle? It is frustrating after spending so much time carefully assembling the ingredients.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
My friend told me every recipe she's tried from NYT has been delicious, even if she made some mistakes. So I tried this, using gluten-free flour mix, and the cake was fabulous! It got rave reviews.
I didn't cook the frosting long enough for it to set up, though, I wish they said "soft ball stage" or something like that instead of 6 minutes. BUT next time I am serving ice cream sundaes, this frosting recipe (half) will be used for chocolate sauce, it's perfect. Doesn't get hard, tastes fantastic

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I have celiac disease and have made this cake using King Arthur’s measure for measure flour. It comes out beautifully every time I make it. It has become our go to birthday cake in our house. Absolutely delicious!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This cake was amazing! I only have 8 inch pans, but 2/3 of the recipe into 2 pans made for a tall 2 layer cake, so I will likely split it into 3 8 inch pans next time, and just bake for less time. The frosting was rich and delicious. I did orange marmalade in between the layers with the chocolate frosting, and my family loved it! Thanks you Ms. Donovan for the new family favorite chocolate cake!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: this is the only cake I ever want to make. I started making it for employee birthdays last and now everyone asks for it. thanks Lisa, also enjoyed your book very much xo

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
why too much liquid! it sank into the middle after 45 minutes of baking. I decreased the boiling water to 1/2 cup second time and it still was too much liquid.  
what the heck???

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Terrible waste of time and ingredients. I made this cake a second time today. First time total  disaster. too watery wouldn't cook, sunk into itself. Second time I used only 1/2 cup boiling water instead of 1 and 1/2  cups as called for. Same goopy messy sunken waste of time and ingredients. I can't serve this.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Average. The frosting is dairy-heavy and a bit sweet, not lusciously chocolatey (I used good chocolate and organic whipping cream). The cake is a decent oil cake, moist and reasonably flavorful and easy to make and so a good general purpose recipe, but the contrast to the frosting isn't marked or interesting. A better frosting would make a big difference. 

The cake is also huge, hard to get in the fridge. Not the right amount for a household of two without reducing amounts and pan size.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: Up here in Alaska, we know this cake as the North Douglas Chocolate Cake from the iconic old Fiddlehead Restaurant and Bakery. I always suspected it had roots in something holy!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I understand commenter Linda’s note on the iconic North Douglas chocolate cake most Alaskans know and love. I’ve also noticed similarities to the Texas sheet cake recipes I’ve read. These cakes bring huge dollars at dessert auctions!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: A lovely, rich and beautiful cake, simple to make and easy to frost.  Refrigeration between steps was key for me. Added strawberry jam (more sugar!) between layers.  Had lots of frosting leftover. Will try a peanut butter option between layers next time.  Was forced to leave cakes in their pans overnight for transport, which left the finished product so moist it compressed beneath the slicing knife.  Will bake ahead next time again, but will overnight out of the pans!

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This cake is very similar to a Texas Chocolate Sheet (Sheath) Cake - except that it is much larger and in the form of a layer cake, vs. a sheet pan cake. It was super easy to make and was gorgeous. I found eating one piece, as small as I could cut it, was difficult because it is so rich. Make it for a crowd! You can also try cutting it like a wedding cake, where you cut a circle about 3" into the cake - cut slices from that; then move into cutting the center circle.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Made this recipe a few times now using the below modifications with great results.
Replace hot water with strong black hot coffee, used PAM Canola Oil Baking Spray with Flour,  replaced flour with Cup-For-Cup Gluten Free Flour for a family member with allergies and cake came out the same as its regular flour counterparts (texture, taste, shelf-life, bake times). Used vanilla buttercream frosting in place of chocolate frosting (also adding almond and butter extract to recipe with great fanfare)

This is the BEST, moist chocolate cake ever.  Even tho I would recommend using a different frosting (this is too complicated and the results are not worth the effort), the cake part is sooooo great that I'm giving five stars. 

Use your own chocolate icing or other that you like best, but make this cake!

author: Lisa Donovan

commentBody: Lovely cake. I agree with all those who suggested adding a cup of freshly brewed coffee. Its divine. I use this frosting recipe regularly. Mostly, I do not like frosting on cakes, for me, it's all about enjoying the texture of the cake and frosting gets in the way! But the chocolate cake deserves a frosting:-) BTW, I use the recipe from King Arthur chocolate fudge bundt cake. It's an awesome recipe, try that too. https://www.kingarthurbaking.com/recipes/chocolate-fudge-bundt-cake-recipe

I appreciate that the NYT gives the South credit for delicious food but sometimes we're puzzlingly given credit for things we didn't create. All that said, fantastic cake, whoever invented it.

commentBody: This is the best cake I make. Truly flawless. I use black cocoa powder for the chocolatey-est flavor. author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody:
Made this several times now and it's always a hit. Incredibly moist cake, tasty frosting, overall a crowd pleaser for chocolate lovers! 

I have halved the recipe and baked in 3, 6" layers which works perfectly. Only tricky part is the eggs, but I crack two eggs and pour a little out. I've also used semisweet instead of unsweetened chocolate for the frosting since that's what I usually have handy, but cut down on the a sugar. 

To get a lighter colored frosting like the photo, you do need to whip some air into the ganache - I prefer it this way from a texture and visual perspective. Just whipped it in my stand mixer for a minute or two and popped it in the fridge to get the consistency and color I want. 

The layers will be extremely moist, so make sure they are completely cooled before assembling.

commentBody:
I followed the recipe exactly, but the frosting came out like chocolate syrup! I tried to rescue it by adding powdered sugar, but that didn’t make much difference. So I had to throw it all out (what a waste!) and buy frosting from a can, as I ran out of the powdered sugar.

And my cake batter was soup! I added an extra half cup of flour, which didn’t do much. The cakes still baked, they just took about 20 more minutes on the oven and were way too dense. What gives??

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: If the frosting is too loose, chilling it in the fridge will firm it up. As for the cake batter, it's supposed to be quite thin, but when baked will yield an exceptionally moist chocolate cake. This recipe is actually a variation of the one that appears on the back of the Hershey's cocoa can, which has been in circulation for at least fifty years.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: The batter is supposed to be quite thin. Your cake turned out way too dense because you didn't follow the recipe -- no mystery. As for the frosting, it probably was still too warm to set. If you make it again, try chilling it in the fridge to firm up the butter and chocolate it contains.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: We made this triple layer cake today, the third layer cake recipe we’ve followed from The NY Times. This cake, like the others (with quite different recipes) has risen nicely in the oven. But all three cakes have sunk down quite flat as they cool. I’m sure the flavor will be great, but without them rising we can’t get any sort of impressive height! We have this problem regardless of whichever layer cake recipe we follow (and we follow them precisely!). What are we doing wrong?

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: When a cake bakes, the leavener(s) in the batter cause it to puff up and rise. At the same time the heat of the oven causes the batter to "set" and form a semi-solid sponge. Ideally, the batter sets at the same time it reaches its maximum rise. But if it reaches its maximum rise before it sets, then the cake will collapse. There are several reasons this might happen: (1) Too much leavener; i.e., leavener not measured accurately. (2) Oven temp is too hot or too cool. (3) Cake pans are too small.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: This a marvelously chocolatey cake.  It’s simple to make, no mixer required.  I used leftover boiling coffee instead of boiling water.  I omitted the vanilla.  (Why is it even there?  Habit leftover from when it was much less expensive, I suspect.) I decreased the sugar by 100 grams, or so, and nobody complained. Nay, they couldn’t stop talking about how good it was.

author: Lisa Donovan
commentBody: I made this in an 11 x 15 glass pan for a group. I used 6 ounces less sugar. The cake was so moist and perfect. Don’t overcook it! For me even though it hadn’t pulled away from the edges, it popped back when the middle was pressed.  I did not have whipping cream for the frosting so I made simple cooked fudge frosting. This fool proof recipe is done by heating 1 cup sugar, 1/3 cup milk and 5 T butter over medium heat stirring often in a sauce pan to boiling point. Boil one minute and remove from the heat. Add 1 cup chocolate chips (I like extra dark) and stir until melted. Pour this mixture while still hot over the cooled cake. It sets to perfection in a few minutes. I had leftovers and froze them. It has been a tasty treat a month later. This is a great, reliable and very easy recipe.

commentBody: made this jan 21 2025 the day after inauguration. wonder if it would be good to swap out the hot water for fresh brewed coffee? i added a bit more cocoa power -- 20g more. made the cake darker, i like. buttered 9x16 pan, with cocoa dust, no parchment, worked fine. halved the frosting recipe, covered the cake fine. i love this cake!
